Introduction

Continual learning for neural dialogue systems aims to enable a model to acquire new conversational skills over time without catastrophically forgetting previously learned abilities. Prior neural dialogue models (Mou et al., 2016; Xing et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017) demonstrate that large-scale corpora can endow systems with diverse capacities, including persona consistency (Qian et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b), empathy and affect modeling (Zhou et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2019), and knowledge-grounded responses supported by retrieval modules.

A central challenge in this setting is balancing stability and plasticity as tasks arrive sequentially. In dialogue, task boundaries may be fuzzy, user intents can drift, and skills often share overlapping primitives such as grounding, turn-taking, and style adaptation. Consequently, techniques that isolate task-specific knowledge while still promoting beneficial transfer are particularly attractive.

Pretrained language models provide rich inductive biases that can be harnessed for continual dialogue learning. Inductive transfer from such models to downstream dialogue tasks has been categorized into feature-based, fine-tuning-based, and adapter-based paradigms (Ruder, 2019). Feature-based transfer freezes the backbone and learns shallow heads, whereas full fine-tuning updates all parameters but risks interference when tasks accumulate. Adapter-based methods insert lightweight modules into frozen transformers to encode task-specific information with fewer trainable parameters.

We adopt an adapter-based approach because it offers a compact mechanism to store task-local adjustments while preserving general linguistic competence in the frozen backbone. Conventional adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Poth et al., 2021; Pfeiffer et al., 2021) have proven effective across a range of NLP tasks, including classification, generation, and multilingual transfer. However, in multi-task or sequential regimes, naïvely stacking or swapping adapters can still lead to negative transfer if shared activations entangle incompatible features.

Madotto et al. (2020) explored adapters for acquiring conversational skills and reported strong parameter efficiency in multi-skill setups. Their study primarily optimized per-skill adapters with limited mechanisms to modulate cross-skill interference during sequential acquisition. This motivates designing adapters that explicitly separate generalizable knowledge from narrow, task-bound adjustments while facilitating forward transfer to future skills.

Recent work has also examined mechanisms for representing and routing shared knowledge in modular architectures. Techniques such as sparsely gated experts, router-based mixtures, and task-conditioned controllers allow selective activation of submodules, potentially reducing interference and improving sample efficiency. These ideas intersect with dialogue modeling where contextual factors like persona, domain, or knowledge source can serve as routing signals.

Recent work has also explored memory-augmented continual learning for dialogue and language tasks, including elastic weight consolidation and synaptic intelligence for parameter regularization (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Zenke et al., 2017), rehearsal and pseudo-rehearsal with exemplar buffers (Rolnick et al., 2019; Chaudhry et al., 2019), architectural expansion via progressive networks and dynamically expandable networks (Rusu et al., 2016; Yoon et al., 2018), and modular or prompt-based transfer with prefix/prompt tuning and LoRA-style low-rank adaptation (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022).

Our perspective builds on these insights to structure adapter capacity into distinct channels for general and task-specific knowledge. The general channel encodes patterns that recur across dialogue skills, such as grounding schemas and discourse markers. The task-specific channel isolates idiosyncratic behaviors, e.g., domain lexicons or policy constraints that should not bleed into other skills.

To operationalize this separation, we introduce gating that conditions on a compact task signature estimated from limited supervision. The gate allocates representation flow between general and specific channels, enabling dynamic sharing when beneficial and isolation when harmful. Crucially, the backbone remains frozen, and only the lightweight parameters within the adapters and gates are updated during task acquisition.

We further incorporate a knowledge distillation signal that aligns emerging task adapters with signals from the general channel. This guidance encourages reusable abstractions to be captured centrally while unique residuals are retained locally. Distillation is applied on hidden states and token-level logits, balancing expressivity and stability.

In contrast to simultaneous multi-task training, our sequential protocol uses a replay-lite strategy that does not require full data retention. A small, privacy-preserving sketch of prior skills, such as synthetic utterances generated from compact prototypes, suffices to maintain prior performance. This approach is compatible with deployment constraints that restrict storage of user conversations.

We evaluate the framework on multi-skill dialogue benchmarks spanning persona consistency, empathetic response, and knowledge-grounded generation. Tasks are introduced in varying orders to assess order sensitivity and robustness. We report standard automatic metrics as well as human judgments of coherence, style, and factual grounding.

Ablations examine the contribution of channel separation, gating, and distillation. Additional studies vary adapter size and initialization to understand parameter-efficiency trade-offs. We also measure forward transfer by introducing unseen skills after training on subsets of tasks and quantifying zero-shot adaptation.

The results indicate that structured adapters reduce catastrophic forgetting relative to conventional adapters and full fine-tuning at comparable parameter budgets. Moreover, forward transfer improves especially for skills sharing grounding requirements or discourse patterns. These gains persist across model scales, suggesting architectural rather than purely capacity-driven effects.

Finally, we analyze latency and memory overhead in interactive settings. Because the backbone is frozen and adapters are small, the approach maintains near-baseline inference cost and supports rapid skill updates. This property is critical for real-world conversational agents that must evolve with minimal downtime and resource consumption.

Since Rashkin et al. (2019) release EmpatheticDialogues, many approaches have been proposed to generate empathetic responses. Lin et al. (2019) propose mixture of emotional experts. Majumder et al. (2020) propose emotion grouping, emotion mimicry and stochastic sampling. Li et al. (2020) extract emotional words through lexicon and propose an adversarial generative model. Shen et al. (2021) apply dual-learning with unpaired data for the bidirectional empathy. Gao et al. (2021) integrate emotion cause into response generation process through gated mechanism. Sabour et al. (2021); Li et al. (2022) use implicit commonsense for context modelling. Kim et al. (2021) train a model to extract words that cause the speaker's emotion and attach RSA Framework (Frank and Goodman, 2012) to any generative models to generate responses, focusing on emotion cause words.

Recently, many studies have shown remarkable improvements through recognizing transitions of features between utterances in open-domain multiturn dialogues. Qiu et al. (2020) perceive transitions of emotion states for context modelling. Zou et al. (2021) propose a module to manage keyword transitions. Zhan et al. (2021) model external knowledge transitions to select a knowledge used for generation. Beyond empathetic response generation, related strands include affect-aware retrieval (Rashkin et al., 2017;Zhou et al., 2018), persona-grounded dialogue (Zhang et al., 2018;Wolf et al., 2019), controllable text generation with emotion labels (Ficler and Goldberg, 2017;Sreejith et al., 2019;Asghar et al., 2018), emotion-cause extraction for conversations (Poria et al., 2021;Ding et al., 2020), and knowledge-enhanced empathy using ConceptNet or ATOMIC (Speer et al., 2017;Sap et al., 2019;Song et al., 2019;Zhong et al., 2020), as well as datasets for emotional dialogue beyond EmpatheticDialogues such as DailyDialog (Li et al., 2017), MELD (Poria et al., 2019), IEMOCAP (Busso et al., 2008), and GoEmotions (Demszky et al., 2020), and pretraining strategies leveraging affective signals or prompt-based conditioning (Radford et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2022;Sanh et al., 2022). In multi-turn empathetic dialogues, we consider emotions, keywords, and utterancelevel meaning (Gu et al., 2021) as important features of each utterance and propose a novel approach of recognizing feature transitions between utterances.

Examples of the cross-modal learning paradigm. As described in Section 2.1, many of the existing methods for cross-modal learning fit into the paradigm where encoders are modalityindependent. This paradigm has been shown to be effective by achieving state-of-the-art retrieval performance on benchmark datasets with the modality pairs that we considered in this work: videotext (Bain et al., 2021;Luo et al., 2021), videoaudio (Monfort et al., 2021Rouditchenko et al., 2020), and image-audio (Harwath et al., 2018, 2020. While these prior works relied on different pre-training datasets, model architectures, and objective functions, they all leverage modalityindependent encoders. One of the most important features of this paradigm is the fixed inference time for retrieval. Since the encoders are modalityindependent, embedding vectors for samples in a given modality can be computed without using any samples from the other modality. Thus retrieval only involves computing the dot product between embedding vectors from two different modalities. As a consequence, these models are more flexible for large-scale retrieval, and the embedding vectors from each modality can be used independently for other downstream tasks.

Other cross-modal learning frameworks. In contrast to the aforementioned works, some methods leverage cross-modal relations within the encoders instead of using modality-independent encoders. This has been done with both cross-modal encoders (Lei et al., 2021;Luo et al., 2021) and cross-modal attention mechanisms (Miech et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2019b,a;Gabeur et al., 2020). However, the cross-modal interactions increase the complexity for retrieval since every instance of a specific modality must be used as input with every instance of another modality to obtain the embedding vectors. With m and n samples in the modalities respectively, this increases the complexity from the modality-independent approach from O(m + n) to O(mn). Further, it also makes analysis of the embedding vectors from any individual modality challenging and inhibits single-modality downstream tasks. Our proposed framework builds on the modality-independent approach to enable light-weight retrieval, but it also enables crossmodal interaction through our proposed codebook and Cross-Modal Code Matching objective.

Uncovering semantic-level correspondences. Image-audio models have been shown to discover spoken words and visual objects without supervision through retrieval tasks (Synnaeve et al., 2014;Harwath and Glass, 2015;Harwath et al., 2017;Kamper et al., 2018), and the audio embedding vectors have been shown to cluster into word-like speech units (Harwath and Glass, 2017;Wang and Hasegawa-Johnson, 2019;Harwath et al., 2020). Some work has studied the ability of video-audio models to relate spoken words to visual objects and actions in videos (Boggust et al., 2019;Rouditchenko et al., 2020). However, none of these models incorporated a shared embedding space that enabled modality-invariant representations. VQ units have been used in the audio encoder of an image-audio model (Harwath et al., 2020), which allowed it to capture the hierarchical structure of spoken language. While our proposed framework is similar in that it also discretizes the audio sequence  with VQ units, our work differs significantly by capturing the cross-modal interactions between visual and audio inputs in the shared embedding space rather than solely capturing the tree structure of speech. Further, besides image-audio data, our proposed framework can handle video-audio and video-text data.

spans: Early computational models of visual attention have used bottom-up approaches to model the neural circuitry representing pre-attentive selection processes from visual input (Koch and Ullman, 1985) and later the central idea of a saliency map was introduced (Niebur and Koch, 1996). A central hypothesis studying eye movements under task conditions is known as Yarbus theorem stating that a task can be directly decoded from fixation patterns (Yarbus, 1967) which has found varying support (Greene et al., 2012;Henderson et al., 2013;Borji and Itti, 2014). More recently, extracting features from deep pre-trained filters in combination with readout networks has boosted performance on the saliency task (Kümmerer et al., 2016). This progress has enabled modeling of more complex gaze patterns, e.g. vision-language tasks such as image captioning (Sugano and Bulling, 2016), visual question answering (Das et al., 2016) or text-guided object detection (Vasudevan et al., 2018). Additional datasets and benchmarks have further supported developments including MIT300 (Bylinskii et al., 2015), SALICON (Jiang et al., 2015), CAT2000 (Borji and Itti, 2015), and OSIE (Xu et al., 2014), and methodological advances encompass graph-based saliency (Harel et al., 2007), spectral residual models (Hou and Zhang, 2007), boolean map saliency (Zhang and Sclaroff, 2013), and deep fixation prediction with skip connections (Kümmerer et al., 2017).

Human attention patterns as regularization during model training have resulted in comparable or improved task performance in tagging part-of-speech (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015a,b;Barrett et al., 2018), sentence compression (Klerke et al., 2016), detecting sentiment (Mishra et al., 2016;Mishra et al., 2017), and reading comprehension (Malmaud et al., 2020), and related studies integrate gaze features for named entity recognition (Hollenstein and Zhang, 2019), machine translation (Miculicich et al., 2018), summarization (Mohammad et al., 2016), and multi-task learning with eye-tracking corpora such as Dundee, GECO, and ZuCo (Kennedy et al., 2003;Cop et al., 2017;Hollenstein et al., 2018). Complementary approaches leverage attention supervision from human fixations (Jain and Wallace, 2019), combine fixation duration and saccade statistics with contextual embeddings (Sood et al., 2020b), distill gaze to token-level importances (Pruthi et al., 2020), and evaluate robustness on cross-domain reading datasets (Bannur and Hovy, 2020).

Introduction

Saliency modeling has become a cornerstone of computational attention research and provides a bridge between low-level feature processing and high-level task demands. Early computational models of visual attention have used bottom-up approaches to model the neural circuitry representing pre-attentive selection processes from visual input (Koch and Ullman, 1985) and later the central idea of a saliency map was introduced (Niebur and Koch, 1996). A central hypothesis studying eye movements under task conditions is known as Yarbus theorem stating that a task can be directly decoded from fixation patterns (Yarbus, 1967) which has found varying support (Greene et al., 2012;Henderson et al., 2013;Borji and Itti, 2014). More recently, extracting features from deep pre-trained filters in combination with readout networks has boosted performance on the saliency task (Kümmerer et al., 2016). This progress has enabled modeling of more complex gaze patterns, e.g. vision-language tasks such as image captioning (Sugano and Bulling, 2016), visual question answering (Das et al., 2016) or text-guided object detection (Vasudevan et al., 2018). Additional datasets and benchmarks have further supported developments including MIT300 (Bylinskii et al., 2015), SALICON (Jiang et al., 2015), CAT2000 (Borji and Itti, 2015), and OSIE (Xu et al., 2014), and methodological advances encompass graph-based saliency (Harel et al., 2007), spectral residual models (Hou and Zhang, 2007), boolean map saliency (Zhang and Sclaroff, 2013), and deep fixation prediction with skip connections (Kümmerer et al., 2017).

Despite advances in image-based saliency, reading and language-guided attention introduce distinct temporal and linguistic constraints. Predicting text gaze patterns has been studied extensively, often in the context of probabilistic models of fixation timing and skipping (Feng, 2006;Hara et al., 2012;Matthies and Søgaard, 2013;Hahn and Keller, 2016) or token transition models that capture sequential dependencies across words (Nilsson and Nivre, 2009;Haji-Abolhassani and Clark, 2014;Coutrot et al., 2017). These models highlight the role of lexical frequency, syntactic complexity, and discourse structure when estimating fixation probabilities and dwell times. More recently, deep language features derived from contextual encoders have been used as feature extractors in modeling text saliency (Sood et al., 2020a), raising questions about the cognitive plausibility of representations learned from predictive objectives versus human reading strategies. However, existing approaches rarely contrast natural reading with goal-directed processing over the same textual inputs.

Eye-tracking signals for NLP promise both interpretability and potential performance gains across tasks. Human attention patterns can serve as auxiliary supervision, priors for token importance, or calibration signals for uncertainty estimates in modern architectures. Human attention patterns as regularization during model training have resulted in comparable or improved task performance in tagging part-of-speech (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015a,b;Barrett et al., 2018), sentence compression (Klerke et al., 2016), detecting sentiment (Mishra et al., 2016;Mishra et al., 2017), and reading comprehension (Malmaud et al., 2020), and related studies integrate gaze features for named entity recognition (Hollenstein and Zhang, 2019), machine translation (Miculicich et al., 2018), summarization (Mohammad et al., 2016), and multi-task learning with eye-tracking corpora such as Dundee, GECO, and ZuCo (Kennedy et al., 2003;Cop et al., 2017;Hollenstein et al., 2018). Complementary approaches leverage attention supervision from human fixations (Jain and Wallace, 2019), combine fixation duration and saccade statistics with contextual embeddings (Sood et al., 2020b), distill gaze to token-level importances (Pruthi et al., 2020), and evaluate robustness on cross-domain reading datasets (Bannur and Hovy, 2020).

From natural to task-specific reading, recent work has examined how reading strategies adapt to instructions, incentives, and downstream objectives. Studies combine eye-tracking with EEG to capture rapid attentional shifts and semantic integration (Wenzel et al., 2017) and with fMRI to localize networks implicated in language and control (Hillen et al., 2013;Choi et al., 2014). Research questions have focused on detecting relevant parts in text via saliency estimation (Loboda et al., 2011;Wenzel et al., 2017) and on differences between natural and pseudo-reading using syntactically or semantically degraded materials (Hillen et al., 2013) or pseudo-words (Choi et al., 2014). Yet, a systematic comparison of fixation behavior between natural reading and task-specific reading on classical NLP tasks such as relation extraction or sentiment classification remains largely unexplored, particularly with respect to how instruction-induced priorities reshape scanpaths, regressions, and re-reading under limited time or attention budgets. To the best of our knowledge, there has not been a controlled evaluation that links task framing to predictive utility of gaze features across multiple datasets and model families.

spans: Recent advances also explore self-supervision and transfer via large pre-trained language models using in-context learning and adapter-based tuning, alongside metric-learning baselines and episodic training protocols that benchmark cross-domain robustness (Brown et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2022;Rebuffi et al., 2017;Snell et al., 2017;Vinyals et al., 2016;Finn et al., 2017;Raghu et al., 2020;Ye et al., 2020).
document: Few-shot Learning is often tackled by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021;Bansal et al., 2020;Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Arthaud et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2021;Kumar et al., 2019), and prompt-based fine-tuning (Gao et al., 2021;Tam et al., 2021;Schick and Schütze, 2021). Basu et al. (2021) applies contrastive learning in their fewshot semi-supervised intent classification, by using hybrid pseudo-labels to mine positive pairs from unlabeled data. Despite these advances, transferring across domains and label spaces with minimal supervision remains challenging. Prior meta-learning methods typically assume stable task distributions and access to numerous episodes during training, which is rare in practical deployments. Data augmentation approaches can alleviate sparsity but often distort label semantics when perturbations are not label-preserving. Prompt-based methods leverage task templates and label verbalizers but can be brittle under domain shift. Recent advances also explore self-supervision and transfer via large pre-trained language models using in-context learning and adapter-based tuning, alongside metric-learning baselines and episodic training protocols that benchmark cross-domain robustness (Brown et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2022;Rebuffi et al., 2017;Snell et al., 2017;Vinyals et al., 2016;Finn et al., 2017;Raghu et al., 2020;Ye et al., 2020). In contrast, our work studies few-shot transfer with constrained adaptation budgets and explicit cost models for supervision. We formalize the setting where annotation cost is variable across classes and propose allocation strategies that prioritize informative examples. To enable fair comparison, we curate cross-domain benchmarks with matched label cardinalities and consistent evaluation protocols. Our approach integrates uncertainty-aware selection with structure-preserving augmentations that respect label semantics. We further disentangle gains from representation reuse and selection policies through ablations on pretraining corpora size and domain similarity. Empirically, we demonstrate improvements on intent classification and slot tagging under strict budget regimes. We also analyze failure modes, highlighting brittleness under extreme label imbalance and synonym-rich label spaces. Finally, we release code and datasets to facilitate reproducible research and standardized evaluation.

spans: Recent studies INFOXLM (Chi et al., 2021), HICTL , VECO  and ERNIE-M (Ouyang et al., 2021) use contrastive learning, back translation and other tricks further enhancing the performance of the multilingual model. Beyond these, approaches such as LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), RemBERT (Chung et al., 2021), and mDeBERTa (He et al., 2021) expand the multilingual pre-training landscape with larger vocabularies, sentence-level objectives, and disentangled attention. Work on dual-encoder retrieval for cross-lingual tasks (Ni et al., 2021; Reimers and Gurevych, 2020; Formal et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022) emphasizes alignment at the sentence and document levels. Complementary lines include multilingual adapters and lightweight finetuning (Pfeiffer et al., 2020; Ansell et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022; Vidoni et al., 2023), as well as curriculum and temperature-based sampling for data balancing (Conneau et al., 2020a; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020).

Besides, (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020;Artetxe et al., 2020;Libovický et al., 2020;Muller et al., 2021) have analyzed the cross-lingual ability of multilingual masked language models in terms of language similarity, shared model parameters cross languages, model structure, training objectives, language marker. Parallel examinations of representational geometry and probing (Sabet et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019; Gonen et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2020) document how layer-wise features evolve across languages. Studies of tokenization and subword overlap (Rust et al., 2021; Bostrom and Durrett, 2020; Park et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2020) investigate vocabulary effects, while analyses of training dynamics and scaling (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Tay et al., 2022) report sensitivity to data mixtures and objective weighting.

Multilingual Pre-Training mBERT and XLM-R train multilingual MLM without using any parallel corpus and show strong cross-lingual ability. mBERT is an extension of BERT which is pre-trained on Wikipedia data over 100 languages to learn a language-invariant feature space shared across multiple languages. XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020a) is trained on 2.5T data over 100 languages extracted from Common Crawl (Wenzek et al., 2020), which demonstrates the effect of the model trained on a large-scale corpus. Results of XLM-R on a large number of downstream cross-lingual tasks show that a large-scale training corpus can significantly improve the performance of multilingual models.

Other methods use parallel corpus in multilingual pre-training. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) introduces a Translation Language Model (TLM) based on the parallel corpus which shows significant improvement on downstream tasks. Unicoder (Huang et al., 2019) introduces a multitask learning framework to learn cross-lingual representations with monolingual and parallel corpora, achieving further gains. ALM (Yang et al., 2020) allows the model to learn cross-lingual code-switch sentences, enhancing the transfer ability. Recent studies INFOXLM (Chi et al., 2021), HICTL , VECO  and ERNIE-M (Ouyang et al., 2021) use contrastive learning, back translation and other tricks further enhancing the performance of the multilingual model. Beyond these, approaches such as LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), RemBERT (Chung et al., 2021), and mDeBERTa (He et al., 2021) expand the multilingual pre-training landscape with larger vocabularies, sentence-level objectives, and disentangled attention. Work on dual-encoder retrieval for cross-lingual tasks (Ni et al., 2021; Reimers and Gurevych, 2020; Formal et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022) emphasizes alignment at the sentence and document levels. Complementary lines include multilingual adapters and lightweight finetuning (Pfeiffer et al., 2020; Ansell et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022; Vidoni et al., 2023), as well as curriculum and temperature-based sampling for data balancing (Conneau et al., 2020a; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020). Our work focus on studying the model only with multilingual MLM, and leave the study of works with parallel data as future work.

Probing Multilingual MLM mBERT and XLM-R have successfully achieved excellent crosslingual transfer performance without using any parallel corpus. Researchers have wondered what the source of this cross-lingual ability is. (Pires et al., 2019) examines the zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on NER (Pan et al., 2017) and part-ofspeech (POS) tagging. They believe this success comes from the shared anchor words between languages. Not coincidentally, similar conclusion is reached by (Wu and Dredze, 2019). However, this conclusion is proven inaccurate by (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020). Their experiments show that the model still learns cross-lingual transfer ability on the corpus without anchor words at all. Besides, (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020;Artetxe et al., 2020;Libovický et al., 2020;Muller et al., 2021) have analyzed the cross-lingual ability of multilingual masked language models in terms of language similarity, shared model parameters cross languages, model structure, training objectives, language marker. Parallel examinations of representational geometry and probing (Sabet et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019; Gonen et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2020) document how layer-wise features evolve across languages. Studies of tokenization and subword overlap (Rust et al., 2021; Bostrom and Durrett, 2020; Park et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2020) investigate vocabulary effects, while analyses of training dynamics and scaling (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Tay et al., 2022) report sensitivity to data mixtures and objective weighting. The results suggest that structure similarity and shared parameters between languages are crucial for cross-lingual transfer. In this paper, we focus on analyzing language struc-ture. We decompose it into constituent order, composition, and word co-occurrence and study the effect of each part separately.

Non-Autoregressive TTS. Promising progress has taken place in non-autoregressive TTS systems to synthesize audio with high efficiency and high fidelity thank to the advancement in deep learning. A non-autoregressive TTS system maps the input text sequence into an acoustic feature or waveform sequence without using the autoregressive decomposition of output probabilities. Fast-Speech (Ren et al., 2019) and ParaNet (Peng et al., 2019) requires distillation from an autoregressive model, while more recent non-autoregressive TTS systems, including FastPitch (La'ncucki, 2021), AlignTTS (Zeng et al., 2020) and FastSpeech 2 (Ren et al., 2021), do not rely on any form of knowledge distillation from a pre-trained TTS system. In this paper, the proposed CUC-VAE TTS system is based on FastSpeech 2. FastSpeech 2 replaces the knowledge distillation for the length regulator in FastSpeech with mean-squared error training based on duration labels, which are obtained from frame-to-phoneme alignment to simplify the training process. Additionally, FastSpeech 2 predicts pitch and energy from the encoder output, which is also supervised with pitch contours and L2-norm of signal amplitudes as labels respectively. The pitch and energy prediction injects additional prosody information, which improves the naturalness and expressiveness in the synthesized speech.

Pre-trained Representation in TTS. It is believed that prosody can also be inferred from language information in both current and surrounding utterances (Shen et al., 2018;Fang et al., 2019;. Such information is often entailed in vector representations from a pre-trained LM, such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Some existing work incorporated BERT embeddings at word or subword-level into autoregressive TTS models (Shen et al., 2018;Fang et al., 2019).More recent work  used the chunked and paired sentence patterns from BERT. Besides, a relational gated graph network with pretrained BERT embeddings as node inputs  was used to extract word-level semantic representations, thus enhancing expressiveness.

(Zhu and Gupta, 2018) compared small-dense models and large-sparse models with the same parameters and showed that the latter outperforms the former, showing the large-sparse models have better expressive power than their small-dense counterparts. However, under the pretrain-and-finetune paradigm, pruning leads to overfitting as discussed. Knowledge Distillation (KD). As a common method in reducing the number of parameters, the main idea of KD is that the small student model mimics the behaviour of the large teacher model and achieves a comparable performance (Hinton et al., 2015;Mirzadeh et al., 2020). (Sanh et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2020;Sun et al., 2020) utilized KD to learn universal language representations from large corpus. However, current SOTA knowledge distillation methods are not able to achieve a high model compression rate (less than 10% remaining weights) while achieving an insignificant performance decrease. Progressive Learning. The key idea of progressive learning is that student learns to update module by module with the teacher.  utilized a dual-stage distillation scheme where student modules are progressively grafted onto the teacher network, it targets the few-shot scenario and uses only a few unlabeled samples to achieve comparable results on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. (Xu et al., 2020) gradually increased the probability of replacing each teacher module with their corresponding student module and trained the student to reproduce the behavior of the teacher.

To summarize, our contribution is determining the overfitting problem of pruning under the pretrainand-finetune paradigm and proposing the sparse progressive distillation method to address it. We demonstrate the benefits of the proposed framework through the ablation studies. We validate our method on six datasets from the GLUE benchmark.

To test if our method is applicable across tasks, we include the tasks of both single sentence and sentence-pair classification. Experimental results show that our method outperforms the leading competitors by a large margin. Network Pruning.  be reduced without sacrificing accuracy loss, such as magnitude-based pruning (Han et al., 2015) and ottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle and Carbin, 2018). (Zhu and Gupta, 2018) compared small-dense models and large-sparse models with the same parameters and showed that the latter outperforms the former, showing the large-sparse models have better expressive power than their small-dense counterparts. However, under the pretrain-and-finetune paradigm, pruning leads to overfitting as discussed. Knowledge Distillation (KD). As a common method in reducing the number of parameters, the main idea of KD is that the small student model mimics the behaviour of the large teacher model and achieves a comparable performance (Hinton et al., 2015;Mirzadeh et al., 2020). (Sanh et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2020;Sun et al., 2020) utilized KD to learn universal language representations from large corpus. However, current SOTA knowledge distillation methods are not able to achieve a high model compression rate (less than 10% remaining weights) while achieving an insignificant performance decrease. Progressive Learning. The key idea of progressive learning is that student learns to update module by module with the teacher.  utilized a dual-stage distillation scheme where student modules are progressively grafted onto the teacher network, it targets the few-shot scenario and uses only a few unlabeled samples to achieve comparable results on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. (Xu et al., 2020) gradually increased the probability of replacing each teacher module with their corresponding student module and trained the student to reproduce the behavior of the teacher. However, the performance on Transformer-based models of the aforementioned first method is unknown while the second method has an obvious performance drop with a low sparsity (50%).

Prechelt (1998) experimented on 14 different validation-based stop criteria. Prechelt (1998) focused on an issue that the validation error during training may represent many local minima prior to a global optimum.

Existing non-validation stop-criteria are generally based on statistical inference. Research on non-validation stopping has explored Bayesian and information-theoretic angles, including marginal-likelihood-based criteria derived from variational interpretations of stochastic optimization (Duvenaud et al., 2016), gradient evidence thresholds grounded in local curvature surrogates (Mahsereci et al., 2017), dimensionality-based indicators such as intrinsic dimensionality and its local variant LID for complexity tracking (Amsaleg et al., 2015;Lee and Chung, 2021), and heuristic pre-estimation of training horizons via repeated runs or cross-validation to set a stop epoch (Choi and Lee, 2021), alongside classical validation-error heuristics and patience schedules. Duvenaud et al. (2016) interpreted stochastic gradient descent in terms of the variational inference and proposed an estimation method for the marginal likelihood of the posterior, which was applied as an early stopping criterion. However, this method requires considerable computation for the Hessian, which is not practical in large models. Mahsereci et al. (2017) also proposed a gradient-related stopping method referred to as evidence-based stopping (EB). The EB-criterion is based on the fast-to-compute local statistics of the computed gradients. The criterion represents whether the gradients of the training samples lie within the expected range. Intrinsic dimensionality (ID), which refers to the minimum number of parameters required to represent a dataset, has been used for analyzing the training or redundancy of neural networks (Amsaleg et al., 2015). LID is a version of ID that estimates the subspace dimensions of the local regions. Lee and Chung (2021) found that LID works well as a stopping-criterion in several few-shot image classification datasets. Moreover, LID can be applied to unlabeled samples. Another method involves the pre-estimation of the the number of training epochs by training the model multiple times, such as cross validation (Choi and Lee, 2021); the model can stop at the pre-estimated (PE) stop-epoch when training all the labeled samples.

However, these methods have not been commonly studied for NLP tasks and do not consider the performance metrics during training. Furthermore, comparisons among the non-validation stopmethods have not been reported. In this study, we compare our method with the EB, LID, PE, and validation-based stopping methods on five text classification datasets. In this section we first discuss standard approaches to hate countering and studies on CN effectiveness on Social Media Platforms, then the existing CN data collection and generation strategies.

Hate countering. NLP has started addressing the phenomenon of the proliferation of HS by creating datasets for automatic detection (Mathew et al., 2021;Cao et al., 2020;Kumar et al., 2018;Hosseinmardi et al., 2015;Waseem, 2016;Burnap and Williams, 2016). Several surveys provide a review on the existing approaches on the topic (Poletto et al., 2020;Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017;Nunes and Fortuna, 2018), also addressing the ethical challenges of the task (Kiritchenko et al., 2020). Automatic detection of HS presents some drawbacks (Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). First of all, the datasets might include biases, and the models tend to replicate such biases (Binns et al., 2017;Davidson et al., 2019;Sap et al., 2019;Tsvetkov, 2020). Moreover, the end goals for which HS detection is employed are often charged with censorship of the freedom of speech by concerned users (Munger, 2017;Myers West, 2018). In this scenario, NGOs have started employing CNs to counter online hate. CNs have been shown to be effective in reducing linguistic violence (Benesch, 2014;Gagliardone et al., 2015;Schieb and Preuss, 2016;Silverman et al., 2016;Mathew et al., 2019); moreover, even if they might not influence the view of the extremists, they are still effective in presenting alternative and non-hateful viewpoints to the bystanders (Allison and Bussey, 2016;Anderson et al., 2014).

Prior work on generating or curating counter narrative (CN) resources spans semi-automatic authoring with expert templates, paraphrase-based augmentation, crowdsourced rewriting, stance-conditioned response generation, and retrieval-augmented conversational agents, with datasets and methods including CONAN (Chung et al., 2019), ALWYN (Fortuna et al., 2020), PERSPECTRUM (Chen et al., 2019), POLITICS-IR (Liu et al., 2020), counter-speech corpora collected from YouTube and Twitter (Mathew et al., 2019; Qian et al., 2019), style-transfer frameworks for toxicity reduction (Rizo et al., 2021), conditional generation with CTRL or GPT-2 prompts (Keskar et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2019), and retrieval-enhanced generation pipelines (Lewis et al., 2020; Thoppilan et al., 2022).

Data collection strategies for CNs have therefore ranged from manual expert annotation to large-scale scraping with keyword seeding and subsequent human filtering. Semi-structured interviews with moderators have also informed the design of category taxonomies for countering strategies, such as empathy, factual correction, or norm appeal. On the generation side, conditional language models fine-tuned on counter-speech datasets have been used to produce responses tailored to target groups and hate types. Recent studies incorporate safety filters and controllable attributes to maintain non-toxicity while preserving informativeness. Despite these advances, evaluation remains challenging due to context dependence, user reception, and platform moderation dynamics. Researchers have begun to adopt human-in-the-loop protocols and A/B testing on live platforms to estimate real-world impact. Complementary automatic metrics, such as stance shift or civility scores, have been proposed but lack robust validation across communities. Finally, cross-lingual CN transfer is underexplored, and multilingual benchmarks capturing cultural nuance are scarce. In what follows, we describe our dataset design criteria, ethical safeguards, and evaluation protocol.

Introduction

Word Analogy Recognition in NLP has long served as a litmus test for relational structure in distributed representations. Early benchmarks for word analogy recognition (Turney et al., 2003;Mikolov et al., 2013b;Gladkova et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018a) examine mostly linear relations between words (Ethayarajh et al., 2019). Such analogies can often be effectively solved by vector arithmetic for neural word embeddings, such as Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013a) and GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014). While this arithmetic view helped clarify geometric properties of embeddings, it also arguably narrowed the scope of analogical phenomena that benchmarks target.

Recent studies (Brown et al., 2020;Ushio et al., 2021) extend evaluation to pre-trained language models (PLMs) (Radford et al., 2019;Brown et al., 2020) on analogy benchmarks using both zero-shot prompting and few-shot in-context learning. These works reveal that prompt format, demonstration order, and surface cues can substantially affect apparent analogy performance. Complementary analyses show that models may leverage lexical overlap rather than abstract relation matching in certain settings. Together, these observations motivate revisiting benchmark design to separate rote association from genuine reasoning.

An exceptional benchmark is , where they build a knowledge-enhanced analogy benchmark that leverages word sense definitions in a commonsense knowledge base (Ma and Shih, 2018). By incorporating definitional context, that resource attempts to disambiguate polysemous terms and to foreground the relation rather than frequency-based co-occurrence. However, these benchmarks are mainly set up for evaluating learned representations, and few of them have investigated the transfer of analogical reasoning under distribution shift. This gap has practical implications for tasks requiring compositional generalization across domains and registers.

Prior efforts explore diverse angles on analogy and relational reasoning without a unifying perspective, including neural analogy solvers based on relation networks (Santoro et al., 2017), SAT-style analogy datasets such as PGM and Raven’s Progressive Matrices adaptations (Barrett et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2019), program-induction and symbolic approaches to analogical mapping (Gentner, 1983;Holyoak and Thagard, 1989;Lake and Baroni, 2018), and probing studies of relational structure in PLMs (Linzen, 2016;Goldberg, 2019;Tenney et al., 2019;Warstadt et al., 2020).

Despite these advances, robust analogical reasoning likely depends on external knowledge acquisition, sense disambiguation, and systematic mapping beyond surface similarity. Prior work in knowledge-augmented NLP provides tools to inject such information, including lexical resources (Miller, 1995), encyclopedic graphs (Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014), and commonsense repositories (Speer et al., 2017). Methods combining retrieval with generation have also shown promise for knowledge-intensive tasks. These trends suggest that representation learning and knowledge conditioning should be evaluated jointly in analogy settings.

Nevertheless, existing evaluations typically collapse multiple competencies—retrieval, disambiguation, and mapping—into a single accuracy metric. This conflation obscures where models fail and how training interventions should be targeted. Moreover, many datasets exhibit lexical memorization artifacts, allowing models to succeed without representing higher-order relations. A principled benchmark would isolate these competencies and test generalization across relation types, domains, and linguistic forms.

To this end, we consider the role of structured prompts for eliciting relational invariances from PLMs. The prompt format can emphasize role labeling of source and target domains, enforce analogy-consistent slot filling, and discourage reliance on token frequency. Careful prompt design can also enable counterfactual variants that test sensitivity to relation reversal. In practice, prompt engineering should be complemented by data construction that neutralizes spurious cues.

We further review evaluation protocols that go beyond top-1 accuracy. Calibrated confidence, consistency under paraphrase, and counterfactual robustness provide complementary signals of reasoning quality. Error analyses focusing on relation confusion versus entity confusion can reveal whether models misinterpret roles or misidentify candidates. Such fine-grained diagnostics are essential for tracing failure modes in complex analogy tasks.

In summary, the literature indicates that analogy evaluation must align representational geometry, linguistic variation, and knowledge grounding. While word-level analogies offer a convenient starting point, more comprehensive settings include phrasal, clausal, and multi-hop relational forms. Building such evaluations requires careful curation, explicit control of spurious correlations, and transparent reporting standards. These considerations frame the desiderata for a knowledge-intensive analogy benchmark.

However, existing resources offer only partial coverage of these desiderata. Many lack explicit sense definitions, rely on narrow relation taxonomies, or conflate memorization with reasoning. Others under-specify the knowledge prerequisites, making it difficult to assess generalization beyond training distributions. A systematic effort is needed to integrate definitional grounding with controlled variation and rigorous diagnostics.

Thus, the goal of this work largely differs from the conventional use of analogy datasets to probe embeddings. We aim to build a knowledge-intensive benchmark to encourage neural models to perform analogical mapping with explicit grounding. In doing so, we intend to separate representational adequacy from knowledge access and mapping fidelity. The following sections detail our benchmark design, construction protocol, and evaluation methodology.

Related Work

Distributional approaches to analogy leverage linear substructures in embedding spaces. The 3CosAdd and 3CosMul families remain standard baselines for vector arithmetic, though they are sensitive to hubness and frequency effects. Subsequent work studies isotropy and neighborhood structure to mitigate biases that inflate analogy scores. These techniques clarify when linear offsets approximate relational similarity.

PLM-based studies expand the paradigm via prompting and fine-tuning. Zero-shot prompts can surface latent relational templates, whereas few-shot settings supply alignment cues. Instruction-tuned models exhibit stronger adherence to role constraints but may overfit surface forms. Careful control of demonstrations and masking strategies reduces prompt-induced confounds.

Knowledge-enhanced methods integrate lexical and encyclopedic signals. Entity linking and definition retrieval can anchor senses before relation mapping. Graph paths and relation embeddings supply structured cues that complement distributional patterns. Hybrid retriever-reader architectures enable dynamic grounding during inference.

Evaluation best practices emphasize out-of-domain splits, counterfactuals, and adversarial minimal pairs. Reporting calibration, consistency, and ablation analyses supports reproducibility and fair comparison. Community benchmarks should publish artifact checks and bias audits to prevent overestimating reasoning capabilities. These norms collectively push the field toward more faithful assessments of analogical reasoning.

Many recent studies use external knowledge to improve the ERC performance. KET (Zhong et al., 2019) is used as external knowledge based on ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) and emotion lexicon NRC_VAD (Mohammad, 2018) as the commonsense knowledge. ConceptNet is a knowledge graph that connects words and phrases in natural language using labeled edges. NRC_VAD Lexicon has human ratings of valence, arousal, and dominance for more than 20,000 English words. COSMIC (Ghosal et al., 2020) and Psychological (Li et al., 2021) improve the performance of emotion recognition by extracting commonsense knowledge of the previous utterances. Commonsense knowledge feature is extracted and leveraged with COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019) trained with ATOMIC (The Atlas of Machine Commonsense) . ATOMIC has 9 sentence relation types with inferential if-then commonsense knowledge expressed in text. ToDKAT (Zhu et al., 2021) improves performance by combining commonsense knowledge using COMET and topic discovery using VHRED (Serban et al., 2017) to the model.

Ekman (Ekman, 1992) constructs taxonomy of six common emotions (Joy, Sadness, Fear, Anger, Surprise, and Disgust) from human facial expressions. In addition, Ekman explains that a multimodal view is important for multiple emotions recognition. The multi-modal data such as MELD and IEMOCAP are some of the available standard datasets for emotion recognition and they are composed of text, speech and vision-based data. Datcu and Rothkrantz (2014) uses speech and visual information to recognize emotions, and (Alm et al., 2005) attempts to recognize emotions based on text information. MELD and ICON (Hazarika et al., 2018a) show that the more multi-modal information is used, the better the performance and the text information plays the most important role. Multimodal information is not always given in most social media, especially in chatbot systems where they are mainly composed of text-based systems. In this work, we design and introduce a text-based emotion recognition system using neural networks.

In the previous studies, such as Hazarika et al. (2018b); Zadeh et al. (2017); , most works focused on dyadic-party conversation. However, as the multi-party conversation datasets including MELD and EmoryNLP have become available, a lot of recent research is being conducted on multi-party dialogues such as ; Jiao et al. (2020); Ghosal et al. (2020). In general, the multi-party conversations have higher speaker dependency than the dyadic-party dialogues, therefore have more conditions to consider and result in poor performance. Zhou et al. (2018); Zhang et al. (2018a) shows that commonsense knowledge is important for understanding conversations and generating appropriate responses.  reports that the lack of external knowledge makes it difficult to classify implicit emotions from the conversation history. EDA (Bothe et al., 2020) expands the multi-modal emotion datasets by extracting dialog acts from MELD and IEMOCAP and finds out that there is a correlation between dialogue acts and emotion labels. Prior work spans a wide range of resources and techniques, including graph-augmented encoders and commonsense generators, such as ConceptNet-based injectors (Speer et al., 2017), emotion lexicons like NRC_VAD (Mohammad, 2018), generative commonsense models including COMET trained on ATOMIC (Bosselut et al., 2019), knowledge-enriched architectures like KET and COSMIC (Zhong et al., 2019; Ghosal et al., 2020), and topic or dialogue structure models such as VHRED and ToDKAT (Serban et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2021), as well as multimodal benchmarks and corpora like MELD, IEMOCAP, and ICON (Poria et al., 2019; Busso et al., 2008; Hazarika et al., 2018a).

Injecting Structured Knowledge into LMs. Conceptually, our work is most related to recent work on knowledge injection into PLMs. Know-BERT (Peters et al., 2019) connects entities in text and KGs via an entity linker and then recontextualizes BERT representations conditioned on the KG embeddings. KG-BERT (Yao et al., 2019) trains BERT directly on knowledge triples by linearizing their entities and relations into a sequence and predicting plausibility of the sequence. Wang et al. (2021a) improve KG-BERT by splitting a subject-relation-object knowledge triple into a subject-relation pair representation and an object entity representation, then modeling their similarities with a dual/Siamese neural network. TuckER (Balažević et al., 2019), RotatE (Sun et al., 2019), TransE (Bordes et al., 2013), and ComplEx (Trouillon et al., 2016) offer complementary formulations for reasoning over triples, and retrieval-augmented architectures (Lewis et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020) explore external memory for factual queries, while prompt-based factual probing has been surveyed in Petroni et al. (2019), Roberts et al. (2020), and Liu et al. (2021a). While prior studies have focused on incorporating monolingual (English) structured knowledge into PLMs, our work focuses on connecting knowledge in many languages, allowing knowledge in each language to be transferred and collectively enriched.

Multilingual LMs pretrained via MLM, such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), cover 100+languages and are the starting point (i.e. initialization) of Prix-LM. With the notable exception of Calixto et al. (2021) who rely on the prediction of Wikipedia hyperlinks as an auxiliary/intermediate task to improve XLM-R's multilingual representation space for crosslingual transfer, there has not been any work on augmenting multilingual PLMs with structured knowledge. Previous work has indicated that off-the-shelf mBERT and XLM-R fail on knowledge-intensive multilingual NLP tasks such as entity linking and KG completion, and especially so for low-resource languages (Liu et al., 2021b). These are the crucial challenges addressed in this work.

KB Completion and Construction. Before PLMs, rule-based systems and multi-staged information extraction pipelines were typically used for automatic KB construction (Auer et al., 2007;Fabian et al., 2007;Hoffart et al., 2013;Dong et al., 2014). However, such methods require expensive human effort for rule or feature creation (Carlson et al., 2010;Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014), or they rely on (semi-)structured corpora with easy-to-consume formats (Lehmann et al., 2015). Petroni et al. (2019) showed that modern PLMs such as BERT could also be used as KBs: querying PLMs with fill-in-the-blank-style queries, a substantial amount of factual knowledge can be extracted. This in turn provides an efficient way to address the challenges of traditional KB methods. Jiang et al. (2020) and Kassner et al. (2021) extended the idea to extracting knowledge from multilingual PLMs.

Work in monolingual settings closest to ours is COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019): Prix-LM can be seen as an extension of this idea to multilingual and cross-lingual setups. Prix-LM's crucial property is that it enables knowledge population by transferring complementary structured knowledge across languages. This can substantially enrich (limited) prior knowledge also in monolingual KBs.

In another line of work, multilingual KG embeddings (Chen et al., 2017Sun et al., 2020aSun et al., , 2021 were developed to support cross-KG knowledge alignment and link prediction. Such methods produce a unified embedding space that allows link prediction in a target KG based on the aligned prior knowledge in other KGs (Chen et al., 0). Research on multilingual KG embeddings has made rapid progress recently, e.g., see the survey of Sun et al. (2020b). A parallel thread surveys multilingual and cross-lingual KG alignment and completion methods that build shared embedding spaces or learn mapping functions between KGs (Chen et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2020a; Pei et al., 2019; Ali et al., 2021), examines cross-lingual entity linking and mention grounding with Wikipedia-derived supervision (Tsai and Roth, 2016; Sil et al., 2018; Upadhyay et al., 2018; Botha et al., 2020), and evaluates multilingual PLMs on knowledge-intensive tasks using datasets such as XOR-TyDi QA (Asai et al., 2021), X-FACT (Mackenzie et al., 2022), MKQA (Longpre et al., 2021), and Mewsli-X (Botha et al., 2020). However, these methods focus on a closed-world scenario and are unable to leverage open-world knowledge from natural language texts. Prix-LM combines the best of both worlds and is able to capture and combine knowledge from (multilingual) KGs and multilingual texts.

Introduction

Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved great success in recent years across a broad spectrum of NLP tasks. Their capacity to capture linguistic and world knowledge, however, comes at the cost of substantial computational budgets during inference. High latency and energy consumption impede real-time or resource-constrained deployment, motivating a line of work on accelerating PLM inference without severely degrading accuracy.

Two major strategies have emerged for efficient inference. The first seeks to reduce the computation required by compressing the model through techniques such as knowledge distillation, pruning, and quantization. The second activates only a subset of parameters or layers at run time, often using confidence-based early exits or input-conditioned routing. These approaches have demonstrated notable speed-ups while retaining competitive task performance in many settings.

Recent advances span structured pruning for attention and feed-forward blocks (Michel et al., 2019;Voita et al., 2019;Lagunas et al., 2021), low-bit post-training and quantization-aware techniques for transformers (Shen et al., 2020;Zafrir et al., 2019;Dettmers et al., 2022), distillation schemes including task-specific, patient, and multi-teacher variants (Sanh et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2020;Turc et al., 2019), and early-exit or adaptive-depth mechanisms such as DeeBERT, PABEE, LeeBERT, and CascadeBERT (Xin et al., 2020;Zhou et al., 2020;Zhu, 2021;Li et al., 2021a). Despite promising empirical gains, deployment choices remain sensitive to model size, dataset difficulty, and hardware characteristics.

At the same time, selective computation methods aim to allocate effort where it matters most. Universal Transformers adapt depth per token via halting policies, while confidence-driven early-exit methods terminate inference once a threshold is met. Mixture-of-experts and routing-based layers further reduce average computation by activating a subset of experts per input. These designs offer a complementary axis to compression and can be combined for additional efficiency.

Nevertheless, several open challenges persist for practitioners. Early-exit systems often rely on auxiliary classifiers at intermediate layers, introducing training complexity and extra parameters. Distillation objectives may fail to transfer calibration and uncertainty estimates, which are crucial for confident stopping decisions. Quantization can degrade numerical stability for attention operations without careful calibration.

Motivated by these gaps, we investigate a hash-based early-exit strategy that avoids auxiliary supervision. Our approach leverages inexpensive similarity signals to decide whether further layers are necessary for a given input. By design, it integrates with off-the-shelf PLMs and requires minimal architectural changes. We evaluate the method across classification and sequence labeling tasks under strict latency budgets.

We study three questions: how reliably low-cost signals correlate with downstream confidence, how to schedule exits across depth to trade accuracy for speed, and how robustness behaves under distribution shift. To this end, we compare against representative baselines from compression, early-exit, and routing families under standardized budgets. We also analyze compute-accuracy Pareto fronts and calibration properties.

Our contributions are threefold. First, we formalize a training-free exit criterion based on hash-consistency of internal representations. Second, we provide a systematic evaluation across datasets and scales, with ablations that disentangle representation stability from classifier confidence. Third, we present deployment-oriented analysis on commodity GPUs and CPUs, detailing latency variance and memory footprints.

Related Work

Model compression via distillation, pruning, and quantization has been extensively explored for transformers. Knowledge distillation transfers behavior from a teacher to a smaller student, often with intermediate-layer matching and softened logits. Pruning methods remove redundant heads, neurons, or blocks based on saliency or sparsity-inducing regularizers. Quantization reduces arithmetic precision, sometimes with mixed-precision recipes to safeguard sensitive layers.

Selective activation and early-exit methods reduce average depth or width at inference time. Confidence-based exits insert lightweight classifiers at multiple layers, while adaptive depth halts when predictions stabilize. Other work considers dynamic token pruning or routing tokens to experts for conditional computation. These techniques can be orthogonal to compression and benefit from careful calibration.

Our study complements these lines by eliminating auxiliary heads and supervision for exit decisions. We show that hashing-based stability can act as a proxy for semantic convergence in hidden states. The resulting system delivers competitive speed-ups and favorable calibration without retraining.

In this section, we discuss various attempts on N-NER corpora. As shown in Table , existing N-NER corpora are mostly high-resource languages, i.e., English and German, while Vietnamese is the only Asian language that has an N-NER dataset.

In terms of the number of classes, it is also worthnoting that three out of six corpora has less than ten and only NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) has more than 100 classes. The details of these corpora are given as follows.  ACE-2004 (Doddington et al., 2004) and ACE-2005 (Walker et al., 2006) are early examples of N-NER datasets. ACE-2005 (Walker et al., 2006) dataset comprises 30,966 mentions from 12,548 sentences with 7 coarse-grained entity types. In addition to N-NER annotations, ACE-2005 also contains labels for other tasks such as recognition of relation and event extraction. GENIA  introduces an N-NER data for bioinformatics. This project provides a high-quality corpus annotated for biological entity names. The dataset composed of 2,000 abstracts, 92,681 mentions from 9,533 sentences with 32 entity types.

NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) is a recent large finegrained N-NER dataset composed of 114 classes. Unlike previous N-NER corpora, the NNE dataset annotates entities with more details. For example, "6 September 2019", a date named entity mention, in the NNE dataset, each element in this mention is annotated with finer detail, "6" is annotated with day tag, "September" with month tag, and "2019" with year tag. NoSta-D (Benikova et al., 2014)  DAN+ (Plank et al., 2020) presents the first N-NER dataset for Danish. This work investigates the possibility of transfer-learning between languages for the N-NER task. Moreover, DAN+ is a multi-domain dataset; they also study the challenges of domain-shift in their dataset. The dataset contains 6,425 mentions, 130,095 tokens, 4 classes from 6,867 sentences, obtained from multiple domains such as news and social media (Reddit, Twitter, and Arto). NoSta-D, VLSP-2018, and DAN+ have a modest corpus size and a small number of entity types comparing to the NNE dataset. This shows that there is still a resource gap for non-English corpora. On the other hand, for Thai, there are only coarsegrained flatten-NER datasets which are publicly available. NoSta-D (Benikova et al., 2014), VLSP-2018 (Nguyen et al., 2018), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), OntoNotes 5.0 (Pradhan et al., 2013), CoNLL-2003 (Sang and De Meulder, 2003), Polyglot-NER (Al-Rfou et al., 2015), and CrossNER (Rossiello et al., 2021) are frequently referenced in discussions of named entity recognition resources across languages and domains.

In this section, we introduce background knowledge about contrastive learning and our phrase encoder LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020).

Contrastive learning aims to learn representations by bringing semantically similar views closer while pushing dissimilar ones apart. A canonical formulation maximizes agreement between augmented views of the same instance under an InfoNCE objective with temperature scaling and large batches to approximate negatives. Recent work on contrastive learning spans instance discrimination with noise-contrastive estimation and memory banks (Wu et al., 2018), momentum contrast and dynamic dictionaries (He et al., 2020), MoCo v2 and stronger augmentations (Chen et al., 2020b), SimCLR with large-batch training and projection heads (Chen et al., 2020a), BYOL and target network prediction (Grill et al., 2020), SimSiam without negative pairs (Chen and He, 2021), as well as supervised contrastive formulations for labels (Khosla et al., 2020). We focus on text where augmentations include dropout-based views, token masking, and span deletion, and where negatives can be drawn in-batch or via memory mechanisms.

For textual encoders, contrastive losses are often coupled with transformer backbones and pretraining corpora of varying domain coverage. The stability of training depends on temperature, batch size, and the use of predictors or momentum encoders that mitigate collapse. Beyond instance-level views, phrase-aware or span-level alignment can leverage syntactic constituents and named entities to shape invariances. However, alignment quality is sensitive to augmentation semantics, and phrase perturbations can inadvertently remove meaning if not constrained.

LUKE extends transformer architectures with dedicated entity embeddings and an entity-aware attention mechanism to jointly encode tokens and entities. It introduces entity positions and type embeddings to inform attention about mention boundaries and knowledge-linked nodes. Prior studies on entity- and phrase-aware encoders include contextual representations with entity-aware attention (Peters et al., 2019), span-based pretraining with span corruption (Joshi et al., 2020), entity masking and knowledge grounding in K-Adapter (Wang et al., 2021), knowledge-embedded transformers such as ERNIE (Zhang et al., 2019), and phrase-level supervision in span-based contrastive objectives (Gunasekara et al., 2021). LUKE is pre-trained on large corpora with entity annotations and optimized to capture interactions between surface forms and linked entities.

In practice, integrating contrastive objectives with LUKE requires defining positive views that preserve entity spans and negative sampling that avoids trivial lexical overlaps. We consider span-consistent masking, entity-level dropout, and augmentation policies that maintain mention boundaries. We also discuss training schedules that interleave masked language modeling with contrastive steps to stabilize optimization. Finally, we outline evaluation settings on semantic similarity, entity typing, and phrase retrieval to assess the benefits of phrase-aware contrast.

Knowledge Probing Benchmarks for PLMs.

LAMA (Petroni et al., 2019), which starts this line of work, is a collection of single-token knowledge triples extracted from sources including Wikidata and ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017). To mitigate the problem of information leakage from the  head entity, Poerner et al. (2019) propose LAMA-UHN, which is a hard subset of LAMA that has less token overlaps in head and tail entities. X-FACTR (Jiang et al., 2020a) and mLAMA  extend knowledge probing to the multilingual scenario and introduce multi-token answers. They each propose decoding methods that generate multi-token answers, which we have shown to work poorly on MedLAMA. BioLAMA (Sung et al., 2021) is a concurrent work that also releases a benchmark for biomedical knowledge probing. We provide a comparison between LAMA, BioLAMA and MedLAMA in terms of (# relations, # queries, avg # answers per query, avg # characters per answer) in the Appendix. 12 Probing via Prompt Engineering. Knowledge probing is sensitive to what prompt is used (Jiang et al., 2020b). To bootstrap the probing performance, Jiang et al. (2020b) mine more prompts and ensemble them during inference. Later works parameterised the prompts and made them trainable (Shin et al., 2020b;Fichtel et al., 2021;Qin and Eisner, 2021). We have opted out promptengineering methods that require training data in this work, as tuning the prompts are essentially tuning an additional (parameterised) model on top of PLMs. As pointed out by Fichtel et al. (2021), prompt tuning requires large amounts of training data from the task. Since task training data is used, the additional model parameters are exposed to the target data distribution and can solve the set set by overfitting to such biases . In our work, by adaptively finetuning the model with a small set of raw sentences, we elicit the knowl- 12 Our comparison indicated that MedLAMA and BioLAMA have no overlaps in queries, allowing both resources to complement each other. edge out from PLMs but do not expose the data biases from the benchmark (MedLAMA). Biomedical Knowledge Probing. Nadkarni et al. (2021) train PLMs as KB completion models and test on the same task to understand how much knowledge is in biomedical PLMs. BioLAMA focuses on the continuous prompt learning method OptiPrompt (Zhong et al., 2021), which also requires ground-truth training data from the task. Overall, compared to BioLAMA, we have provided a more comprehensive set of probing experiments and analysis, including proposing a novel probing technique and providing human evaluations of model predictions.

Abstractive Opinion Summarization aims to generate a fluent summary that reflects salient opinions in input reviews. Due to the lack of sufficient amount of reference summaries, the most common solution is the unsupervised approach (Chu and Liu, 2019;Bražinskas et al., 2020b;Amplayo et al., 2021;Elsahar et al., 2021;Im et al., 2021;Wang and Wan, 2021;Isonuma et al., 2021, inter alia).

Recent opinion summarization models use the few-shot learning approach that fine-tunes a pretrained Transformer model with a limited amount of pairs of input reviews and reference summaries. Bražinskas et al. (2020a) and Oved and Levy (2021) show that the few-shot learning approach substantially outperforms unsupervised learning models.

All the existing methods listed above are designed for general opinion summarization and, thus, are not necessarily suitable for comparative opinion summarization, as shown in the experiments. Comparative Summarization There is a line of work on extracting comparative information from single/multiple documents. Lerman and McDonald (2009) defined the contrastive summarization problem and presented early work on the problem. Their method selects sentences so that two sets of summaries can highlight differences. Wang et al. (2013) developed an extractive summarization method for a problem of Comparative Document Summarization, which is to select the most discriminative sentences from a given set of documents. Bista et al. (2019) tackled a similar problem by selecting documents that represent in-cluster documents while they are useful to distinguish from other clusters.

Other studies (Kim and Zhai, 2009;Huang et al., 2011;Sipos and Joachims, 2013;Ren et al., 2017) tackled similar tasks by developing extracting sentences/phrases from given sets of documents for comparative document analysis. Topic models have been also used to capture comparative topics for better understanding text corpora, but they do not generate textual summaries (Ren and de Rijke, 2015;He et al., 2016;Ibeke et al., 2017).

Recent advances in neural summarization provide complementary perspectives that have influenced how models are pretrained, adapted, and evaluated across domains, including sequence-to-sequence pretraining with denoising objectives (Lewis et al., 2020), instruction tuning for generalization (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022), contrastive learning for representation robustness (Gao et al., 2021; Giorgi et al., 2021), prompt-based and in-context learning with large language models (Brown et al., 2020; Min et al., 2022), coverage and salience modeling in abstractive summarization (See et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019), evaluation with reference-free metrics (Bhandari et al., 2020; Laban et al., 2022), and datasets emphasizing opinion-centric or factual summarization such as Yelp, Amazon, Rotten Tomatoes, CNN/DailyMail, and XSum.

Our work differs from the existing work in two points. First, none of them focuses on generating common summaries. Second, all of the previous studies for contrastive summary generation use the extractive approach. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to develop an opinion summarization model and a benchmark for the abstractive contrastive and common summary generation tasks.

A key finding in previous studies in discourse analysis is that most sentences have an independent well-formed sub-tree in the full document-level discourse tree (Joty et al., 2012). Researchers have taken advantage of this finding to build parsers that utilize different granularity levels of the document to achieve the state-of-the-art results (Kobayashi et al., 2020). This design has been empirically verified to be a generally advantageous framework, improving not only works using traditional feature engineering (Joty et al., 2013;Wang et al., 2017), but also deep learning models (Jia et al., 2018b;Kobayashi et al., 2020). We, therefore, introduce this design to our dependency parsing framework. Specifically, sub-trees for each sentence in a discourse are first built separately, then assembled to form a complete discourse tree. However, our model differs from prior works in that we make a clear distinction to derive better contextualized representations of EDUs from fine-tuning BERT separately for intra-and intersentential levels to dynamically capture different information needed for discourse analysis at different levels. We are also the first to design stacked sequence labeling models for discourse relation identification so that its hierarchical structure can explicitly capture both intra-sentential and intersentential writing patterns.

In the case of implicit relations between EDUs without clear connectives, it is crucial to introduce sequential information from the context to resolve ambiguity. Feng and Hirst (2014) rely on linearchain CRF with traditional feature engineering to make use of the sequential characteristics of the context for discourse constituent parsing. However, they greedily build up the discourse structure and relations from bottom up. At each timestep, they apply the CRF to obtain the locally optimized structure and relation. In this way, the model assigns relation gradually along with the construction of the parsing tree from bottom up, but only limited contextual information from the top level of the partially constructed tree can be used to predict relations. Besides, at each time-step, they sequentially assign relations to top nodes of the partial tree, without being aware that those nodes might represent different levels of discourse units (e.g. EDUs, sentences, or even paragraphs). In contrast, we explicitly train our sequence labeling models on both intra-and inter-sentential levels after a complete discourse tree is constructed so that we can infer from the whole context with a clear intention of capturing different writing patterns occurring at intra-and inter-sentential levels. Recent advances in representation learning for discourse and related structured prediction tasks span latent tree induction (Kim et al., 2019), neural CRF and semi-CRF architectures (Ma and Hovy, 2016; Sarawagi and Cohen, 2004), contextual encoders such as ELMo and BERT (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019), hierarchical transformers for document modeling (Yang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020), discourse parsing with pointer networks (Li et al., 2018), and graph-based methods that leverage dependency structures (Kiperwasser and Goldberg, 2016; Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017).
