Introduction

Cross-lingual and multilingual machine reading comprehension (MRC) evaluates whether models can understand passages and answer questions across languages without task-specific supervision in the target language. Hsu et al. (2019) investigate cross-lingual transfer capability of multilingual BERT (mBERT) on MRC tasks and find that zeroshot learning based on PLM is feasible, even between distant languages, such as English and Chinese. Various approaches have been proposed on top of multilingual MRC based on PLMs. Hsu et al. (2019) investigate cross-lingual transfer capability of multilingual BERT (mBERT) on MRC tasks and find that zeroshot learning based on PLM is feasible, even between distant languages, such as English and Chinese, while Cui et al. (2019) propose a method that combines multilingual BERT and back-translation for cross-lingual MRC, and other multilingual PLM-based MRC systems report competitive zero-shot transfer through translation-based alignment, adversarial training, and data augmentation (Artetxe et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; Conneau et al., 2020), alongside benchmarks that consolidate cross-lingual evaluation protocols across typologically diverse languages (Clark et al., 2020; Longpre et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Xue et al., 2021). In order to effectively leverage translation signals in the absence of target-language labels, several works explore round-trip translation and consistency regularization to create pseudo-parallel supervision. Different from the above studies, we mainly consider the impact of syntactic divergences between the source and target language in zero-shot crosslingual transfer based on multilingual PLMs, and attempt to disassociate semantics from syntax and only transfer semantic to the target language.

Related Work: Disentangled Representation Learning

Recently, there has been a growing amount of work on learning disentangled latent representations in NLP tasks (Zhang et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018). The vMF-Gaussian Variational Autoencoder (VGVAE) model proposed by Chen et al. (2019) is a generative model using two latent variables to represent semantics and syntax of the sentence, developed for monolingual settings and trained with paraphrases. It uses paraphrase reconstruction loss and a discriminative paraphrase loss to learn semantic representations and word order information for syntactic representations. Recent research in multilingual disentanglement spans contrastive learning for semantic invariance (Fang et al., 2020; Giorgi et al., 2021; Pan et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020), structured latent-variable models for syntax modeling (Kim et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Zhou and Neubig, 2021), probing studies that quantify what PLMs encode about POS and dependency relations (Tenney et al., 2019; Hewitt and Liang, 2019; Rogers et al., 2020; Pimentel et al., 2020), and cross-lingual alignment techniques leveraging parallel corpora or dictionaries to stabilize representations (Lample et al., 2018; Ruder et al., 2019; Artetxe et al., 2018; Schuster et al., 2019), with datasets that emphasize typological variety and evaluation fidelity (Bender, 2011; Ponti et al., 2019; Zeman et al., 2020; Goyal et al., 2022). We adapt the VGVAE model to multilingual syntax-semantics disentanglement using bilingual sentence pairs to train our model with a cross-lingual reconstruction loss and semantic discrimination loss. To better disentangle semantics from complex and diverse syntax in multilingual PLMs, we introduce two additional syntax-related losses for incorporating POS tags and syntax trees. We further discuss design choices for latent dimensionality, regularization coefficients, and sampling strategies to ensure stable training across languages.

Overview of Our Approach

Our method operates in a zero-shot transfer setting from English to multiple target languages using a single multilingual encoder. We first obtain latent semantic and syntactic variables and then enforce language-invariant semantics via bilingual contrastive objectives. We incorporate universal POS taggers and projected dependency trees as weak supervision for the syntax channel without requiring target-language task labels. We evaluate on cross-lingual MRC benchmarks spanning typologically diverse families and report improvements under strict zero-shot conditions. Finally, we analyze robustness to word order variation and morphological richness to illuminate the role of syntactic divergence in cross-lingual transfer.

Multiple efforts have looked into defining, measuring, and mitigating biases in NLP models (Sun et al., 2019;Mehrabi et al., 2019a;Sheng et al., 2021). Dwork et al. (2012) formalized individual fairness in terms of similar treatment for similar individuals, connecting algorithmic decisions to task-specific similarity metrics and sparking a line of metric-learning approaches for fairness. Multiple recent works also focus on developing new dataset and associated metrics to capture various types of biases. For example,  and Nangia et al. (2020) propose dataset and metrics to measure social biases and stereotypes in language model generations, Bolukbasi et al. (2016); Caliskan et al. (2017); Manzini et al. (2019) define metrics to access gender and race biases in word vector representations, and  define metrics to quantify and mitigate biases in visual recognition task. Ethayarajh (2020) propose Bernstein bounds to represent uncertainty about the bias. Majority of these bias metrics are automatically computed, for example, using a regard classifier (Sheng et al., 2019), sentiment classifier , toxicity classifier (Dixon et al., 2018) or true positive rate difference between privileged and underprivileged groups (De-Arteaga et al., 2019b). A few works additionally validate the alignment of these automatically computed bias metrics with human understanding of biases by collecting annotations of biases on a subset of test data from crowdworkers (Sheng et al., 2019;. Blodgett et al. (2021Blodgett et al. ( , 2020 discuss the limitations of several of these bias datasets and measurements.

However, the majority of existing bias metrics are specific to the model type and the application domain used, they may not be tested for correlation with human judgement of biases, and their relationship to existing definitions of fairness has not been explored. Additionally, metrics such as true positive or error difference between groups requires ground truth labels, thereby making their computation in real-time systems difficult. Speicher et al. (2018) have attempted to present unified approach to measuring group and individual fairness via inequality indices, however we note that such metrics are non-trivial to extend to unstructured data such as text. For example, gender information in a text may be subtle (e.g. mention of softball) and it is unclear whether presence of this word should be considered to impact the genderness of the text. Research on fairness in machine learning has also examined group fairness constraints and individual fairness notions in supervised classification and ranking, explored counterfactual fairness and causal perspectives, and investigated auditing practices and bias mitigation strategies across modalities (Hardt et al., 2016;Dwork et al., 2012;Kilbertus et al., 2017;Kusner et al., 2017;Barocas et al., 2019;Wadsworth et al., 2018;Friedler et al., 2019;Zhao et al., 2017). Accumulated prediction sensitivity metric, presented in this paper, attempts to address all the above limitations of existing bias metrics. We acknowledge that the proposed metric is yet to be associated with other notions of fairness (e.g. preference based notion of fairness (Zafar et al., 2017)).

Several approaches to zero-shot cross-lingual transfer exist and can broadly be divided into: a) Databased Transfer, which focuses on training data transformation and b) Model-based Transfer that centres around modifying models' training routine.

Data-based Transfer Translating utterances for the intent classification task is relatively straightforward so previous works focused on projecting and/or aligning the entity labels between translated utterances (Dou and Neubig, 2021). This technique is followed by standard supervised training with those pseudo-labels and is commonly known as a translate-train method. One of the earliest works still being used for this purpose is fastalign (Dyer et al., 2013). It's an unsupervised word aligner trained on a parallel corpus to map each word (thus its entity label) in the source utterance to the word(s) in the target user utterance. Projecting the entity labels can also be done with word-by-word translation and source label copying (Yi and Cheng, 2021). A teacher model then weakly labels the target data, which is used to train the final student model. Sometimes, this type of label projection is complemented with an additional entity alignment step (Li et al., 2021a). Better performance can be achieved by using machine translation with entity matching and distributional statistics (Jain et al., 2019) though this can be a costly process for each language. A category of 'word substitution' methods such as code-switching (Qin et al., 2020;Kuwanto et al., 2021) or dictionary-enhanced pretraining have also been shown to improve cross-lingual transfer.

Model-based Transfer Prior to the adoption of multilingual transformers (Lample and Conneau, 2019), task-oriented XNLU methods employed a BiLSTM encoder combined with different multilingual embeddings (Schuster et al., 2019). Newer approaches usually involve a pretrained XLM and the addition of some new training component with the inference routine remaining mostly unchanged. Xu et al. (2020) learn to jointly align and predict entity labels by fusing the source and target language embeddings with attention and using the resulting cross-lingual representation for entity prediction. Qi and Du (2020) include an adversarial language detector in training whose loss encourages the model to generate language-agnostic sentence representations for improved zero-shot trans-fer. Pan et al. (2020) and Chi et al. (2020) added a contrastive loss to pretraining that treats translated sentences as positive examples and unrelated sentences as negative samples. This training step helps the XLM produce similar embeddings in different languages. However, these methods require large annotated datasets and expensive model pretraining (Chi et al., 2020). Our proposed methods only use the English task data (which is relatively limited) and its translations for each language.

Recent works explore multilingual pretraining objectives and alignment strategies across a range of architectures and datasets without converging on a single paradigm (Lample and Conneau, 2019; Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Ruder et al., 2021; Lauscher et al., 2020; Keung et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Pires et al., 2019), and complementary lines investigate data augmentation and pseudo-labeling for cross-lingual NLU (Ni and Florian, 2019; Rahimi et al., 2019; Ponti et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2021), while others analyze cross-lingual generalization failures and robustness (K et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021), along with evaluations on benchmarks such as XTREME and XGLUE (Hu et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021b).

The most related prior works are Arivazhagan et al. (2019) for machine translation and for task-oriented XNLU. Both of these are cross-lingual alignment methods that use translated training data to zero-shot transfer the source language model to the target language. We focus on the latter work, called XeroAlign, which reported the most recent SOTA scores on our evaluation datasets. XeroAlign works by generating a sentence embedding of the user utterance for each language, e.g. English (source) and Thai (target) using the CLS token of the XLM. A Mean Squared Error loss function minimises the difference between the multilingual sentence embeddings and is backpropagated along with the main task loss. XeroAlign aims to bring sentence embeddings in different languages closer together with a bias towards intent classification due to the CLS embedding, which is the standard input to the intent classifier. We reproduce this method for analysis and comparisons but add a small post-processing step that distinctly improves the reported scores.

This section reports a survey on closely related popular QA datasets that 1) focus on narratives and/or 2) are designed for educational purposes (dataset features in Table ) 1.
We structure the review by narrative source (stories, literature, curricula) and by the cognitive skills each dataset elicits.
Narrative datasets typically vary along three axes: passage length, question style, and answer format.
Educationally oriented datasets additionally encode curricular alignment and assessment objectives.
Prior work highlights both the potential and the pitfalls of constructing questions that require synthesis across multiple sentences.
Supervision signal, annotation protocol, and evidence marking are especially consequential for downstream evaluation.
Prior narrative QA corpora span a wide design space in supervision, annotation granularity, and reasoning depth, including NarrativeQA (Kociský et al., 2018), RACE (Lai et al., 2017), MCTest (Richardson et al., 2013), SQuAD 2.0 (Rajpurkar et al., 2018), DREAM (Sun et al., 2019), Cosmos QA (Huang et al., 2019), QuAC (Choi et al., 2018), CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), and ARC (Clark et al., 2018), as well as benchmarks emphasizing explanation such as e-SNLI (Camburu et al., 2018) and ERASER (DeYoung et al., 2020), along with multi-hop datasets like HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018) and QAngaroo (Welbl et al., 2018), program-induction oriented benchmarks such as DROP (Dua et al., 2019) and GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019), teacher-student style examination sets like BioASQ (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015) and PubMedQA (Jin et al., 2019), and dialogue-centric resources including DSTC7-8 (Gunasekara et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2019).
We consider two complementary dimensions when comparing these resources: whether questions are answerable without external background knowledge, and whether rationales are explicitly annotated.
Along the first dimension, narrative QA often assumes familiarity with events and characters, whereas educational QA tends to anchor questions to curricular texts and standards.
Along the second, rationalized datasets provide token- or span-level evidence, whereas others rely on post-hoc model explanations.
We adopt a standardized schema to record document provenance, narrative form, and evaluation protocol.
This schema facilitates consistent reporting of training/dev/test splits, licensing, and demographic coverage where available.
We further separate datasets by the degree of temporal reasoning required versus static factual retrieval.
Temporal reasoning is especially salient in long-form narratives with flashbacks or parallel subplots.
In contrast, short-answer educational items frequently emphasize definitional clarity and concept recall.
We also note the prevalence of multiple-choice formats in standardized exams relative to free-form span extraction in open-domain settings.
Taken together, these observations motivate an apples-to-apples comparison of narrative and educational QA under unified evaluation criteria.

2.1 Pre-trained language model (PLM)

Most modern language models are based on the transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture. The PLMs generally use only the encoder block of the transformer, which consists of two sublayers, a self-attention layer, and a feed-forward layer. BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) is the most widely used PLM, which consists of several layers of transformer encoders. The BERT was pre-trained for 4 days with a large amount of text data, which consists of 3.3 billion words, using masked language modeling and the next sentence prediction task in an unsupervised manner. This pre-trained parameter can be easily used in various downstream tasks by fine-tuning the model using the labeled dataset. Since the success of BERT, a variety of similar PLMs have emerged, including RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), ALBERT (Lan et al., 2020), ELECTRA (Clark et al., 2020), XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), DeBERTa (He et al., 2021), ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019), SpanBERT (Joshi et al., 2020), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), and DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019). In contrast to autoencoding models that mask tokens, some architectures incorporate permutation-based objectives or replaced-token detection to improve sample efficiency and generalization. These design choices affect how contextual information is integrated across layers, which in turn influences transfer performance on token-level and sentence-level tasks. Moreover, architectural refinements such as disentangled attention, relative position encoding, and parameter sharing have been proposed to control capacity while retaining expressivity. Pre-training corpora also vary widely in size and domain composition, from curated encyclopedic text to web-scale mixtures that emphasize diversity and coverage. Alongside objectives and data, optimization strategies—learning rate schedules, large-batch training, and mixed precision—contribute substantially to stability and convergence speed. Despite these differences, the fine-tuning paradigm has remained dominant for adapting PLMs to specific tasks with minimal architectural changes. Recent studies further explore prompt-based adaptation and lightweight parameter-efficient tuning to reduce task-specific overhead. Evaluation continues to highlight trade-offs between efficiency and accuracy, particularly when models are deployed under constrained compute or latency budgets. Finally, reproducibility and fair comparison require standardized benchmarks, transparent reporting of pre-training datasets, and careful control of hyperparameters across baselines. In the following sections, we discuss how these choices interface with downstream modeling for our target application and outline the experimental settings used to assess their impact on robustness.

spans: Explainability evaluation methods (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017) include measuring faithfulness (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), enabling model simulatability (Hase et al., 2019), behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020), diagnostic tasks and challenge sets (Gardner et al., 2020;Nie et al., 2020;Kiela et al., 2021;Sobieszczańska et al., 2022), perturbation- and counterfactual-based probes (Ross et al., 2017;Kim et al., 2018;Wachter et al., 2017;Karimi et al., 2020), and evaluating intelligent user interactions (Nguyen et al., 2018), with additional survey perspectives outlining taxonomies and desiderata (Guidotti et al., 2018;Miller, 2019;Arrieta et al., 2020;Molnar, 2022).

Within misinformation research, fact verification datasets and benchmarks have proliferated across modalities and settings, including LIAR (Wang, 2017), FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), MultiFC (Augenstein et al., 2019), COVID-19 rumor corpora (Shahi and Nandini, 2020;Hossain et al., 2020), social-context datasets for claim spread (Vosoughi et al., 2018;Shu et al., 2020), stance and evidence corpora (Mohtarami et al., 2018;Hanselowski et al., 2018;Allaway and McKeown, 2020), and propaganda-annotated resources (Da San Martino et al., 2019;Da San Martino et al., 2020;Barrón-Cedeño et al., 2020), alongside model families spanning retrieval-augmented transformers (Lewis et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), rationale extraction methods (Lei et al., 2016;DeYoung et al., 2020;Jain et al., 2020;Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019), and stance-aware graph models (Yao et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2021;Baly et al., 2018;Guo et al., 2018).

Explainable classification Unlike post-hoc analysis approaches for explainability (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Sundararajan et al., 2017), prototype classification networks (Li et al., 2018;Hase et al., 2019) are white-box models with explainability built-in via case-based reasoning (Kolodner, 1992) rather than extractive rationales (Lei et al., 2016;Bastings et al., 2019;Jain et al., 2020;Glockner et al., 2020). They are the neural variant of prototype classifiers (Bien and Tibshirani, 2011;Kim et al., 2014), predicting based on similar known instances. Contemporary work (Rajagopal et al., 2021) also stressed the importance of "global" explainability through training examples, yet in their approach, the similar training examples are not directly integrated in the decision itself; in contrast, we do so via learned prototypes to provide more transparency.

Our work builds on Li et al. (2018), which we lay out in Section 3.1. Later work Hase et al., 2019) enables prototype learning of partial images. In NLP, Guu et al. (2018) retrieved prototype examples from the training data for edit-based natural langauge generation. Hase and Bansal (2020) used a variant of 's work to examine among other approaches; unlike our work, they used feature activation to obtain explanations similar to post-hoc approaches, and did not handle the absence of relevant content.

Evaluating explainability Explainability is a multi-faceted problem. HCI concerns include: a) For whom are we designing the explanations? b) What goals are they trying to achieve? c) How can we best convey information without imposing excessive cognitive load? and d) Can explainable systems foster more effective human+AI partnerships (Amershi et al., 2019;Wickramasinghe et al., 2020;Liao et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021;Bansal et al., 2021)? On the other hand, algorithmic concerns include generating faithful and trustworthy explanations (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), local vs. global explanations, and post-hoc vs. self-explanations (Danilevsky et al., 2020).

Explainability evaluation methods (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017) include measuring faithfulness (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), enabling model simulatability (Hase et al., 2019), behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020), diagnostic tasks and challenge sets (Gardner et al., 2020;Nie et al., 2020;Kiela et al., 2021;Sobieszczańska et al., 2022), perturbation- and counterfactual-based probes (Ross et al., 2017;Kim et al., 2018;Wachter et al., 2017;Karimi et al., 2020), and evaluating intelligent user interactions (Nguyen et al., 2018), with additional survey perspectives outlining taxonomies and desiderata (Guidotti et al., 2018;Miller, 2019;Arrieta et al., 2020;Molnar, 2022).

Human+AI fake news detection While explainable fact-checking (Kotonya and Toni, 2020b) could better support human-in-the-loop factchecking (Nakov et al., 2021;Demartini et al., 2020), studies rarely assess a human+AI team in combination (Nguyen et al., 2018). In fact, hu-man+AI teams often under-perform the human or AI working alone (Bansal et al., 2021), emphasizing the need to carefully baseline performance.

Propaganda detection (Da San Martino et al., 2019) occupies the broader space of detecting disinformation. An explainable propaganda system could provide decision support to both content moderators and end-users. Because propaganda detection is hard task for non-expert users and the stateof-the-art models are not accurate enough for practical use, explainability may help in larger adoption of computational propaganda detection systems (Martino et al., 2020). Our approach of building a white box model with human evaluation -rooted in an human+AI partnership -aims to bring automated models closer to practical adoption.

Within misinformation research, fact verification datasets and benchmarks have proliferated across modalities and settings, including LIAR (Wang, 2017), FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), MultiFC (Augenstein et al., 2019), COVID-19 rumor corpora (Shahi and Nandini, 2020;Hossain et al., 2020), social-context datasets for claim spread (Vosoughi et al., 2018;Shu et al., 2020), stance and evidence corpora (Mohtarami et al., 2018;Hanselowski et al., 2018;Allaway and McKeown, 2020), and propaganda-annotated resources (Da San Martino et al., 2019;Da San Martino et al., 2020;Barrón-Cedeño et al., 2020), alongside model families spanning retrieval-augmented transformers (Lewis et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), rationale extraction methods (Lei et al., 2016;DeYoung et al., 2020;Jain et al., 2020;Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019), and stance-aware graph models (Yao et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2021;Baly et al., 2018;Guo et al., 2018).

Pretrained LM in Open-domain dialogue Many prior works tried to pretrain the models on large-scale social comment chains data like Reddit to model conversational behavior (Zhang et al., 2020; Adiwardana et al., 2020), followed by finetuning on the diverse target dialogue dataset to improve engagingness and humanness . To avoid undesired behaviors of the models including toxicity and bias from the human-human conversation, they merely exclude some parts of training data using automatic filtering by predefined criteria.

Open-domain dialogue systems increasingly rely on large-scale pretraining for linguistic competence and world knowledge. Early sequence-to-sequence approaches were supplanted by transformer-based architectures that scaled parameters and data. Instruction tuning further aligned general-purpose LMs with dialogue objectives through curated conversational prompts and feedback. Safety concerns motivated the adoption of lightly supervised detoxification in both pretraining and finetuning stages. Despite progress, robust mitigation of harmful content remains an open problem.

Recent work spans neural conversational models and instruction-tuned systems, including DialoGPT (Zhang et al., 2020), Meena (Adiwardana et al., 2020), BlenderBot (Roller et al., 2021), GPT-3 style few-shot prompting (Brown et al., 2020), InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022), RLHF pipelines (Stiennon et al., 2020), safety-constrained decoding methods like PPLM (Dathathri et al., 2020), and detoxification approaches using RealToxicityPrompts (Gehman et al., 2020) and Perspective API filters (Lees et al., 2022).

Filtering methods typically use lexicon-based rules, toxicity classifiers, or blocklists to remove high-risk samples. While effective at scale, these strategies suffer from domain shift and can degrade model coverage of nuanced language. Post-hoc decoding constraints reduce exposure to harmful continuations but may conflict with fluency and relevance. Reinforcement learning from human feedback offers finer control over value alignment. However, aligning preferences for safety without sacrificing informativeness requires careful reward design and evaluation. Existing evaluations report mixed outcomes across demographic and topical slices.

Datasets for dialogue safety and alignment include curated adversarial probes, moderated forum excerpts, and red-team dialogues. Metrics span automatic toxicity scores, refusal rates, and human-rated helpfulness and harmlessness. Nevertheless, measuring generalization to unseen contexts continues to be challenging. Concurrently, retrieval-augmented dialogue can amplify or attenuate risk depending on document selection quality. Overall, the literature underscores a tension between expressiveness and safety that motivates revisiting data curation, objective design, and evaluation protocols.

Quality Estimation Current SOTA models in sentence-level QE, which is typically framed as a regression task, mainly use multilingual representations from pre-trained transformers (Devlin et al., 2018), notably XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2019). The input to a sentence-level QE model is a concatenation of the source and translated sentences, separated by the [SEP] token. The sequence is encoded by the pre-trained Transformer model, and the [CLS] token is passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) layer to obtain a sentence-level score. During fine-tuning both the parameters of the pre-trained model and the parameters corresponding to the MLP layer are updated.

Word-level QE is typically addressed as a binary classification task, where the QE model needs to predict a binary label indicating whether a word is correct or wrong for each word in the MT output (Lee, 2020). As illustrated in Figure  (left), some supervised approaches use both sentence-level and word-level objectives in a multi-task setting, which results in superior performance (Kim et al., 2017;Lee, 2020). Methods that do not require word-level training data either need access to the MT model (Rikters and Fishel, 2017;Fomicheva et al., 2020b), or still treat the problem as a supervised task but use synthetically generated data for supervision (Tuan et al., 2021).

Rationale Extraction for NLP SOTA NLP models based on deep neural networks achieve high performance in a variety of tasks, often at the cost of interpretability (Lipton, 2016). Recent work aims to address this issue by focusing on two different goals. On the one hand, the aim is to produce justifications for model predictions that are plausible to the users, in order to increase users' trust (Ribeiro et al., 2016). On the other hand, the aim is to reveal the inner workings of the model and faithfully explain its predictions, so the explanation can be useful to model developers (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020).

Typically, explainability methods operate by selecting a portion of the input that justifies model prediction for a single data point. This can be done either by modifying the model architecture, or by trying to explain the predictions of a given model. The first type of approaches (a.k.a. rationalization by construction) involves imposing restrictions on the generated rationales to satisfy certain constraints, e.g. compactness (Yu et al., 2019;Chalkidis et al., 2021). Note that such restrictions often result in lower performance and indeed are not guaranteed to explain the behaviour of an unconstrained model (Jain et al., 2020). The second type of approaches (the so called post hoc) usually rely on feature attribution methods, which assign an importance value to each input feature of a network (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Schulz et al., 2020). These methods do not allow for introducing useful biases during training, but focus on faithfully explaining model behaviour.

Feature attribution has a long tradition in image recognition tasks (Simonyan et al., 2013) and only recently have been applied to some NLP tasks, most commonly text classification (DeYoung et al., 2019). QE is fundamentally different from text classification where clues are typically separate words or phrases (Zaidan et al., 2007) which often can be considered independently of the rest of the text. This independence assumption does not hold for the task of evaluating translation quality where a word cannot be identified as a clue (e.g. translation error) without considering the surrounding context. Furthermore, SOTA NLP models based on contextualized representations for input words make rationale extraction especially challenging, as the representation for a given word can encode not only the word identity but also its interactions with other words in the text. Recent work has revealed various interesting properties that characterize the information flow through hidden layers in deep transformer models (Voita et al., 2019;De Cao et al., 2020;Yun et al., 2021). We provide additional insights on this topic in Section 5.2.

Several studies assess domain adaptation with synthetic data, leveraging in-domain monolingual corpora for targeted improvement (Chu and Wang, 2018;Freitag and Al-Onaizan, 2016;Currey et al., 2017), while others examine multilingual transfer where synthetic data is generated via pivoting or zero-shot directions (Johnson et al., 2017;Aharoni et al., 2019;Arivazhagan et al., 2019). Research has also considered synthetic supervision for morphological richness and low-resource settings using subword segmentation and character-level decoders (Sennrich et al., 2016b;Ataman and Federico, 2018;Kreutzer et al., 2020;Bojar et al., 2019).

In parallel, evaluation of synthetic translations has been discussed through automatic metrics like BLEU and chrF (Papineni et al., 2002;Popović, 2015), learned metrics such as COMET and BLEURT (Rei et al., 2020;Sellam et al., 2020), calibration and uncertainty estimates for synthetic-label reliability (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019;Malinin and Gales, 2018), and human assessments focusing on adequacy and fluency in noisy-channel or teacher-student setups (Yuan et al., 2021;Kocmi and Federmann, 2021).

Introduction

Synthetic Translations Generating synthetic translations has mainly been studied as a means of data augmentation for NMT through forward translation (Zhang and Zong, 2016) or backtranslation (Sennrich et al., 2016a;Marie et al., 2020) of monolingual resources. Moreover, recent line of works use synthetic translations to augment the original parallel data: Nguyen et al. (2020) diversify the parallel data via translating both sides using multiple models and then merge them with the original to train a final NMT model; Jiao et al. (2020) employ a similar approach to rejuvenate inactive examples that contribute the least to the model performance. Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations. While its original goal was to guide the training of a student model of small capacity with the output of a teacher of high capacity, distillation is also necessary to effectively train some categories of MT architectures such as non-autoregressive models (Gu et al., 2017). While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021). Beyond data augmentation, prior work has explored synthetic translations in a variety of training regimes, including iterative back-translation (Hoang et al., 2018;Edunov et al., 2018), tagged back-translation to control noise (Caswell et al., 2019), dual learning for mutual reinforcement of source and target models (He et al., 2016;Xia et al., 2017), curriculum strategies that schedule synthetic and real data (Zhang et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2018), and quality-aware filtering of synthetic corpora using cross-entropy or language model scores (Moore and Lewis, 2010;Junczys-Dowmunt, 2018). Several studies assess domain adaptation with synthetic data, leveraging in-domain monolingual corpora for targeted improvement (Chu and Wang, 2018;Freitag and Al-Onaizan, 2016;Currey et al., 2017), while others examine multilingual transfer where synthetic data is generated via pivoting or zero-shot directions (Johnson et al., 2017;Aharoni et al., 2019;Arivazhagan et al., 2019). Research has also considered synthetic supervision for morphological richness and low-resource settings using subword segmentation and character-level decoders (Sennrich et al., 2016b;Ataman and Federico, 2018;Kreutzer et al., 2020;Bojar et al., 2019).

Despite this breadth of work, there remains a need to clarify when synthetic data behaves as a substitute versus a complement to authentic bitext across architectures and data regimes. Existing analyses have emphasized distributional simplification, regularization effects, and noise properties, but they often conflate model capacity with data quality factors. In low-resource conditions, synthetic corpora can dominate training dynamics and bias lexical choice, whereas in high-resource settings they may serve primarily as regularizers that smooth hypothesis spaces. A principled account must therefore separate effects due to sampling strategy, translation directionality, and filtering criteria. We also note that reliance on round-trip or teacher-generated hypotheses can entrench systematic errors, motivating careful curation. In particular, we investigate how signal-to-noise trade-offs interact with tokenization schemes and decoding temperature to shape downstream generalization.

We build on the observation that synthetic translations alter conditional entropy and calibration in ways that depend on the match between monolingual and parallel domains. Our study operationalizes this via controlled mixtures of real and synthetic data, fixing model capacity and decoding parameters while varying synthetic quality and coverage. We compare autoregressive and non-autoregressive decoders under identical data budgets to isolate data effects. To mitigate confounds, we align tokenization, vocabulary size, and training schedules across settings and audit label noise via reference-based and reference-free estimators. The experimental design targets attribution of gains to data composition rather than architecture-specific tricks.

Our contributions are threefold. First, we present a taxonomy of synthetic data roles—augmentation, substitution, and regularization—and tie each to measurable properties of source and target distributions. Second, we provide a causal analysis framework based on interventions on sampling and filtering pipelines, including tagged versus untagged synthetic data. Third, we release reproducible recipes and diagnostics that quantify simplicity, coverage, and calibration in synthetic corpora. In parallel, evaluation of synthetic translations has been discussed through automatic metrics like BLEU and chrF (Papineni et al., 2002;Popović, 2015), learned metrics such as COMET and BLEURT (Rei et al., 2020;Sellam et al., 2020), calibration and uncertainty estimates for synthetic-label reliability (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019;Malinin and Gales, 2018), and human assessments focusing on adequacy and fluency in noisy-channel or teacher-student setups (Yuan et al., 2021;Kocmi and Federmann, 2021).

We position this work within ongoing efforts to understand data-centric training strategies for NMT and to delineate principled guidelines for mixing synthetic and authentic resources. The following sections detail our empirical setup, intervention design, and evaluation protocols.

Conversational question answering. In recent years, several conversational question answering datasets have emerged, such as QuAC (Choi et al., 2018), CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), and DoQA (Campos et al., 2020). Different from single-turn QA datasets (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), CQA requires the model to understand the question in the context of conversational history. There have been many methods proposed to improve CQA performance (Ohsugi et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Qu et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2021) and significant improvement has been made on CQA benchmarks. Recent advances in modeling have also been reported across pre-training and response selection, with approaches leveraging context-aware attention, hierarchical encoders, and span-based retrievers on prominent datasets and benchmarks (Qu et al., 2020;Yatskar, 2019;Lewis et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;Huang et al., 2021;Gao et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021;Reddy et al., 2019), along with multi-task learning and domain adaptation techniques that integrate external supervision signals and curriculum strategies (Zhang et al., 2021;Kumar and Talukdar, 2020;Liu et al., 2020;Talmor et al., 2019).

Besides text-based CQA tasks, there also exist CQA benchmarks that require other forms of modeling ability, such as combining textual evidence with background knowledge (Saeidi et al., 2018), utilizing structured knowledge base (Saha et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2018), as well as CQA in other modalities (Das et al., 2017). These extensions broaden the scope of conversational understanding and present different forms of context grounding. In turn, they emphasize the need for methods that can track dialogue states, disambiguate co-references, and manage long-term dependencies. They also introduce evaluation complexities that go beyond single utterance quality and hinge on multi-turn coherence.

Evaluation with predicted history. Only recently has it been noticed that the current method of evaluating CQA models is flawed. Mandya et al. (2020); Siblini et al. (2021) point out that using gold answers in history is not consistent with the real-world scenario and propose to use predicted history for evaluation. Building on this critique, subsequent studies explored how error propagation affects downstream reasoning and retrieval under imperfect histories. However, most automated metrics remain insensitive to pragmatic repair and contextual grounding that humans readily perform during conversation. To address this mismatch, we design a large-scale human study focusing on faithfulness under noisy histories and calibrate automatic proxies against these judgments. We further analyze the distribution of error types in predicted histories and introduce a question rewriting protocol that improves robustness without requiring oracle interventions. Our findings inform the construction of future CQA benchmarks and provide guidance for evaluation under realistic deployment constraints.

Introduction

Natural language inference (NLI) has catalyzed interest in interpretable reasoning under the broader agenda of trustworthy NLP. Early benchmarks such as SNLI and MultiNLI standardized evaluation and encouraged methods that justify entailment decisions with explanations. As models scaled, interpretability shifted from post-hoc heuristics toward structured, human-readable rationales that align with model predictions. The community has progressively framed interpretability not only as a diagnostic tool but also as a training signal that can shape model behavior.

Motivation and scope

Despite strong predictive performance, neural NLI systems often exploit shortcuts and artifacts, motivating explanation methods that expose evidence and reasoning chains. Sentence-level explanations, in particular, provide a compact unit for conveying multi-hop support while remaining accessible to non-expert annotators. At the same time, interpretability research now interfaces with data curation, adversarial evaluation, and learning paradigms that jointly optimize prediction and explanation quality. We focus on sentence-level interpretability for NLI-style judgments, aiming to situate methods that integrate explanations with model training and inference.

Background on forms of interpretation

Feature-based interpretation aggregates signals from internal representations to highlight salient cues and has been adapted to transformers via attention rollout and input gradient analyses. Token-level rationales aim to mark contiguous spans but can struggle to capture discourse-level relations and causal structure. Sentence-level interpretations promise higher readability and facilitate evaluation against annotated evidence sets.

Related work landscape

With the great success of natual language inference, many recent works explore the interpretability of neural networks through providing interpretation to support their inference results (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Chen et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2018;Thorne et al., 2019;Kumar and Talukdar, 2020). Three forms of interpretation are provided by these works: (1) feature-based interpretation (Chen et al., 2016a(Chen et al., , 2018Ribeiro et al., 2016Ribeiro et al., , 2018Li et al., 2016;Nguyen, 2018;Feng et al., 2018;Gururan-gan et al., 2018) such as attention distribution (Xu et al., 2015), heatmap (Samek et al., 2017), alignment rationale (Jiang et al., 2021), gradients (Li et al., 2016), magnitude of hidden states (Linzen et al., 2016), etc.; (2) token-level interpretation that relatively easy to comprehend but prone to ambiguity (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Liu et al., 2018;Thorne et al., 2019), and (3) sentence-level interpretation which has the best human-readability Camburu et al. ( 2018); Talmor et al. (2018); Kumar and Talukdar (2020). Different from the previous work which only include one-side promotion, we proposed the mutual promotion mechanism that can improve the performance of both inference and sentence-level interpretation.

Positioning within interpretability research

Prior research spans a variety of interpretability strategies across tasks and modalities, including attention-based rationales (Jain and Wallace, 2019; Serrano and Smith, 2019), gradient and saliency methods (Simonyan et al., 2013; Sundararajan et al., 2017; Shrikumar et al., 2017), example-based explanations and influence functions (Koh and Liang, 2017; Han and Tsvetkov, 2020), concept activation vectors (Kim et al., 2018; Ghorbani et al., 2019), counterfactual data augmentation (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020), and evaluation datasets designed to probe spurious correlations and reasoning (Kaushik et al., 2021; Niven and Kao, 2019; McCoy et al., 2019; Poliak et al., 2018).

Bridging explanation and learning

Joint learning of predictions and explanations has been realized through multi-task objectives that penalize disagreement between model rationales and gold evidence. Rationale supervision can improve out-of-domain robustness by discouraging reliance on annotation artifacts. Furthermore, contrastive learning with explanation-aware augmentations has been shown to calibrate confidence while preserving task accuracy. These trends suggest that explanation signals can regularize representations toward causal features.

Sentence-level evidence in NLI

Datasets such as e-SNLI and Evidence Inference introduced natural language justifications and document-level evidence annotations. Subsequent corpora broadened the granularity from token spans to sentences and paragraphs, enabling evaluation of both sufficiency and minimality. Modeling advances include pointer networks over retrieved sentences and hierarchical encoders that propagate evidence summaries to the classifier. Retrieval-augmented architectures align the selection of supporting sentences with end-task loss.

Limitations of existing approaches

Post-hoc methods can be brittle under distribution shift, and attention rationales may not correspond to causal mechanisms. Token-level highlights rarely capture entailment structure requiring compositional reasoning across multiple premises. Supervised rationales often inherit dataset biases, complicating evaluation of faithfulness. Finally, pipeline designs that separate evidence selection from prediction can suffer from exposure bias and miscalibration.

Our perspective

We study how to couple sentence-level evidence extraction with entailment prediction through a shared training signal that rewards agreement and penalizes conflicts. Our framework operationalizes mutual promotion between tasks by jointly optimizing selection confidence, textual justification quality, and label probabilities. We further analyze the trade-offs between sparsity of selected sentences and completeness of support under adversarially constructed hypotheses.

Contributions

We present a unified model that integrates retrieval, sentence selection, and NLI classification with a consistency-driven objective. We introduce a calibration-aware rationale loss that aligns predictive entropy with evidence sufficiency. We also provide a benchmark protocol for faithfulness that combines counterfactual deletion tests with cross-dataset transfer. Our experiments demonstrate gains on multiple NLI-with-explanations datasets while improving robustness to artifacts.

Implications and outlook

By treating explanations as first-class training signals, interpretability moves from peripheral diagnostics to central components of learning. This framing invites richer forms of supervision, including weakly labeled evidence and human-in-the-loop correction. Future work will explore scalable annotation strategies and theoretical links between explanation sufficiency and generalization bounds.

Automatic evaluation of open-domain dialogue systems has a multifaceted nature with many finegrained quality aspects (Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Turn-level aspects show the quality of the system's utterance given a dialogue context from different perspectives including appropriateness, relevance, engaging, and etc (Lowe et al., 2017;Tao et al., 2018;Ghazarian et al., 2020). Whereas, conversation-level facets such as coherence, diversity, informative take into account the whole conversation and dialog flow (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2021;Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Dialogue coherence evaluation is pertinent to the discourse coherence as a dialogue is counted as a multi-party discourse. Similar to discourse coherence, many original coherence evaluation metrics derived from Centering Model for monitoring the local focus of utterances and the entities distribution over utterances (Grosz and Sidner, 1986;Miltsakaki and Kukich, 2004;Lapata and Barzilay, 2005). A group of studies assess the coherence of dialogues with respect to entities and dialogue acts (Cervone and Riccardi, 2020;Mesgar et al., 2020). Another inspected approach for dialogue coherence evaluation is to represent dialogue in a structured graph format where contextually dependent neighbor utterances or concepts are connected nodes in the graph (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020). Graph convolutional networks are used to complete this task.

High quality training dataset is identified as one of the momentous and indelible components in automatic coherence evaluation. Some previous works construct such datasets by collecting human judgments (Higashinaka et al., 2014;Cervone and Riccardi, 2020). While many recent works rely on a more timely and costly affordable approach by automatically generating negative samples. The utterances of the coherent conversations from humanhuman interactions are manipulated by shuffling  their order, inserting or replacing irrelevant utterances (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In this work, we show that such changes can not truly represent machine generated incoherent conversations. Prior work introduces a variety of automatic metrics and resources for dialogue assessment without a unifying perspective on their interplay, including embedding-based metrics like BERTScore and BLEURT (Zhang et al., 2020; Sellam et al., 2020), reference-free turn-level estimators such as ADEM and USR (Lowe et al., 2017; Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020), conversation-level graph-structured evaluators (Vakulenko et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2020), and datasets constructed via perturbation protocols or crowd judgements (Higashinaka et al., 2014; Mesgar et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). We concentrate on coherence because downstream user satisfaction correlates with logically consistent exchanges in multi-turn settings. We therefore focus our review on methods that explicitly model entity continuity, discourse relations, and turn-taking structure. Entity-centric approaches operationalize local coherence by tracking mentions and role continuity over time. Dialogue-act aware methods augment this with pragmatic labels to capture adjacency pair expectations. Graph-based approaches externalize these dependencies as nodes and edges and then apply message passing to estimate consistency. Recent neural metrics attempt to learn such signals implicitly from large annotated or perturbed corpora. Yet their calibration under distribution shift between synthetic and model-produced errors remains uncertain. Consequently, we examine dataset construction strategies that aim to approximate real incoherence patterns. We analyze whether perturbation recipes produce artifacts that evaluators can shortcut. We also discuss evaluation protocols that reduce overlap between train and test distortions. Finally, we outline desiderata for future benchmarks that capture longer-range dependencies and topical drifts. Taken together, these considerations motivate a closer look at how training data shape coherence evaluators' inductive biases.

We review prior work on visual semantic AI, on the geometry and semantic properties of representations formed by language models, and on semantic intrinsic evaluation tasks.

A central theme in visual semantic AI is aligning perceptual signals with linguistic structure to enable recognition, grounding, and reasoning under distribution shift.

Recent studies document a wide range of techniques and benchmarks across multimodal vision-language understanding, compositional generalization, and semantic probing, including CLIP for contrastive pretraining on image–text pairs, ViLBERT and LXMERT for early cross-modal fusion, ALIGN for large-scale noisy web supervision, Florence and BEiT-3 for unified encoders, SimVLM and CoCa for generative pretraining with caption prefixing, BLIP and BLIP-2 for bootstrapped language–image pretraining, Flamingo for few-shot multimodal in-context learning, PaLI and PaLI-X for multilingual multimodal modeling, and datasets such as COCO Captions, Flickr30k, VQAv2, NLVR2, TextCaps, Visual7W, Hateful Memes, OK-VQA, VizWiz, and A-OKVQA, as well as evaluation protocols like zero-shot retrieval, zero-shot classification via text prompts, linear probing on frozen embeddings, and compositional splits exemplified by COGS and Winoground.

Concurrently, research into the geometry of language model representations examines linearity, clustering, and manifold structure to account for semantic regularities.

Work on linear probes, causal mediation analysis, and representation arithmetic suggests that token and sentence embeddings admit directions corresponding to attributes, relations, and pragmatic frames.

Intrinsic evaluations have sought to quantify such properties using word similarity, semantic textual similarity, lexical substitution, and concept categorization alongside sentence-level diagnostics.

However, these metrics can be confounded by surface form overlap, frequency effects, and annotation artifacts that inflate correlations without measuring semantics per se.

In multimodal contexts, intrinsic probes extend to cross-attention visual grounding checks, retrieval alignment scores, and caption–image consistency diagnostics.

Despite growing sophistication, many benchmarks remain saturated, motivating shifts toward harder compositional and out-of-distribution tests.

Such tests include adversarial caption edits, counterfactual scene graphs, and language-only decoys designed to stress reliance on spurious cues.

Parallel threads study calibration, uncertainty, and robustness, emphasizing that semantic competence includes well-calibrated abstention and epistemic humility.

Work on controllable generation and instruction tuning brings further considerations about disentangling semantic knowledge from formatting priors.

Progress in scalable pretraining and data curation has also foregrounded dataset governance, deduplication, and bias mitigation as determinants of representational semantics.

The relationship between representation geometry and downstream semantic control is increasingly probed with sparse activation steering and targeted finetuning.

Moreover, evaluations that blend intrinsic and extrinsic signals—such as probing before and after task finetuning—illuminate brittleness and transfer gaps.

In vision-language modeling, causal interventions on image regions and textual spans reveal how alignment errors propagate to reasoning tasks.

Large-scale multilingual settings introduce cross-lingual transfer as an additional axis for semantic evaluation and failure analysis.

Unified encoder–decoder architectures promise shared semantic scaffolds but raise questions about modality-specific interference.

Finally, recent community efforts emphasize reproducible protocols, open evaluation suites, and transparent reporting to contextualize claims about semantic competence.

spans: Relevant nonparametric and retrieval-augmented translators include kNN-LM for language modeling and sequence prediction (Khandelwal et al., 2020), kNN-MT for test-time adaptation via external memories (Khandelwal et al., 2021), similarity-based reranking in translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Khayrallah et al., 2018), cache-based NMT adaptation (Kuang et al., 2018; Tu et al., 2018), exemplar-based decoding with translation memories (Farajian et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021), and retrieval-augmented generation with dense indices (Lewis et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022; Izacard et al., 2022), alongside domain adaptation surveys that catalog data selection, fine-tuning, and meta-learning strategies (Chu and Wang, 2018; Ramponi and Plank, 2020; Koehn and Knowles, 2017; Kocmi and Bojar, 2018).
document:
In this section, we will briefly introduce the background of adaptive kNN-MT (Zheng et al., 2021a). Adaptive kNN-MT is derived from kNN-MT (Khandelwal et al., 2021) by inserting a lightweight Meta-k Network that fuses kNN retrievals with various k to alleviate the possible noise induced by a single k. Formally, it is formulated as two steps: target-side datastore creation and Metak Network predictions. Target-side Datastore Creation. The datastore constists of a set of key-value pairs. Given a bilingual sentence pair (s, t) in a corpus (S, T ), a pretrained general domain NMT model autoregressively extracts the context representation h i of the i-th target token conditioned on both source and target context (s, t <i ), denoted as

The datastore is finally constructed by taking h i as keys and t i as values. Relevant nonparametric and retrieval-augmented translators include kNN-LM for language modeling and sequence prediction (Khandelwal et al., 2020), kNN-MT for test-time adaptation via external memories (Khandelwal et al., 2021), similarity-based reranking in translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Khayrallah et al., 2018), cache-based NMT adaptation (Kuang et al., 2018; Tu et al., 2018), exemplar-based decoding with translation memories (Farajian et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021), and retrieval-augmented generation with dense indices (Lewis et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022; Izacard et al., 2022), alongside domain adaptation surveys that catalog data selection, fine-tuning, and meta-learning strategies (Chu and Wang, 2018; Ramponi and Plank, 2020; Koehn and Knowles, 2017; Kocmi and Bojar, 2018). Meta-k Network Prediction. Meta-k Network (f β ) is a two-layer feed-forward network followed by a non-linear activation function. Based on the constructed datastore, it considers a set of various ks that are smaller than an upper bound K, the standard setting is k ∈ Q where Q = {0} ∪ {2 j |j ∈ N, 2 j ≤ K}. K nearest neighbors of the current context query ĥi from the datastore are first retrieved at the i-th decoding step. Then the l 2 distance from ĥi to each neighbor (h j , v j ) is denoted as d i = h j , ĥi 2. And the count of distinct values in top j neighbors are denoted as c j. The normalized weights of each available k are obtained. Finally, the ultimate prediction probability is ensembled. Note that a validation set is usually required to study the Meta-k Network before predicting on test.

spans: A broad body of related work spans compression-focused simplification, lexical substitution resources, and evaluation protocols, including document-level simplification with discourse constraints (Xu et al., 2015;Sulem et al., 2018), paraphrase generation with neural pretraining (Lewis et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2020), controllable text generation via attribute conditioning (Keskar et al., 2019;He et al., 2020), interpretable edit-based frameworks (Alva-Manchego et al., 2017;Dong et al., 2019), and common benchmarks and metrics such as Newsela, WikiLarge, SARI, and BLEU (Xu et al., 2016;Zhang and Lapata, 2017;Krishna et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2016).
document:
Early work on simplification relied on rules, e.g., to split or shorten long sentences (Chandrasekar and Srinivas, 1997;Carroll et al., 1998;Vickrey and Koller, 2008). Later work treated simplification as a monolingual phrase-based machine translation (MT) task (Coster and Kauchak, 2011;Wubben et al., 2012), with syntactic information added, such as constituency trees (Zhu et al., 2010). Recent work, reviewed below, leverages neural models in a generative and revision-based manner.

Supervised Generative Methods employ Seq2Seq (Sutskever et al., 2014) models to implicitly learn simplification operations from aligned complex-simple sentence pairs. Inspired by the success of Seq2Seq neural MT models, Nisioi et al. (2017) applied a Seq2Seq model for text simplification. Zhang and Lapata (2017) used reinforcement learning to optimize a reward based on simplicity, fluency and relevance, building on a Seq2Seq model. Recent methods build on transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) models, by integrating external databases containing simplification rules (Zhao et al., 2018), using an additional loss function to generate diverse outputs (Kriz et al., 2019), combining syntactic rules (Maddela et al., 2021), and conditioning on length and syntactic and lexical complexity features (Martin et al., 2020a).

Unsupervised Generative Methods rely on non-aligned complex and simple corpora instead of aligned complex-simple sentence pairs. Zhao et al. (2020) adopted a back-translation framework, whereas Surya et al. (2019) used an unsupervised style transfer paradigm. Martin et al. (2020b) used a pre-trained BART model fine-tuned on a paraphrased sentence pairs.

Controllable Generative Methods produce outputs at specified grade levels (Scarton and Specia, 2018;Nishihara et al., 2019), or apply syntactic or lexical constraints on the generated sentences (Martin et al., 2020a,b). However, these models do not provide any insights into how the simplification process is done and thus lack interpretability.

Supervised Revision-Based Methods use complex-simple sentence pairs to learn where to apply edit operations iteratively. Alva-Manchego et al. (2017) proposed a model using keep, replace, and delete operations. Some recent work used iterative non-autoregressive models to edit sentences by either predicting token-level editoperations (Omelianchuk et al., 2021) or using a fixed pipeline of edit operations (Agrawal et al., 2021). Dong et al. (2019) proposed a hybrid method with explicit edit operations in an endto-end generative model. Though these methods are more interpretable and controllable than generative methods, they still require complex-simple sentence pairs for training.

Unsupervised Revision-Based Methods such as Narayan and Gardent (2016) may apply a pipeline of edit operations in a fixed order. Kumar et al. (2020) then presented a general unsupervised revision-based approach by modelling text simplification as an unsupervised search problem. While GRS also relies on a revision-based framework and an unsupervised search strategy, we integrate a generative paraphrasing model into the framework to leverage the strengths of both text generation and text revision approaches. A broad body of related work spans compression-focused simplification, lexical substitution resources, and evaluation protocols, including document-level simplification with discourse constraints (Xu et al., 2015;Sulem et al., 2018), paraphrase generation with neural pretraining (Lewis et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2020), controllable text generation via attribute conditioning (Keskar et al., 2019;He et al., 2020), interpretable edit-based frameworks (Alva-Manchego et al., 2017;Dong et al., 2019), and common benchmarks and metrics such as Newsela, WikiLarge, SARI, and BLEU (Xu et al., 2016;Zhang and Lapata, 2017;Krishna et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2016).

spans: Recent work on knowledge-grounded conversation also explores retrieval-augmented generation, controllable style, and multimodal grounding with a variety of architectures and datasets, including Memory Networks (Weston et al., 2015), Transformer-based retrievers and generators such as RAG and FiD (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021), large-scale social chitchat and knowledge benchmarks like Persona-Chat, Wizard of Wikipedia, and Topical-Chat (Zhang et al., 2018; Dinan et al., 2019; Gopalakrishnan et al., 2019), as well as visual-dialogue and vision-language corpora that pair images with dialogic exchanges (Das et al., 2017; Mostafazadeh et al., 2017; Shuster et al., 2018; Huber et al., 2018).
document:
On the vanilla encoder-decoder architecture (Shang et al., 2015;Vinyals and Le, 2015), various extensions have been made to model the structure of dialogue contexts (Serban et al., 2016(Serban et al., , 2017Zhang et al., 2019a); to improve diversity of responses (Li et al., 2015;Xing et al., 2017;Zhao et al., 2017;Tao et al., 2018); to control attributes of responses (Xu et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2018;See et al., 2019); and to bias responses to some specific personas (Li et al., 2016;Zhang et al., 2018). Recently, grounding dialogue generation by extra knowledge has seemed promising to bridge the gap between conversation with existing systems and conversation with humans, and the knowledge could be obtained from knowledge graphs (Zhou et al., 2018b;Moon et al., 2019;Tuan et al., 2019), retrieved from unstructured documents (Dinan et al., 2019;Lian et al., 2019;Zhao et al., 2020a;Kim et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020) or visual background (Mostafazadeh et al., 2017;Shuster et al., 2018;Huber et al., 2018). In this work, we study document-grounded dialogue generation. Rather than selecting knowledge relevant to dialogue context and directly exploiting pre-trained language models to generate the response, we focus on expressing knowledge in this task.

The idea of sequence modeling via segmentation has attracted widespread attention in several natural language processing tasks. In text segmentation task, propose a probabilistic model for sequence modeling via their segmentation and a "Sleep-WAke Network"(SWAN) method. In machine translation, propose a neural phrase-based machine translation system that models phrase structures in the target language using SWAN. In data-to-text generation, Wiseman et al. (2018) develop a neural template-like generation model with an HSMM decoder, which is learned tractably by backpropagating through a dynamic program; to tackle the problem of weak Markov assumption for the segment transition probability, Shen et al. (2020) propose to explicitly segment target text into fragments and align them with their data correspondences, and jointly learn the segmentation and correspondence via dynamic programming. Recent work on knowledge-grounded conversation also explores retrieval-augmented generation, controllable style, and multimodal grounding with a variety of architectures and datasets, including Memory Networks (Weston et al., 2015), Transformer-based retrievers and generators such as RAG and FiD (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021), large-scale social chitchat and knowledge benchmarks like Persona-Chat, Wizard of Wikipedia, and Topical-Chat (Zhang et al., 2018; Dinan et al., 2019; Gopalakrishnan et al., 2019), as well as visual-dialogue and vision-language corpora that pair images with dialogic exchanges (Das et al., 2017; Mostafazadeh et al., 2017; Shuster et al., 2018; Huber et al., 2018). Building on these trends, our focus on expressing knowledge centers on how to structure document evidence into segments that can be planned and realized coherently. We hypothesize that segmentation-aware decoders can mitigate hallucination by constraining realization to evidence-linked spans. We also aim to disentangle retrieval from expression so that improvements in segment planning translate into controllable factuality.

Concretely, we adopt a two-stage perspective in which segment planning precedes lexical realization. The planner operates over document spans, identifies salient units, and sequences them into a rhetorical scaffold. The generator then conditions on this scaffold to produce fluent responses that remain grounded in the selected evidence. We evaluate this approach under varying retrieval noise to assess robustness. Our experiments compare segmentation-aware models against strong pretrained encoder-decoder baselines with attention over documents. We further analyze the effect of segment granularity and transition modeling on coherence and factual support. Finally, we discuss limitations in long-document settings and opportunities for adaptive segment budgets.

spans: Prior work has examined toxicity detection across multiple granularities and modeling paradigms, including character- and subword-level classifiers for robustness to obfuscation (Zhang et al., 2018;Mishra et al., 2018), context-sensitive sequence models for conversational toxicity (Pavlopoulos et al., 2020;Yin et al., 2019), lexicon-augmented neural architectures leveraging hate speech inventories (Waseem and Hovy, 2016;Fortuna and Nunes, 2018), attention-based rationales for interpretability (Lei et al., 2016;DeYoung et al., 2020), span-level supervision via weak labels and distant supervision (Mathew et al., 2021;Kennedy et al., 2020), cross-domain and cross-platform transfer methods (Wulczyn et al., 2017;Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020), adversarial data augmentation and debiasing protocols (Dixon et al., 2018;Hosseini et al., 2017;Sap et al., 2019), and multilingual toxicity resources that extend beyond English (Founta et al., 2018;Risch et al., 2021;Vidgen et al., 2021;Alvarez-Carmona et al., 2018).
document:
Toxicity detection systems (Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017;Pavlopoulos et al., 2017c;Zampieri et al., 2019) are typically trained on datasets annotated at the post level (a text is annotated as toxic or not) (Wulczyn et al., 2017;Borkan et al., 2019). Our work differs from general toxicity detection in that we detect toxic spans, instead of assigning toxicity labels to entire texts. Toxic spans detection can be seen as a case of attribution or rationale extraction (Li et al., 2016;Ribeiro et al., 2016), but specifically for toxic posts, a task that has never been the challenge to preserve anonymity. The full dataset and the code of this work will be released with a CC0 licence.

Prior work has examined toxicity detection across multiple granularities and modeling paradigms, including character- and subword-level classifiers for robustness to obfuscation (Zhang et al., 2018;Mishra et al., 2018), context-sensitive sequence models for conversational toxicity (Pavlopoulos et al., 2020;Yin et al., 2019), lexicon-augmented neural architectures leveraging hate speech inventories (Waseem and Hovy, 2016;Fortuna and Nunes, 2018), attention-based rationales for interpretability (Lei et al., 2016;DeYoung et al., 2020), span-level supervision via weak labels and distant supervision (Mathew et al., 2021;Kennedy et al., 2020), cross-domain and cross-platform transfer methods (Wulczyn et al., 2017;Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020), adversarial data augmentation and debiasing protocols (Dixon et al., 2018;Hosseini et al., 2017;Sap et al., 2019), and multilingual toxicity resources that extend beyond English (Founta et al., 2018;Risch et al., 2021;Vidgen et al., 2021;Alvarez-Carmona et al., 2018). In contrast to post-level labels, span annotations provide localized supervision that can improve interpretability and downstream moderation workflows. However, span-level datasets remain relatively scarce and often exhibit annotator disagreement due to the subjectivity of offensive language. Recent work on rationales has highlighted the importance of token-level explanations, but most resources do not target overtly abusive content at the granularity needed for actionable interventions. Building on these observations, we focus on explicit toxic spans within user-generated text and evaluate extraction quality with character-offset gold references. We further consider the impact of annotation design on model behavior, emphasizing clarity in guidelines and transparency in label provenance. Our experimental setup includes strong baselines and ablations to isolate the effects of modeling choices on span detection.

We contribute a dataset with fine-grained character offsets aligned to offensive substrings in social media posts. We detail an annotation pipeline that balances coverage, quality control, and annotator safety. We investigate modeling approaches that jointly score tokens and aggregate them into contiguous spans for evaluation. We also report robustness analyses under common obfuscation strategies observed in online platforms. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations and release protocols that minimize potential misuse while enabling reproducible research.

You are alive because your weak blood is supported by welfare and food stamps. Please don't reference Darwin in your icon. Loser.

spans: Lifelong learning is also a hot topic for PLMs. Some target at domain adaptation through continual pre-training (Gururangan et al., 2020), parameter-efficient adapters (He et al., 2021) and sparse expert models (Gururangan et al., 2021). Others focus on the incremental acquisition of factual knowledge that changes over time (Dhingra et al., 2021;Jang et al., 2021). However, the existing works seldom consider our lifelong learning setting where streaming data from multiple sources is sequentially gathered. A concurrent work  conducts empirical studies on conventional continual learning algorithms for PLM adaptation. However, they do not focus on PLM's training efficiency, which is different from our setting.

Many attempts have been made towards improving the efficiency of pre-training, such as designing novel pretraining tasks (Clark et al., 2020), model architectures (Zhang and He, 2020), optimization algorithms (You et al., 2020) and parallel architectures (Shoeybi et al., 2019;Shazeer et al., 2018). Until recently, researchers propose to back-distill or reuse capacity with methods such as knowledge distillation for pretraining acceleration (Qin et al., 2021), progressive resizing and parameter recycling (Gong et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2021), mixture-of-experts scaling (Fedus et al., 2021;Lepikhin et al., 2020), data selection and curriculum scheduling (Raffel et al., 2020;Xie et al., 2020), and compute-aware training protocols (Clark et al., 2022;Li et al., 2023). These studies explore efficiency across objectives, architectures, schedules, and distributed systems without situating their findings in relation to streaming multi-source lifelong pre-training or clarifying the unresolved limitations that motivate the present work.
document:
Lifelong Learning for PLMs. Lifelong learning aims at incrementally acquiring new knowledge, and in the meantime, mitigating the catastrophic forgetting issue. Numerous efforts have been spent towards this goal, including (1) memory-based methods (Rebuffi et al., 2017;Rolnick et al., 2019), which perform experience replay with authentic data (de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019), automatically generated data (Sun et al., 2020), or previously computed gradients (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017) conserved in the memory, (2) consolidationbased methods (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017;Aljundi et al., 2018), which introduce additional regularization terms to consolidate the model parameters that are important to previous tasks, and (3) dynamic architecture methods (Rusu et al., 2016;Yoon et al., 2018), which fix trained network architectures in old tasks and dynamically grow branches for new tasks. Lifelong learning is also a hot topic for PLMs. Some target at domain adaptation through continual pre-training (Gururangan et al., 2020), parameter-efficient adapters (He et al., 2021) and sparse expert models (Gururangan et al., 2021). Others focus on the incremental acquisition of factual knowledge that changes over time (Dhingra et al., 2021;Jang et al., 2021). However, the existing works seldom consider our lifelong learning setting where streaming data from multiple sources is sequentially gathered. A concurrent work  conducts empirical studies on conventional continual learning algorithms for PLM adaptation. However, they do not focus on PLM's training efficiency, which is different from our setting. More detailed comparisons are left in appendix F.

Efficient Pre-training in NLP. Many attempts have been made towards improving the efficiency of pre-training, such as designing novel pretraining tasks (Clark et al., 2020), model architectures (Zhang and He, 2020), optimization algorithms (You et al., 2020) and parallel architectures (Shoeybi et al., 2019;Shazeer et al., 2018). Until recently, researchers propose to back-distill or reuse capacity with methods such as knowledge distillation for pretraining acceleration (Qin et al., 2021), progressive resizing and parameter recycling (Gong et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2021), mixture-of-experts scaling (Fedus et al., 2021;Lepikhin et al., 2020), data selection and curriculum scheduling (Raffel et al., 2020;Xie et al., 2020), and compute-aware training protocols (Clark et al., 2022;Li et al., 2023). These studies explore efficiency across objectives, architectures, schedules, and distributed systems without situating their findings in relation to streaming multi-source lifelong pre-training or clarifying the unresolved limitations that motivate the present work. However, these methods typically focus on training PLMs on one static corpus, and thus cannot be directly applied to our lifelong pre-training setting.
