In this section, we discuss what privacy in ML means ( §2.1), followed by overview of private training ( §2.2) and privacy in language models ( §2.3).
We begin by clarifying the threat models, including honest-but-curious servers, malicious users, and external adversaries with black-box or white-box access.
We focus on data confidentiality during training and inference, as well as resistance to membership and attribute inference attacks.
Differential privacy (DP) offers a rigorous stability guarantee that limits the influence of any single record on the algorithm’s output.
Under the standard definition, neighboring datasets differ in one record, and the privacy loss is bounded by parameters ε and δ.
We also consider alternative formalizations such as concentrated DP and Rényi DP that support tighter composition and subsampling analysis.
Section §2.2 surveys private training for centralized and federated regimes with an emphasis on practical accounting.
We distinguish between gradient perturbation, objective perturbation, and output perturbation, and note implementation nuances around clipping, noise calibration, and sampling.
In centralized training, DP-SGD clips per-example gradients to a norm bound and adds calibrated Gaussian noise before parameter updates.
Its utility depends critically on the clipping distribution, batch size, sampling rate, and the privacy accountant used for composition over steps.
Federated settings introduce client heterogeneity, partial participation, and secure aggregation to reduce server visibility of local updates.
A key engineering challenge is synchronizing clipping and noise across clients while maintaining straggler tolerance and fairness.
We further discuss privacy accounting, contrasting moments/Rényi methods with privacy profiles and f-DP calibrations for tighter end-to-end budgets.
Calibration must align the sampling mechanism, data cadence, and training horizon to avoid budget underestimation.
Recent advances span multiple strands: differential privacy for learning and release (Dwork et al., 2006; Dwork et al., 2014), privacy amplification by subsampling and shuffling (Balle et al., 2018; Erlingsson et al., 2019), private stochastic gradient methods (Abadi et al., 2016; Bassily et al., 2014), Rényi and f-DP accounting (Mironov, 2017; Dong et al., 2022), private aggregation for federated analytics (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Goryczka and Xiong, 2011), privacy tests for synthetic data (Stadler et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2020), privacy-preserving prompt tuning and adapters (Dinan et al., 2022; Li and Liang, 2021), and audits of memorization in large language models (Carlini et al., 2019; Carlini et al., 2023).
Section §2.3 turns to language models, where privacy risks emerge from memorization of rare or unique sequences.
Empirical work shows that next-token prediction objectives can overfit to outliers, enabling extraction via adaptive prompting and beam search.
Mitigations include DP training, deduplication, filtering of PII, and response-time defenses such as refusal policies and output perturbation.
However, these mitigations interact with utility, safety, and calibration in nontrivial ways, especially for instruction-tuned and RLHF models.
We therefore structure our overview around risk surfaces: pretraining corpora, fine-tuning data, and deployment-time interactions.
For pretraining, data curation and deduplication reduce exposure, while DP-SGD offers formal guarantees at potentially high compute cost.
For fine-tuning, smaller datasets exacerbate privacy leakage unless clipping and noise are adapted to the narrow distribution shift.
At deployment, model audits, canary analysis, and red-team extraction testing help quantify residual risk under black-box constraints.
Throughout, we emphasize reproducible accounting, transparent reporting of ε, δ, and sampling assumptions, and sensitivity to downstream evaluation metrics.
We close by outlining open problems in aligning privacy budgets to longitudinal data reuse and continual learning without compounding loss.

spans: Early approaches mainly utilized hand-crafted prompts to adapt to different generation tasks (Brown et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2020;Zou et al., 2021). However, manually designed prompts are not flexible and cannot be applied to more kinds of tasks. Thus, recent works have focused on automating the learning of discrete prompts (Shin et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2020). However, learning prompts over discrete space is hard to optimize and likely to be sub-optimal. To address these problems, many works proposed to optimize continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021c;Li and Liang, 2021), which are more flexible to any kinds of tasks.

Beyond single-task setups, researchers have explored cross-domain and cross-format transfer signals in prompt-based and non-prompt paradigms, including multi-task pretraining (Sanh et al., 2022;Aghajanyan et al., 2021), adapter-based transfer (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2021), meta-learning for few-shot generalization (Finn et al., 2017;Bansal et al., 2020), cross-lingual prompting and translation-based transfer (Winata et al., 2021;Ponti et al., 2020), curriculum or task-routing schedulers (Rosenfeld et al., 2020;Tay et al., 2023), and instruction tuning corpora and methods (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022;Ouyang et al., 2022;Longpre et al., 2023). In prompt-centric literature, transfer has been examined via prompt ensembling (Zhou et al., 2022), prompt composition (Schick and Schütze, 2021), prompt calibration (Zhao et al., 2021), and prompt parameter sharing across tasks (Lester et al., 2021;He et al., 2022), with extensions to controllable generation and safety constraints (Keskar et al., 2019;Askell et al., 2021). Yet related datasets for generation and understanding tasks span SuperGLUE, XSum, CNN/DailyMail, CoNLL03, TyDiQA, and XNLI, among others, and evaluation has leveraged standard automatic metrics and human judgments.

Prompt-based Language Models. Prompt-based learning is a way of leveraging PLMs by prepending task-specific instructions to the task input when feeding into PLMs. Early approaches mainly utilized hand-crafted prompts to adapt to different generation tasks (Brown et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2020;Zou et al., 2021). However, manually designed prompts are not flexible and cannot be applied to more kinds of tasks. Thus, recent works have focused on automating the learning of discrete prompts (Shin et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2020). However, learning prompts over discrete space is hard to optimize and likely to be sub-optimal. To address these problems, many works proposed to optimize continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021c;Li and Liang, 2021), which are more flexible to any kinds of tasks. Among these studies, prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) prepended a sequence of vectors to the input for text generation tasks. By contrast, we utilize soft prompts to investigate transfer learning for text generation and demonstrate that generation tasks can often help each other via prompt transfer.

Transferability of Natural Language Processing. We are also closely related to existing works on transferability of NLP tasks (Jeong et al., 2020;Wiese et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019). Prior studies have shown that cross-task transfer can address the data scarcity issue (Wiese et al., 2017), enhance the ability to complex reasoning and inference (Jeong et al., 2020), or learn effective word representations (Liu et al., 2019). Efforts to transfer prompts for addressing NLU tasks have also been developed (Vu et al., 2021;Su et al., 2021). Beyond single-task setups, researchers have explored cross-domain and cross-format transfer signals in prompt-based and non-prompt paradigms, including multi-task pretraining (Sanh et al., 2022;Aghajanyan et al., 2021), adapter-based transfer (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2021), meta-learning for few-shot generalization (Finn et al., 2017;Bansal et al., 2020), cross-lingual prompting and translation-based transfer (Winata et al., 2021;Ponti et al., 2020), curriculum or task-routing schedulers (Rosenfeld et al., 2020;Tay et al., 2023), and instruction tuning corpora and methods (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022;Ouyang et al., 2022;Longpre et al., 2023). In prompt-centric literature, transfer has been examined via prompt ensembling (Zhou et al., 2022), prompt composition (Schick and Schütze, 2021), prompt calibration (Zhao et al., 2021), and prompt parameter sharing across tasks (Lester et al., 2021;He et al., 2022), with extensions to controllable generation and safety constraints (Keskar et al., 2019;Askell et al., 2021). Yet related datasets for generation and understanding tasks span SuperGLUE, XSum, CNN/DailyMail, CoNLL03, TyDiQA, and XNLI, among others, and evaluation has leveraged standard automatic metrics and human judgments. As an representative work, Vu et al. (2021) used the learned prompt to directly initialize the prompt for a target task while not considered the specific input. Our work focuses on the challenging text generation tasks by utilizing prompts to extract implicit taskrelated knowledge and considering specific model inputs for the most useful knowledge transfer.

spans: Recent neural graph parsers leverage pretrained encoders, structured inference, and learning-to-search variants in diverse ways across formalisms and languages, including higher-order CRF-style models and global decoders for semantic graphs (Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Dozat and Manning, 2018; He and Choi, 2020; Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020; Kurita and Søgaard, 2019; Oepen et al., 2015; Dozat and Manning, 2017).
document:
Semantic dependency parsing is the task of producing a dependency graph for a sentence, each dependency generally representing a predicate-argument relation. If one views each dependency as a decision to make, both plain dependency parsing (DP) and semantic dependency parsing (SDP) are known to exhibit high interdependence of decisions. For instance in DP, when parsing a question answering machine, choosing machine as root is linguistically coherent with the machine → answering → question analysis only, whereas (wrongly) choosing question as root is syntactically coherent with the question → answering → machine analysis.

Yet, in DP the interdependence between arc decision is partially solved by the tree constraint: choosing one head for a given token amounts to ruling out all other heads. This structural interdependence is absent in SDP. Complex structural, lexical and semantic factors control whether a given dependent should be attached to zero, one or several heads.

Several approaches exist in the literature to capture interdependence of arcs in SDP, which often derive from proposals made for DP. One is to use a higher-order graph-based parser. Wang et al. (2019) achieve state-of-the-art results (without pretrained LM) by using second-order factors to score the graphs, yet at the cost of a O(n 3 ) complexity. Recent neural graph parsers leverage pretrained encoders, structured inference, and learning-to-search variants in diverse ways across formalisms and languages, including higher-order CRF-style models and global decoders for semantic graphs (Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Dozat and Manning, 2018; He and Choi, 2020; Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020; Kurita and Søgaard, 2019; Oepen et al., 2015; Dozat and Manning, 2017).

Another main approach is to use sequential decisions, and hence take advantage of previous decisions by encoding the partial output structure. This is the case in the transition-based parser of Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez (2020), or in the system of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), which iteratively selects heads for all words, using reinforcement learning to order the selection of heads. Both models have a O(n 2 ) complexity (when used without cycle detection), and in both cases, sequential decisions benefit from previous decision encoding, yet at the cost of error propagation. For that reason, Bernard (2021) propose a system close to that of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), yet allowing the system to overwrite previous decisions and hopefully correct itself.

On the contrary, the biaffine system of Dozat and Manning (2018) performs a simultaneous scoring of all candidate arcs, and the decision for each candidate arc is made independently of the other ones. This results in a highly parallelizable O(n 2 ) inference, with surprisingly high performance albeit below second-order parsing.

As for most NLP tasks, SDP performance increases when integrating transformer-based contextual representations when encoding input tokens. On the English dataset from the SemEval 2015 Task 18 (Oepen et al., 2015), (Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020) report a +0.7 and +2.0 increase for ID and OOD respectively. Using the biaffine architecture of (Dozat and Manning, 2018), He and Choi (2020) report a +2 and +3 point increase in ID and OOD.

In this work, we retain the simple O(n 2 ) biaffine architecture of Dozat and Manning (2018) (hereafter DM18), and we investigate how simple auxiliary tasks can introduce some interdependence between arc decisions, in a multi-task learning setting (Caruana, 1997). We show modest but systematic improvements on the widely used Semeval2015-task18 English data (Oepen et al., 2015). We also test another appealing property of the biaffine architecture, which is the absence of formal constraints on the output graphs. Experiments on French deep syntactic graphs , which are highly cyclic, also demonstrate the effectiveness of our auxiliary tasks for SDP.

While much of this paper discusses the state of the NLP literature, a few related works that warrant emphasis as starting points for further reading:

Bender and Koller (2020), Raji et al. (2021) discuss the role of hype in driving bad outcomes from the development of language technology. Jin et al. (2021) and Rogers (2021) offer broader discussion of how to ensure that the net impact of near-future NLP deployments on the world is positive. Morris et al. (2020) and Hauser et al. (2021) highlight overly strong negative claims in papers analyzing models' robustness to synonym substitution. Looking to the longer term, Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) provides an introduction to the AI risk and AI alignment literature from a perspective that emphasizes NLP and language. Welty et al. (2019), Linzen ( 2020  2021), among many others, discuss the challenges involved in designing evaluations that yield trustworthy and accurate depictions of the capabilities of ML models.

The breadth of these contributions underscores a shared concern with establishing reliable, well-calibrated knowledge about model behavior. The debate around hype and overclaiming is not purely rhetorical; it shapes incentives for data curation, reporting standards, and the interpretation of benchmark results. Evaluation methodology, in particular, has emerged as a pivot point linking safety, robustness, and deployment readiness. Prior work on adversarial and contrastive testing shows that small perturbations can expose brittle shortcuts, calling for metrics that go beyond average-case accuracy. At the same time, alignment-oriented analyses caution that naively scaling models does not resolve specification gaps and can entrench opaque failure modes. Recent surveys and position papers chart complementary territory across model evaluation, alignment, robustness, and societal impact without integrating these strands into a unifying perspective, including Bommasani et al. (2021), Bender and Koller (2020), Raji et al. (2021), Jin et al. (2021), Rogers (2021), Morris et al. (2020), Hauser et al. (2021), and Welty et al. (2019). These observations motivate a closer look at how claims are operationalized within experimental designs.

In what follows, we organize the discussion around three recurring sources of ambiguity in current practice. First, construct validity is often assumed rather than demonstrated, particularly when tasks are operationalized via convenience datasets. Second, the statistical framing of results tends to omit uncertainty quantification beyond standard error bars, blurring the line between practical and theoretical significance. Third, cross-dataset generalization is frequently asserted through point estimates, despite distributional shifts that demand targeted diagnostics. We draw on recent methodological guidance to propose reporting patterns that make hidden assumptions explicit. We also highlight opportunities where small changes to experimental protocols can yield outsized gains in interpretability. Finally, we delimit the scope of our review to supervised NLP settings with text-only inputs, noting that multimodal and interactive systems raise additional challenges outside our present focus.

spans: Recent advancements introduce doubly robust estimators, targeted maximum likelihood estimation, outcome regression with machine learning, and balancing approaches such as entropy balancing and kernel mean matching (Bang and Robins, 2005; van der Laan and Rubin, 2006; Athey et al., 2018; Hainmueller, 2012; Gretton et al., 2009), as well as modern causal forests, Bayesian propensity score models, deep representation learning for deconfounding, and semiparametric efficiency frameworks (Wager and Athey, 2018; McCandless et al., 2009; Johansson et al., 2016; Robins et al., 1994).
document:
The ATE is defined as τ = E[Y i (1)−Y i (0)], where Y i (t) is the potential outcome for unit i under treatment t. It can be identified in randomized experiments (Rubin, 1974). However, the situation is more complicated in the observational setting, where the treatment is not randomized to units but could be correlated with confounders, X i , that are associated with the treatment and the outcome.

In that setting, we can, with additional assumptions, still recover the ATE using Inverse Propensity Weighting (IPW) or related robust methods (Funk et al., 2011), where observations are weighted by the inverse of their estimated treatment probabilities π(X i ) = Pr(T i = 1|X i ) (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983). Recent advancements introduce doubly robust estimators, targeted maximum likelihood estimation, outcome regression with machine learning, and balancing approaches such as entropy balancing and kernel mean matching (Bang and Robins, 2005; van der Laan and Rubin, 2006; Athey et al., 2018; Hainmueller, 2012; Gretton et al., 2009), as well as modern causal forests, Bayesian propensity score models, deep representation learning for deconfounding, and semiparametric efficiency frameworks (Wager and Athey, 2018; McCandless et al., 2009; Johansson et al., 2016; Robins et al., 1994). Yet these techniques rely on correct model specification, overlap, and careful diagnostics to mitigate bias.

A complementary line of work emphasizes sensitivity analysis to departures from unconfoundedness (Rosenbaum, 2002; Imbens, 2003). Practical implementations often combine flexible nuisance estimation with cross-fitting to reduce overfitting and achieve asymptotic normality (Chernozhukov et al., 2018). Estimation quality further depends on design choices such as trimming rules, stabilized weights, and covariate balance checks (Crump et al., 2009; Cole and Hernán, 2008; Austin, 2011). In applied settings, researchers must report uncertainty, conduct placebo tests, and evaluate robustness across specifications.

Throughout, we adopt the potential outcomes framework and focus on binary treatments for clarity. We consider assumptions of consistency, positivity, and conditional exchangeability as the foundation for identification. Our discussion highlights common sources of finite-sample instability in IPW estimators. We also outline diagnostic tools that help assess propensity score model fit. Finally, we summarize implications for study design and transparent reporting in observational causal inference.

spans: Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context. They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications. A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis. Related evaluations of social bias in multilingual or cross-lingual settings appear in StereoSet (Nadeem et al., 2021), Multilingual LIWC-based analyses (Pennebaker et al., 2015), bias measures for contextualized embeddings (May et al., 2019; Kurita et al., 2019), and transfer-oriented debiasing approaches leveraging XLM-R and mBERT (Conneau et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; Lauscher et al., 2020; Prost et al., 2019), alongside corpus-level resources that facilitate demographic attribute probing across languages (Bhardwaj et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2018; Swinger et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020).
document:
Few studies have addressed bias in language models in French. Irvine et al. (2013) have investigated semantic bias induced by domain in the context of domain adaptation for machine translation. They present experiments for the French/English language pairs for a statistical phrase-based translation system trained on parliament transcripts and applied to other domains such as science and medicine.

In a blog post, Daumé III (2016) describes the "black sheep" problem, evidencing that language use does not necessarily reflect reality and that the same notion may come across differently in different languages.

Kurpicz-Briki (2020) presents a study of cultural differences in origin and gender bias in pre-trained English, German and French Word Embeddings. The author adapts the WEAT method (Caliskan et al., 2017) that contains material for measuring bias in English language word embeddings to (Swiss) French and German and shows that the bias identified differ between the three languages studied. This is probably the effort that is closest to the present study. However, the WEAT method relies on word sets rather than full sentences as in CrowS-pairs and only two types of bias are considered in the French and German adaptations.

More importantly, Goldfarb-Tarrant et al. ( 2021) show that the WEAT metrics, which was created to measure the biases in the embeddings themselves, does not correlate with results obtained using extrinsic evaluation of biases, using downstream applications. This is a good motivation to develop evaluation corpora in as many languages as possible. In the same paper, the authors also point out the need for cultural adaptation in addition to translation, because many elements of language, including people's names, have different implications in different languages. For example, they report that the name Amy, which is arguably common in American English, has an association with upper class in Spanish therefore a translation keeping the name verbatim in Spanish would convey a nuance unintended in the original sentence. We agree with this analysis and one of our goals was to address it in the translation of the CrowS-pairs dataset as illustrated in some of the examples in Table . Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context. They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications. A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis. Related evaluations of social bias in multilingual or cross-lingual settings appear in StereoSet (Nadeem et al., 2021), Multilingual LIWC-based analyses (Pennebaker et al., 2015), bias measures for contextualized embeddings (May et al., 2019; Kurita et al., 2019), and transfer-oriented debiasing approaches leveraging XLM-R and mBERT (Conneau et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; Lauscher et al., 2020; Prost et al., 2019), alongside corpus-level resources that facilitate demographic attribute probing across languages (Bhardwaj et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2018; Swinger et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020).

Blodgett et al. ( 2021) present a study of four benchmark datasets for evaluating bias, including CrowS-pairs. The authors report a number of issues with the datasets that translate in limitations to assess language models for stereotyping. Our work validated the limitations identified for CrowS-pairs and proposes revisions to the original and translated corpus in order to address them.

spans: Recent neural formulations explore contextual embeddings and graph structures for EFP and related semantics, including span-based models and graph neural networks for factuality and uncertainty (Lee et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2020; Rudinger et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022), dependency-based causal and modal reasoning frameworks (Kwon et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Pyatkin et al., 2021), prompt-tuned encoders for information extraction (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023), and instruction-following large language models for event semantics (Brown et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023).
document: Early works cast event factuality prediction (EFP) as a classification or regression problem and have employed rule-based (Nairn et al., 2006; Lotan et al., 2013) or machine learning approaches (Diab et al., 2009; Rudinger et al., 2018) using hand-engineered features and local lexical cues. Subsequent research incorporated compositional semantics to handle negation, modality, and source attribution, showing that clausal structure and scope profoundly affect factuality judgments. Datasets such as FactBank, MEANTIME, and UW have standardized evaluation and highlighted the importance of consistent annotation schemes across genres. Cross-lingual studies further revealed that morphosyntactic markers of evidentiality and modality vary, complicating transfer and necessitating careful typological consideration. Neural architectures leveraging contextual encoders improved robustness to lexical variation while still struggling with nested scope and long-distance dependencies. Recent neural formulations explore contextual embeddings and graph structures for EFP and related semantics, including span-based models and graph neural networks for factuality and uncertainty (Lee et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2020; Rudinger et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022), dependency-based causal and modal reasoning frameworks (Kwon et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Pyatkin et al., 2021), prompt-tuned encoders for information extraction (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023), and instruction-following large language models for event semantics (Brown et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023). These advances coincide with a shift from purely sentence-level modeling to discourse-aware frameworks that propagate beliefs across mentions and documents. Prior document-level EFP systems often rely on pipeline architectures that separately detect events, sources, and cues before attributing factuality. While pipelines can modularize the task, they incur cascading errors and require extensive feature engineering or auxiliary components. Joint models mitigate error propagation but can be brittle when supervision is sparse or annotation schemas diverge. Probabilistic formulations that encode scope constraints offer principled inference yet tend to be computationally intensive for large corpora. Our work is most closely related to that of Yao et al. (2021), which casts EFP as modal dependency parsing. However, they first extract events and conceivers and then build the MDS by ranking the candidate parents for each event. In contrast, we perform modal dependency parsing by constructing a prompt with the event to predict its conceiver parent, simplifying the pipeline. We further reduce reliance on task-specific labels through in-context formulations that allow the model to condition on exemplars rather than fixed classifiers. This approach aims to couple source attribution with factuality inference via a unified decoding problem. To evaluate, we consider both sentence- and document-level settings to assess generalization under distribution shift. We adopt standard metrics for factuality and parent assignment, ensuring comparability with prior reports. Our training objective balances correctness of parent selection with calibration of factuality scores. We also analyze error types associated with cue detection, implicit sources, and conflicting attributions across a document. Our ablations test sensitivity to prompt format, context window size, and negative sampling of distractor parents. We evaluate data efficiency by progressively subsampling supervision and measuring degradation in attribution accuracy. To facilitate reproducibility, we release code, prompts, and processed datasets aligned with prior benchmarks. Finally, we discuss implications for downstream event analytics where provenance-aware factuality supports trustworthy summarization and forecasting. Our prompt-based approach also bears resemblance to works applying prompt-based learning to other NLP tasks, such as event extraction (Liu et al., 2020; Fincke et al., 2021), relation extraction, named entity recognition, and coreference resolution. We conclude by outlining open challenges in modeling nested modality and capturing multi-source disagreement under limited supervision.

spans: Prior work spans multiple input modalities and training paradigms, including knowledge-grounded dialogue systems that use external documents and memory networks for knowledge selection (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018b), graph-based grounding with knowledge graphs and relational reasoning (Zhou et al., 2018a; Moon et al., 2019; Tuan et al., 2019), visual dialog with image-grounded context encoders (Das et al., 2017; Niu et al., 2019), reinforcement learning for policy-aware knowledge picking (Zhao et al., 2020b; Chen et al., 2020), and sequential latent-variable models and variational selectors designed to capture discourse dynamics across turns (Kim et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021).
document:
There is a substantial literature in the field of Knowledge-grounded conversation. With the grounding of external knowledge in format of knowledge graph (Zhou et al., 2018a;, document (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018;Zhou et al., 2018b) or visual background (Das et al., 2017), it is regarded as a critical method towards intelligent dialogue system. Nowadays, existing methods in KGC often share a paradigm that decomposes the task into two related sub-problems, namely knowledge selection and utterance generation (Kim et al., 2020). In this work, we mainly focus on the knowledge selection task. To this end, a great deal of methods have been proposed to retrieve the most relevant knowledge by memory network (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018), sequential latent variables (Kim et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020), reinforcement learning (Zhao et al., 2020b) and so on. However, no methods shed light on the personalization issue of knowledge selection.

Prior work spans multiple input modalities and training paradigms, including knowledge-grounded dialogue systems that use external documents and memory networks for knowledge selection (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018b), graph-based grounding with knowledge graphs and relational reasoning (Zhou et al., 2018a; Moon et al., 2019; Tuan et al., 2019), visual dialog with image-grounded context encoders (Das et al., 2017; Niu et al., 2019), reinforcement learning for policy-aware knowledge picking (Zhao et al., 2020b; Chen et al., 2020), and sequential latent-variable models and variational selectors designed to capture discourse dynamics across turns (Kim et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021).

Our work is related to dual learning as well. First proposed in neural machine translation by He et al. (2016), dual learning is a semi-supervision learning scheme aiming at utilizing large-scale unlabeled data. Together with its newly appeared variants in recent years , dual learning has been successfully applied in neural machine translation He et al., 2017), image-image-translation (Yi et al., 2017;Lin et al., 2018), sentiment analysis , automatic speech recognition (Ren et al., 2019), question answering (Tang et al., 2017), and knowledge-grounded dialogue (Meng et al., 2020). Our work is related to dual learning as well. First proposed in neural machine translation by He et al. (2016), dual learning is a semi-supervision learning scheme aiming at utilizing the large scale unlabeled data. In this work, we apply dual learning to model the inter-dependency relationship between one's personal memory and his or her choice of knowledge.

spans: Interpretability research has also drawn on saliency and feature-attribution surveys and benchmarks across tasks and modalities, including gradient-based and perturbation-based explainers (Simonyan et al., 2014;Zeiler and Fergus, 2014;Kindermans et al., 2019), attention-as-explanation debates and evaluations (Jain and Wallace, 2019;Serrano and Smith, 2019;Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019), causal and counterfactual explainability frameworks (Pearl, 2009;Vig et al., 2020;Mothilal et al., 2020;Karimi et al., 2020), and standardized evaluation toolkits and datasets for explanations in NLP and vision (Yang and Kim, 2019;Hooker et al., 2019;DeYoung et al., 2020;Samek et al., 2017), alongside work on stability and faithfulness metrics (Adebayo et al., 2018;Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018;Camburu et al., 2020).
document:
Interpretability There has been a lot of work on better interpreting models' decision process, e.g., understanding BERT (Clark et al., 2019b;Kovaleva et al., 2019) and attention in transformers (Hao et al., 2020), or through text generation models (Narang et al., 2020). In this paper we utilize the attention scores as a generic way to understand what features a model relies on for making its predictions. Other common model interpretation techniques (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Ribeiro et al., 2016), or more recent work on hierarchical attentions (Chen et al., 2020) and contrastive explanations (Jacovi et al., 2021), can be used as well. In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers. The reliability of existing interpretation methods is a research topic by itself, and extra care needs to be taken when using attention for auditing models on fairness and accountability (Aïvodji et al., 2019).

Interpretability research has also drawn on saliency and feature-attribution surveys and benchmarks across tasks and modalities, including gradient-based and perturbation-based explainers (Simonyan et al., 2014;Zeiler and Fergus, 2014;Kindermans et al., 2019), attention-as-explanation debates and evaluations (Jain and Wallace, 2019;Serrano and Smith, 2019;Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019), causal and counterfactual explainability frameworks (Pearl, 2009;Vig et al., 2020;Mothilal et al., 2020;Karimi et al., 2020), and standardized evaluation toolkits and datasets for explanations in NLP and vision (Yang and Kim, 2019;Hooker et al., 2019;DeYoung et al., 2020;Samek et al., 2017), alongside work on stability and faithfulness metrics (Adebayo et al., 2018;Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018;Camburu et al., 2020).

Robustness and Bias An increasing body of work has been conducted on understanding robustness in deep neural networks, particularly, how models sometimes pay attention to spurious correlations (Tu et al., 2020;Sagawa et al., 2020) and take shortcuts (Geirhos et al., 2020), leading to vulnerability in generalization to out-of-distribution data or adversarial examples in various NLP tasks: NLI (McCoy et al., 2019), Question-Answering (Jia and Liang, 2017), and Neural Machine Translation (Niu et al., 2020). Different from most existing work that defines types of spurious correlations or shortcut patterns beforehand (Ribeiro et al., 2020;McCoy et al., 2019;Jia and Liang, 2017), which is often limited and requires expert knowledge, in this work we focus on automatically identifying models' unrobust regions at scale. Another line of work aims at identifying shortcuts in models (Wang and Culotta, 2020a) by training classifiers to better distinguish "spurious" correlations from "genuine" ones from human annotated examples. In contrast, we propose a cross-dataset approach and a knowledge-aware perturbation approach to automate this identification process with less human intervention in-between.

Mitigation Multiple approaches have been proposed to mitigate shortcut learning and data biases (Clark et al., 2020;Bras et al., 2020;Zhou and Bansal, 2020;Minderer et al., 2020) data augmentation (Jin et al., 2020;Alzantot et al., 2018), domain adaptation (Blitzer et al., 2006(Blitzer et al., , 2007, and multi-task learning (Tu et al., 2020). Du et al. (2021) proposes to mitigate shortcuts by suppressing model's prediction on examples with large shortcut degree. Recent study has also shown removing spurious correlations can sometimes hurt model's accuracy (Khani and Liang, 2021). Orthogonal to existing works, we propose to first identify unrobust correlations in an NLP model and then propose a targeted mitigation to encourage the model to rely less on those unrobust correlations.

spans: Prior work has explored dataset construction, modeling strategies, and contextual features for toxic language and adjacent phenomena without a unifying perspective on their interplay or implications for counter speech, including keyword-based collection and annotation protocols (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Nobata et al., 2016; Gao and Huang, 2017), platform-specific corpora spanning Twitter, Reddit, Gab, and news comments (Qian et al., 2019; Mathew et al., 2021; Qian et al., 2019; Gao and Huang, 2017), context-aware annotation and modeling for conversational threads (Pavlopoulos et al., 2020; Vidgen et al., 2021; Menini et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2016), and targeted studies of counter speech across languages and events (Mathew et al., 2019; Ziems et al., 2020; Garland et al., 2020; Chung et al., 2019).
document:
Hate speech in user-generated content has been an active research area recently (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). Researchers have built several datasets for hate speech detection from diverse sources like Twitter (Waseem and Hovy, 2016;Davidson et al., 2017), Yahoo! (Nobata et al., 2016), Fox News (Gao and Huang, 2017), Gab (Mathew et al., 2021) and Reddit (Qian et al., 2019).

Compared to hate speech detection, few studies focus on detecting counter speech (Mathew et al., 2019;Ziems et al., 2020;Garland et al., 2020). Mathew et al. (2019) collect and handcode 6,898 counter hate comments from YouTube videos targeting Jews, Blacks and LGBT communities. Ziems et al. (2020) use a collection of hate and counter hate keywords relevant to COVID-19 and create a dataset containing 359 counter hate tweets targeting Asians. Garland et al. (2020) work with German tweets and define hate and counter speech based on the communities to which the authors belong. Another line of research focuses on curating datasets for counter speech generation using crowdsourcing (Qian et al., 2019) or with the help of trained operators (Chung et al., 2019;Fanton et al., 2021). However, synthetic language is rarely as rich as language in the wild. Even if it were, conclusions and models from synthetic data may not transfer to the real world. In this paper, we work with user-generated content expressing hate and counter-hate rather than synthetic content.

Table  summarizes existing datasets for Hate and Counter-hate detection. Most of them do not include context information. In other words, the preceding comments are not provided when annotating Targets. Context does affect human judgments and has been taken into account for Hate detection (Gao and Huang, 2017;Vidgen et al., 2021;Pavlopoulos et al., 2020;Menini et al., 2021). Gao and Huang (2017) annotate hateful comments in the nested structures of 10 Fox News discussion threads. Vidgen et al. (2021) Prior work has explored dataset construction, modeling strategies, and contextual features for toxic language and adjacent phenomena without a unifying perspective on their interplay or implications for counter speech, including keyword-based collection and annotation protocols (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Nobata et al., 2016; Gao and Huang, 2017), platform-specific corpora spanning Twitter, Reddit, Gab, and news comments (Qian et al., 2019; Mathew et al., 2021; Qian et al., 2019; Gao and Huang, 2017), context-aware annotation and modeling for conversational threads (Pavlopoulos et al., 2020; Vidgen et al., 2021; Menini et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2016), and targeted studies of counter speech across languages and events (Mathew et al., 2019; Ziems et al., 2020; Garland et al., 2020; Chung et al., 2019). Utilizing conversational context has also been explored in text classification tasks such as sentiment analysis (Ren et al., 2016), stance (Zubiaga et al., 2018) and sarcasm (Ghosh et al., 2020). To our knowledge, we are the first to investigate the role of context in Hate and Counter-hate detection.

spans: A number of works examine pretraining and architectural advances in NER and related sequence labeling without a unified perspective, including multilingual transformers for cross-lingual transfer (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), span classification with boundary-sensitive inference (Eberts and Ulges, 2020; Yu et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Fu et al., 2021), gazetteer and lexicon integration for Chinese NER (Zhang and Yang, 2018; Ma et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a; Ding et al., 2020), and prompt- or query-based formulations that cast NER as QA or masked language modeling (Li et al., 2020b; Cui et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021).
document:
Named Entity Recognition The mainstream NER systems are designed to recognize flat entities and based on a sequence tagging framework. Collobert et al. (2011) introduced the linear-chain conditional random field (CRF) into neural networkbased sequence tagging models, which can explicitly encode the transition likelihoods between adjacent tags. Many researchers followed this work, and employed LSTM as the encoder. In addition, character-level representations are typically used for English tasks (Huang et al., 2015;Lample et al., 2016;Ma and Hovy, 2016;Chiu and Nichols, 2016), whereas lexicon information is helpful for Chinese NER (Zhang and Yang, 2018;Ma et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020a).

Nested NER allows a token to belong to multiple entities, which conflicts with the plain sequence tagging framework. Ju et al. (2018) proposed to use stacked LSTM-CRFs to predict from inner to outer entities. Straková et al. (2019) concatenated the BILOU tags for each token inside the nested entities, which allows the LSTM-CRF to work as for flat entities. Li et al. (2020b) reformulated nested NER as a machine reading comprehension task. Shen et al. (2021) proposed to recognize nested entities by the two-stage object detection method widely used in computer vision.

Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing.

In addition, pretrained language models, also known as contextualized embeddings, were also widely introduced to NER models, and significantly boosted the model performance (Peters et al., 2018;Devlin et al., 2019). They are used in our baseline by default.

Label Smoothing Szegedy et al. (2016) proposed the label smoothing as a regularization technique to improve the accuracy of the Inception networks on ImageNet. By explicitly assigning a small probability to non-ground-truth labels, label smoothing can prevent the models from becoming too confident about the predictions, and thus improve generalization. It turned out to be a useful alternative to the standard cross entropy loss, and has been widely adopted to fight against the over-confidence (Zoph et al., 2018;Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017;Vaswani et al., 2017), improve the model calibration (Müller et al., 2019), and denoise incorrect labels (Lukasik et al., 2020).

Our proposed boundary smoothing applies the smoothing technique to entity boundaries, rather than labels. This is driven by the observation that entity boundaries are more ambiguous and inconsistent to annotate in NER engineering. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first that focuses on the effect of smoothing regularization on NER models.

Robustness of MRC Models. The robustness of MRC models are usually evaluated against testtime perturbations and out-of-domain data. Research on test-time perturbation proposes perturbation methods at different levels as attacks (Si et al., 2021), such as word replacement with neighbors in the vector space (Rychalska et al., 2018;, question paraphrasing (Gan and Ng, 2019;Ribeiro et al., 2018), sentence distractor injection (Jia and Liang, 2017;Zhou et al., 2020). Another line of research (Fisch et al., 2019;Sen and Saffari, 2020) tests a model on data with out-of-domain passage or question distributions, usually from different datasets. Our work mainly falls into the category of test-time perturbation. We distinguish from previous work by focusing on the effect of entity replacement, with the motivation that entities can have flexible and diverse names in the real life.

Model Robustness to Entity Substitution. It is non-trivial for NLP models to be able to properly handle the large space of named entities. Previous works use entity substitution to audit or improve model robustness on different tasks like NER (Agarwal et al., 2020;Lin et al., 2021), Natural Language Inference (Mitra et al., 2020). Recent studies on robustness-enhancing strategies in MRC and related tasks span data augmentation with adversarial triggers and paraphrases (Jia and Liang, 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2018; Si et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021), calibration and uncertainty-aware decoding (Kadavath et al., 2022; Desai and Durrett, 2020; Minderer et al., 2021; Jagannatha et al., 2020), robust pretraining and domain generalization (Hendrycks et al., 2020; Arjovsky et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Sagawa et al., 2020), and entity-aware modeling or substitution protocols (Agarwal et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021; Zmigrod et al., 2019; Balasubramanian et al., 2020), but the literature has also explored counterfactual data augmentation and invariance testing across benchmarks (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020; Khashabi et al., 2020; Talmor et al., 2020). Despite these efforts, entity substitution in MRC poses distinct challenges because answer spans, coreference chains, and world knowledge interact, creating failure modes that differ from token-level paraphrases.

In particular, prior analyses have shown that span extractors can overfit to surface cues when entities are mutated, leading to semantic drift in attention patterns and spurious alignment between questions and passages. These observations suggest that robustness must be assessed not only at the lexical level but also in terms of entity-level consistency and role preservation within passages. We therefore focus our study on controlled entity replacement regimes that preserve grammaticality while perturbing the salience and ambiguity of named mentions. Concretely, we construct replacements that vary along type compatibility, popularity priors, and cross-document frequency to examine model sensitivity to entity prominence. We complement this with diagnostics that track calibration error, answer span stability, and evidence selection under targeted entity swaps. Our evaluation spans extractive and generative MRC models to capture architectural differences in sensitivity to entity perturbations. We additionally compare synthetic replacements with naturally occurring entity variants to gauge external validity. Finally, we release a reproducible benchmark and analysis toolkit to facilitate consistent measurement of entity-aware robustness in MRC. Our results motivate a reexamination of how MRC models internalize entity knowledge and suggest design principles for future architectures.

We organize the remainder of this paper as follows. Section 2 formalizes the entity replacement framework and describes our datasets. Section 3 details the models and training protocols. Section 4 presents robustness metrics and diagnostic probes. Section 5 reports empirical results and ablations. Section 6 discusses limitations and future work.

Introduction Textual EL has matured with robust benchmarks and methods, yet entity-centric applications increasingly involve images and text together in the wild. There is vast prior research on textual entity linking. Multiple datasets have been proposed over the years including the manuallyannotated high-quality datasets like AIDA (Hoffart et al., 2011), automatically-annotated large-scale datasets like CWEB (Guo and Barbosa, 2018) and zero-shot datasets like Zeshel (Logeswaran et al., 2019). To evaluate the EL models' performance, it is usual to train on the AIDA-train dataset, and test on the datasets of AIDA-test, MSNBC (Cucerzan, 2007), AQUAINT (Milne and Witten, 2008), DBpedia Spotlight (Daiber et al., 2013), TAC-KBP EL (Ji et al., 2010), and WNED (Guo and Barbosa, 2018). Recent neural models span local-collective graph approaches (Ganea and Hofmann, 2017), bi-encoder and cross-encoder retrievers (Gillick et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020), span-based disambiguation with pre-trained transformers (Yamada et al., 2020), and end-to-end EL pipelines over Wikipedia/Wikidata (Kolitsas et al., 2018; Broscheit, 2019). Additional resources include Wikipedia hyperlinks (Spitkovsky et al., 2012), Wikidata descriptions (Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014), and cross-lingual corpora such as Mewsli-9 (Botha et al., 2020). However, as mentioned in (Cao et al., 2021), many methods have achieved high and similar results within recent three years. One possible explanation is that it may simply be near the ceiling of what can be achieved for these datasets, and it is difficult to conduct further research based on them. This observation motivates a closer look at settings where textual context alone is insufficient for disambiguation.

Background and Motivation Multimodal EL aims to ground mentions in text to a knowledge base while leveraging complementary visual cues to reduce ambiguity. In recent years, the growing trend towards multimodality requires to extend the research of EL from monomodality to multimodality. Moon et al. (2018), Zhang et al. (2020), Chen et al. (2021), and Peng (2021) propose three MEL datasets, which are built from Weibo, Wikipedia, and Richpedia information and use CNDBpedia, Wikidata, and Richpedia as the corresponding KBs, alongside related benchmarks such as XMEL (Zhang et al., 2022), WikiDiverse-MEL (Liu et al., 2023), and WebQSP-Vis (Huang et al., 2022). Methods range from visual-semantic alignment via multimodal transformers (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019), cross-modal contrastive pretraining (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021), entity-aware visual grounding (Kim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021), and fusion-based disambiguation with region features (Anderson et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019). Datasets for related tasks include entity-centric image-caption corpora (Biten et al., 2019; Tran et al., 2020), multimodal NER (Lu et al., 2018; Arshad et al., 2021), and multimodal relation extraction (Zheng et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). However, using Wikipedia as the target dataset may lead to the data leakage problem as many language models are pretrained on it. Prior work has also noted that noisy social media images and informal text present new failure modes for canonical EL pipelines.

Problem Setting and Contributions We formulate multimodal entity linking as ranking KB candidates given a mention, its textual context, and associated images. We define a candidate generation step based on surface forms and cross-modal retrieval, followed by a re-ranking model that fuses text and vision. Our MEL dataset is also related to other named entity-related multimodal datasets, including entityaware image caption datasets (Biten et al., 2019;Tran et al., 2020;, multimodal NER datasets Lu et al., 2018), etc. However, the entities in these datasets are not linked to a unified KB. So our research of MEL can enhance the understanding of named entities, thereby enhancing the research in these areas. We present three contributions: a carefully curated and leakage-aware MEL benchmark, a strong fusion-based baseline with interpretable cross-modal signals, and an analysis of where vision most improves disambiguation. We further release standardized evaluation scripts to encourage reproducible comparison across future methods.

Related Work: Modeling Approaches Early multimodal EL used late fusion with separate text and image encoders and simple heuristics for entity priors. Recent works adopt unified transformers with cross-attention to align tokens and visual regions, enabling fine-grained grounding signals. Contrastive pretraining has proven useful for robust representations, while knowledge-aware adapters inject KB semantics without retraining full backbones. Our work builds on these insights by integrating candidate-aware prompts and visual-region gating into the re-ranker, tailored to mitigate leakage and ambiguity.

Related Work: Evaluation and Fairness Evaluation protocols for MEL vary widely in negative sampling, candidate set size, and use of in-KB versus NIL labels. Leakage-aware splits, rigorous ablations over vision availability, and cross-domain generalization remain under-specified in many benchmarks. We therefore articulate a transparent protocol, report per-entity-type breakdowns, and include analyses under missing or misleading images to provide a reliable basis for future research.

Open-Domain Response Generation Recent work of open-domain response generation gener-ally follows the work of Ritter et al. (2011) where the task is treated as a machine translation task, and many of them use a Seq2Seq structure (Sutskever et al., 2014) following previous work (Vinyals and Le, 2015;Shang et al., 2015;Sordoni et al., 2015). In recent years, substantial improvements have been made (Serban et al., 2017;Li et al., 2016;Wolf et al., 2019), and embeddings are used to control response generation on extra information such as persona (Li et al., 2016), profiles (Yang et al., 2017), coherence (Xu et al., 2018, emotions (Huang et al., 2018), and dialogue attributes like response-relatedness (See et al., 2019). However, there is a lack of work that uses embeddings to control response generation over multiple corpora. Our work follows the common models of opendomain conversational systems, while we study the problem of multiple corpora of different domains.

Open-domain response generation has traditionally contended with the trade-off between diversity and relevance. Early neural approaches prioritized fluency while often sacrificing factual grounding, particularly when trained on single-source conversational logs. Subsequent advances introduced auxiliary signals to steer content selection, but cross-corpus variability remains underexplored in terms of controllable conditioning. The challenge is further amplified when stylistic, topical, or persona cues are not consistently annotated across datasets.

Recent studies span diverse modeling and data angles for open-domain dialogue, including retrieval-augmented generation (Dinan et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020), large-scale pretraining for chit-chat and knowledge grounding (Zhang et al., 2019; Roller et al., 2021), conditional control via side constraints or control codes (Keskar et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2020), persona and empathy conditioning (Zhang et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2019), and benchmark datasets such as DailyDialog, Persona-Chat, ConvAI2, and Wizard of Wikipedia.

To address multi-corpus control, we consider settings in which heterogeneous datasets must be aligned through shared embedding interfaces. We emphasize scenarios where attribute coverage is imbalanced, such that one corpus may provide rich persona labels while another offers fine-grained topical metadata. A key obstacle is mitigating negative transfer when attribute priors conflict or diverge across domains. This motivates mechanisms for disentangling corpus-specific signals from global conversational factors.

We formalize the multi-corpus control objective as learning conditional distributions over responses given inputs and attribute embeddings unified across sources. This view treats corpora as partially observed attribute carriers, enabling missing-attribute inference at training or inference time. A practical implication is the need for robust regularization against spurious correlations that may dominate when one dataset is disproportionately large. We therefore consider representations that can be calibrated across corpora without relying on brittle manual mappings.

In operational deployments, aligning attribute semantics supports consistent user experiences across products that source conversations from different platforms. Multi-turn coherence, safety, and factuality constraints frequently vary by domain and collection protocol, complicating uniform control strategies. By casting control variables as corpus-agnostic keys in an embedding space, systems can decouple user-intent modeling from stylistic or knowledge-grounding choices. This separation is also conducive to faster adaptation as new datasets are added.

Our study focuses on three research questions. First, can shared control embeddings induce consistent responses across corpora with heterogeneous annotations. Second, how does attribute sparsity affect controllability and response quality in low-resource datasets. Third, what training curricula stabilize learning when corpora differ substantially in size, style, and noise. These questions guide the model variants and evaluation protocol.

We design experiments on mixtures of conversational corpora that exhibit complementary attribute coverage. For evaluation, we adopt both automatic metrics sensitive to control fidelity and human studies targeting appropriateness and consistency. To isolate the contribution of shared embeddings, we compare to baselines that train separate models per corpus and to multitask models without explicit control keys. We further probe robustness by varying corpus composition ratios.

Finally, we discuss practical considerations including attribute schema design, negative transfer diagnostics, and efficient inference. We outline data curation guidelines to minimize annotation drift that undermines cross-corpus alignment. We also review privacy and compliance implications when aggregating conversational data from distinct sources with differing consent regimes. Together, these considerations frame a reproducible path toward controllable, multi-corpus open-domain dialogue systems.

Introduction

Given a sentence s = w 1 w 2 ...w n where w i is the ith token in the sentence, an OpenIE system generates triples of the form (subject; predicate; object), where subject, predicate and object are the constituents of a triple. Open Information Extraction (OpenIE) aims to produce relational tuples without relying on a pre-specified schema, enabling scalable knowledge acquisition from unstructured text. Despite impressive progress, extracting tuples that are both informative and faithful to the source sentence remains challenging, particularly for predicates spanning discontiguous tokens and arguments expressed via appositives or coordination. Prior evaluations reveal that systems often trade precision for recall or vice versa, reflecting divergent design choices about granularity, context attribution, and inference vs. extraction boundaries. A recurring difficulty is distinguishing light-verb constructions and nominal predicates from adjuncts that should not be part of the relation.

Prior work on OpenIE spans rule-based extractors (Banko et al., 2007; Fader et al., 2011), clause-based and dependency-driven systems (Del Corro and Gemulla, 2013; Angeli et al., 2015), neural sequence labeling and tagging approaches (Stanovsky et al., 2018; Kolluru et al., 2020), encoder–decoder models with constrained decoding (Cui et al., 2018; Roy et al., 2019), calibration and confidence estimation methods (He et al., 2015; Jiang and Marquez, 2016), and standardized benchmarks such as OIE2016, CaRB, MinIE, and WiRe57, as well as downstream uses in QA and KB population (Etzioni et al., 2011; Mausam, 2016). Recent analyses emphasize the ambiguity in gold annotations across benchmarks, where minimal vs. context-rich tuples are scored differently under token-level and tuple-level metrics. Concurrently, there has been movement toward models that jointly segment, label, and canonicalize extractions, with constraints that penalize overlapping arguments and hallucinated predicates. These developments raise the question of how to evaluate tuple integrity when multiple semantically equivalent forms exist for a single sentence.

In parallel, semantic role labeling (SRL) and dependency parsing offer complementary signals for delimiting argument spans and predicate senses. While SRL provides role inventories aligned with frame semantics, their coverage and granularity differ from the open-domain relations recovered by OpenIE. Dependency edges capture local syntactic relations but can fragment multiword predicates or idioms that act as a single semantic unit. Bridging these resources has motivated multi-task learning setups that share encoders yet preserve task-specific decoding constraints. However, alignment noise between tasks can degrade extraction faithfulness if supervision is not carefully curated.

Another line of work studies confidence calibration and threshold selection to balance tuple precision and coverage in downstream applications. Calibrated scores facilitate integration into pipelines for question answering, summarization, and knowledge base population by enabling task-specific trade-offs. Nevertheless, score distributions are sensitive to domain shift, prompting research into post-hoc calibration and domain-adaptive fine-tuning. A practical desideratum is to preserve calibration under shifts in sentence length, syntactic complexity, and discourse context.

Evaluation remains a bottleneck due to heterogeneous annotation protocols and metrics. Token-level F1 can reward verbose tuples that include spurious modifiers, whereas tuple-level matching may penalize semantically faithful paraphrases. Human-in-the-loop assessment mitigates these issues but is costly and difficult to reproduce at scale. Recent proposals advocate for entailment-based validators that score tuples by their support in the source sentence, though these validators themselves introduce modeling biases. A unified evaluation protocol would benefit both method development and fair comparison across systems.

In this work, we focus on the tension between minimality and completeness in predicate-argument structure for OpenIE. We define criteria for tuple faithfulness that discourage over-aggregation while preserving necessary context such as negation, modality, and attribution. We further examine how discontiguity and coordination affect extraction boundaries and propose mechanisms for structured decoding that respect these phenomena. Our study targets both English newswire and web domains to test generality under stylistic variation.

We also investigate training strategies that reduce reliance on noisy distant supervision. By leveraging partial alignment signals from SRL and controlled paraphrase augmentation, we aim to decrease exposure bias and improve robustness. Additionally, we explore constrained decoding with lattice representations to allow flexible predicate composition without sacrificing precision. These components combine to form an extraction pipeline with explicit guarantees on tuple well-formedness.

Finally, we provide an evaluation suite that reports both calibration-aware and structure-aware metrics. We complement automatic metrics with lightweight human preference judgments to capture semantic adequacy. Through ablations, we assess the contribution of each modeling component and the sensitivity of results to annotation conventions. We release data, code, and trained models to support reproducibility and future research.

Quantization of Pre-trained Language Models. Quantization compresses a model by representing the 32-bit floating-point parameter with a low-bit representation, and has been widely used in various domains as it does not require designing a new model architecture. There have been many attempts to quantize task-specific BERT models (Zafrir et al., 2019;Shen et al., 2020;Zadeh et al., 2020) with only negligible performance drop on natural language understanding tasks. Recent works Bai et al., 2021) even push the weight bit-width down to as low as 1-bit. Despite the success of these approaches for BERT models, at-tempts to quantize generative PLMs are scarce, and the underlying difficulty remains unclear.

Contrastive Learning. Contrastive learning aims at pushing the representations of similar samples together while pulling those of dissimilar ones apart. and is widely used for large-scale self-supervised learning in various domains Sun et al., 2020a;Baevski et al., 2020;Anonymous, 2022), and multi-modal learning (Radford et al., 2021;Jia et al., 2021). Sim-CLR  directly uses other in-batch samples as negatives, and sufficient large batch size is required to work well. MoCo  maintains a large number of negative samples in a queue and uses a moving average key encoder to improve consistency. Contrastive learning without negative samples is also proposed in BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen and He, 2021). Contrastive representation distillation (Tian et al., 2019) distills the knowledge from the teacher network to the student network by maximizing the mutual information between them.

The closest work with our token-level contrastive distillation is Wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), which use in-utterance representations at different positions as negatives in speech learning. Besides the difference in the modality and tasks, our method also differs from theirs in (1) Model: We quantize the model parameters and activations while they do not; (2) Representation: For each sample, we use the output of the full-precision and the quantized networks as its two views, while they use the quantized and the contextualized representation.

(3) Loss: We calculate loss over all tokens in an auto-regressive manner, while they only calculate over the masked tokens non-autoregressively. Recent advances in representation learning for language and vision have reported strong gains with contrastive objectives across diverse settings, including InfoNCE-based frameworks and momentum encoders, with notable successes in SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo v2 (Chen et al., 2020b), CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), DeCLUTR (Giorgi et al., 2021), COLA (Zhuang et al., 2022), SimCSE (Gao et al., 2021), and Sentence-T5 (Ni et al., 2021).

spans: Recent studies on robustness and safety span a broad set of techniques and perspectives, including gradient-based word substitutions and paraphrase generation for adversarial crafting (Ebrahimi et al., 2018; Alzantot et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2020), certified and provable defenses for text classifiers (Jia et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2021), robustness benchmarks that target semantic invariances and perturbation resilience (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Gardner et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Goel et al., 2021), and safety evaluations for toxicity and bias in dialogue and language models (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2020; Welbl et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021).

Parallel threads examine prompt-based and instruction-tuned models under adversarial prompting and safety interventions, covering red teaming protocols (Perez et al., 2022; Ganguli et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022; Perez et al., 2023), jailbreak taxonomies and defenses (Wei et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023; Kumar et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2023), toxicity and harassment benchmarks for conversational systems (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Vidgen et al., 2021), and multilingual robustness datasets and methods (Xu et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2023; Leong et al., 2023).

Crafting adversarial examples and using them in training was previously shown to be an effective technique in improving NLP and ML models (Nie et al., 2020;Dinan et al., 2019;Kiela et al., 2021). Not only that, but adversarial attacks can reveal important vulnerabilities in our systems (Zhang et al., 2020a). Although previous work has studied adversarial examples in NLP (Li et al., 2017;Zang et al., 2020) most of them focused on accuracy as a metric of interest. Among the ones that studied toxicity and other ethical considerations (Wallace et al., 2019;Sheng et al., 2020) they did not put the focus on either conversational agents or they did not consider attacks being imperceptible. Cheng et al. (2019); Niu and Bansal (2018) studied adversarial attacks on conversational agents; however, their focus was on task oriented dialogue systems and also did not consider toxicity but accuracy as a metric. Xu et al. (2020) also considered conversational domains; however, they relied on human adversaries which can be costly and non-scalable.

Recent studies on robustness and safety span a broad set of techniques and perspectives, including gradient-based word substitutions and paraphrase generation for adversarial crafting (Ebrahimi et al., 2018; Alzantot et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2020), certified and provable defenses for text classifiers (Jia et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2021), robustness benchmarks that target semantic invariances and perturbation resilience (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Gardner et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Goel et al., 2021), and safety evaluations for toxicity and bias in dialogue and language models (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2020; Welbl et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021). Building on the observation that imperceptible perturbations are especially harmful in open-domain dialogue, we focus on the intersection of adversarial methods and safety metrics.

Despite this progress, there remains limited understanding of how imperceptible textual perturbations affect toxicity-controlled decoding in conversational agents. We ask whether automatic adversaries can simultaneously preserve semantics and fluency while elevating toxic risk under realistic chat settings. To address this, we develop a controllable adversarial generation framework that operates under a perceptual similarity constraint and targets safety filters typically used in moderating systems. Our approach couples a semantic-preserving paraphraser with a constrained search over subword edits, and evaluates outcomes with multi-dimensional toxicity metrics.

We evaluate on open-domain conversational datasets using both pretrained and safety-aligned dialogue models. Our experiments compare attack success, toxicity elevation, and perceptual indistinguishability under constrained budgets. We further ablate lexicon-sensitive versus semantic-invariance attacks to disentangle surface perturbations from meaning-preserving transformations. Parallel threads examine prompt-based and instruction-tuned models under adversarial prompting and safety interventions, covering red teaming protocols (Perez et al., 2022; Ganguli et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022; Perez et al., 2023), jailbreak taxonomies and defenses (Wei et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023; Kumar et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2023), toxicity and harassment benchmarks for conversational systems (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Vidgen et al., 2021), and multilingual robustness datasets and methods (Xu et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2023; Leong et al., 2023). Finally, we discuss implications for safe deployment and identify open challenges in certifying robustness for conversational safety.

In summary, this paper contributes a method for imperceptible adversarial attacks tailored to dialogue safety, a protocol for evaluating semantic preservation and toxicity elevation jointly, and an analysis of failure modes in toxicity-controlled decoding. We release code, attack artifacts, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future benchmarking.

Traditional sentiment analysis tasks are sentencelevel (Yang and Cardie, 2014;Severyn and Moschitti, 2015) or document-level (Dou, 2017;Lyu et al., 2020) oriented. In contrast, Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) is an aspect or entity oriented fine-grained sentiment analysis task. The most three basic subtasks are Aspect Term Extraction (ATE) (Hu and Liu, 2004;Yin et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018b;Xu et al., 2018;Ma et al., 2019;Chen and Qian, 2020;, Aspect Sentiment Classification (ASC) (Wang et al., 2016b;Tang et al., 2016;Ma et al., 2017;Fan et al., 2018;Li et al., 2018a;Li et al., 2021) and Opinion Term Extraction (OTE) Cardie, 2012, 2013;Fan et al., 2019;Wu et al., 2020b). The studies solve these tasks separately and ignore the dependency between these subtasks. Therefore, some efforts devoted to couple the two subtasks and proposed effective models to jointly extract aspect-based pairs. This kind of work mainly has two tasks: Aspect and Opinion Term Co-Extraction (AOTE) (Wang et al., 2016aDai and Song,  2019; Wang and Pan, 2019;Wu et al., 2020a) and Aspect-Sentiment Pair Extraction (ASPE) (Ma et al., 2018;Li et al., 2019a,b;He et al., 2019).

Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) first proposed the ASTE task and developed a two-stage pipeline framework to couple together aspect extraction, aspect sentiment classification and opinion extraction. To further explore this task, (Mao et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021a) transformed ASTE to a machine reading comprehension problem and utilized the shared BERT encoder to obatin the triplets after multiple stages decoding. Another line of research focuses on designing a new tagging scheme that makes the model can extract the triplets in an endto-end fashion Wu et al., 2020a;Xu et al., 2021;Yan et al., 2021). For instance,  proposed a positionaware tagging scheme, which solves the limitations related to existing works by enriching the expressiveness of labels. Wu et al. (2020a) proposed a grid tagging scheme, similar to table filling (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014;Gupta et al., 2016), to solve this task in an end-to-end manner. Yan et al. (2021) converted ASTE task into a generative formulation. However, these approaches generally ignore the relations between words and linguistic features which effectively promote the triplet extraction.

Beyond ABSA, related problems have been studied under joint extraction and structured prediction paradigms, including table-filling for relation extraction (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014;Gupta et al., 2016), dependency-guided opinion mining (Klinger and Cimiano, 2013;Zhang and Liu, 2014), span-based sentiment-role labeling (He et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019a), and sequence-to-sequence triplet generation (Yan et al., 2021;Xu et al., 2021;Wu et al., 2020a;Mao et al., 2021).

document:
We describe related works on multi-document summarization categorized on the model architecture.

Flat solutions. Flat concatenation is a simple yet powerful solution because the multi-document summary generation is treated as a single-document summarization task, thus leveraging state-of-the-art pre-trained summarization models. Consequently, processing all documents as a flat input requires models capable of handling long sequences. As previously done by DeYoung et al. (2021), Xiao et al. (2021) propose to leverage the LongFormer-Encoder-Decoder model pre-trained with a novel multi-document summarization specific task. They proved that a long-range transformer that encodes all documents is a straightforward yet effective solution, and they achieved new state-of-the-art results in several multi-document summarization datasets. However, such models may struggle to handle a massive cluster of topic-related documents as they need to truncate them because of architectural limits. Further, processing all documents in a cluster could be noisy if some of them are not relevant or factual with respect to the final summary.

Hierarchical solutions. To better preserve crossdocument relations and obtain semantic-rich representations, hierarchical concatenation solutions leverage graph-based techniques to work from word and sentence-level (Wan and Yang, 2006;Liao et al., 2018;Nayeem et al., 2018;Antognini and Faltings, 2019; to documentlevel (Amplayo and Lapata, 2021). Other hierarchical approaches include multi-head pooling and inter-paragraph attention architectures (Liu and Lapata, 2019a), attention models with Maximal Marginal Relevance (Fabbri et al., 2019), and attention across different granularity representations (Jin et al., 2020). Such models are often datasetspecific because of the custom architecture, so they struggle to adapt to other datasets and effectively leverage pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers.

Our solution. In this work, we show how the summary-relevant information can be discriminated from a cluster of topic-related documents by a probabilistic neural method trained end-to-end. In particular, our solution fully leverages pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers and does not apply input truncation that causes performance drop and discards much important content, unacceptable for the medical domain.

spans: Building on these foundational efforts, subsequent temporal KGE approaches diversify along representation choices and training signals, including diachronic embeddings (Trivedi et al., 2017;Goel et al., 2020), time-aware scoring functions such as TNTComplEx and TuckER-Bi (Lacroix et al., 2020;Balazevic et al., 2019), event-centric formulations treating time as first-class entities (García-Durán et al., 2018;HyTE, Dasgupta et al., 2018), and neural encoders that parameterize temporal evolution with recurrent or attention-based dynamics (Sarkar et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2020), alongside evaluation on standard temporal benchmarks like ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, YAGO11k, and WIKIDATA12k.
