In this section, we discuss related studies with repect to the contrastive learning framework and sentence embedding.
Contrastive learning has emerged as a unifying paradigm for representation learning across vision and language, grounded in instance discrimination and mutual information estimation.
Early formulations emphasize noise-contrastive objectives and negative sampling to separate semantically distinct instances while aligning augmented views of the same input.
In sentence embedding, contrastive objectives have been adapted to textual augmentations, dropout-induced views, and in-batch negatives to produce semantically meaningful vector spaces.
Recent work spans a diverse set of architectures and training regimes, including SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), MoCo v2 (Chen et al., 2020b), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), SimSiam (Chen and He, 2021), CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), CoCLR (Han et al., 2020), TSDAE (Wang et al., 2021), SimCSE (Gao et al., 2021), DeCLUTR (Giorgi et al., 2021), Sentence-BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), InfoNCE and its temperature variants (Oord et al., 2018; Wang and Isola, 2020), hard-negative mining strategies (Xiong et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020), and large-scale weakly supervised datasets such as YFCC100M (Thomee et al., 2016) and LAION-400M/5B (Schuhmann et al., 2021; 2022), as well as evaluation benchmarks like STS tasks (Cer et al., 2017; Marelli et al., 2014) and BEIR (Thakur et al., 2021).
A central challenge is balancing the strength of positive augmentations against the diversity of negatives, which influences both isotropy and semantic clustering in the learned space.
Temperature scaling, queue memory, and large-batch training each alter gradient magnitudes and the effective hardness of negatives.
For text encoders, dropout-as-augmentation, adversarial perturbations, and back-translation offer complementary routes to generate positive pairs without requiring paraphrase corpora.
Hard-negative mining, whether mined offline from retrieval indices or online via cross-encoder re-ranking, has been shown to reduce representation collapse and improve retrieval robustness.
Cross-modal extensions, particularly image–text contrast, reveal benefits of aligning complementary modalities, which can regularize language representations through grounded supervision.
At the same time, purely unsupervised sentence encoders benefit from domain-adaptive pretraining that narrows the distribution shift between generic corpora and target evaluation tasks.
Evaluation practices typically rely on semantic textual similarity, transfer to downstream classification, and zero-shot retrieval, but increasingly include robustness and calibration metrics.
Several studies note that margin-based variants and angular losses can encourage wider separation among negatives while preserving local semantic neighborhoods.
Regularization strategies, such as whitening, uniformity losses, and debiased contrastive terms, further shape the geometry of sentence spaces.
Finally, scaling laws observed for contrastive objectives suggest predictable gains with model and data size, though diminishing returns appear without improved negative curation and augmentation design.

spans: Prior work spans a broad range of techniques and settings, including regularization for calibration and overconfidence reduction in vision models (Szegedy et al., 2016; Müller et al., 2019; Pereyra et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2017), sequence-to-sequence training under label uncertainty (Pereyra et al., 2017; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Kannan et al., 2018; Müller et al., 2020), token-level smoothing for translation quality and robustness (Pereyra et al., 2017; Vaswani et al., 2017; Edunov et al., 2018; Ott et al., 2018), distributional targets and knowledge distillation variants (Hinton et al., 2015; Yuan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021), and multi-task or multilingual transfer regimes in which shared label spaces interact with smoothing (Johnson et al., 2017; Conneau et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020).

Label Smoothing The original label smoothing can be formalized as:

K denotes the number of classes, α is the label smoothing parameter, α/K is the soft label, ŷ is a vector where the correct label equals to 1 and others equal to zero and ŷLS is the modified targets.

Label smoothing is first introduced to image classification (Szegedy et al., 2016) and was later adopted across language modeling and machine translation as a simple yet effective regularizer. It modulates one-hot targets toward a uniform prior to reduce overconfidence while preserving task-specific signal in the majority mass on the gold class. Empirically, it improves calibration and sometimes top-1 accuracy, though excessive smoothing can harm rare-class discrimination.

Prior work spans a broad range of techniques and settings, including regularization for calibration and overconfidence reduction in vision models (Szegedy et al., 2016; Müller et al., 2019; Pereyra et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2017), sequence-to-sequence training under label uncertainty (Pereyra et al., 2017; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Kannan et al., 2018; Müller et al., 2020), token-level smoothing for translation quality and robustness (Pereyra et al., 2017; Vaswani et al., 2017; Edunov et al., 2018; Ott et al., 2018), distributional targets and knowledge distillation variants (Hinton et al., 2015; Yuan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021), and multi-task or multilingual transfer regimes in which shared label spaces interact with smoothing (Johnson et al., 2017; Conneau et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020).

Despite its simplicity, the choice of smoothing prior, schedule, and interaction with data imbalance remains unsettled. Recent analyses highlight that uniform priors can be suboptimal when class frequencies are highly skewed or label noise is structured. This motivates examining priors tied to observed confusion patterns or semantic similarity among classes.

Vocabulary Sharing Vocabulary sharing is widely applied in most neural machine translation studies (Vaswani et al., 2017;Song et al., 2019;Lin et al., 2020;Pan et al., 2021). Shared subword vocabularies facilitate parameter tying across source and target embeddings and can reduce the model footprint while improving transfer in morphologically related languages. In multilingual settings, vocabulary sharing also affects cross-lingual generalization by aligning token statistics and enabling consistent segmentation across languages.

However, vocabulary sharing introduces trade-offs between compression and expressivity, especially for distant language pairs with divergent morphologies and scripts. Overly aggressive sharing can increase token collision, degrade rare-word representation, and alter gradient allocation during training. Prior work explores segmentation granularity, script-aware tokenization, and adaptive vocabularies to mitigate these issues.

Connections between label smoothing and vocabulary sharing have begun to emerge in multilingual translation. Both mechanisms influence the distribution of probabilities over tokens and can indirectly regulate the entropy of the output distribution. When combined, their effects on calibration, robustness to noise, and low-resource transfer may be non-additive, suggesting the need for principled joint tuning.

In this paper, we study how smoothing priors interact with shared vocabularies under data imbalance and domain shift. We analyze calibration, BLEU, and robustness to synthetic noise across language pairs with varying script overlap. We further consider schedules that adapt smoothing strength to token frequency and evaluate their impact on rare-word recall and calibration under distributional shift.

Our contributions are threefold. First, we present a taxonomy of smoothing priors that incorporates frequency-aware, class-similarity, and temperature-scaled variants relevant to token-level models. Second, we provide an experimental protocol that disentangles effects from segmentation and vocabulary size by controlling for tokenizer-induced changes to sequence length. Third, we release code and pre-tokenized corpora to facilitate reproducible ablations.

We aim to clarify when uniform smoothing suffices and when structure-aware priors are warranted in the presence of shared subword inventories. The results underline the importance of jointly selecting vocabulary size, segmentation policy, and smoothing schedule to meet calibration and accuracy targets. Finally, we discuss deployment considerations, including computational overhead, hyperparameter stability across domains, and compatibility with distillation-based training.

We conclude by highlighting open questions on learning priors from data and integrating uncertainty estimates into decoding. Future work may explore curriculum-based schedules, multilingual adapters that modulate smoothing per language, and semi-supervised objectives that reconcile pseudo-label confidence with calibrated targets.

spans: Prior work on procedural text processing spans sequence labeling for step identification (Paris et al., 2002; Delpech and Saint-Dizier, 2008; Zhang et al., 2012), temporal and causal reasoning over instructional narratives (Takechi et al., 2003; Regneri et al., 2010; Tandon et al., 2020; Rajagopal et al., 2020), script and schema induction from large corpora (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008; Balasubramanian et al., 2013; Weber et al., 2018; Rudinger et al., 2015), and the construction of task-oriented knowledge bases using wikiHow or similar resources (Jung et al., 2010; Chu et al., 2017; Park and Motahari Nezhad, 2018; Koupaee and Wang, 2018).

Introduction

Linking procedural events across heterogeneous instructional corpora enables knowledge consolidation, reuse, and high-coverage downstream reasoning.
Lagos et al. (2017) share the same task formulation as ours.
Both works try to link steps (or spans within steps) to other wikiHow articles.
We make several important contributions over their work: (1) a search-then-rerank method significantly increases the linking recall; (2) more comprehensive experiments with human and downstream evaluation that showcase the quality and the usefulness of the linked data; and (3) a more beneficial data structure with broader coverage over all of wikiHow, not just the Computer domain.
Our framing also emphasizes cross-article compositionality, allowing links to serve as latent connectors for implicit prerequisites and shared subgoals.

Related Work

Procedural knowledge is commonly formalized through scripts and schemata, which capture stereotyped event sequences and role bindings.
Early cognitive theories introduced scripts (Abelson and Schank, 1977) and schemata (Rumelhart, 1975) as abstractions for routine activities, later instantiated in NLP as event chains, frames, and procedural graphs.
Prior work on procedural text processing spans sequence labeling for step identification (Paris et al., 2002; Delpech and Saint-Dizier, 2008; Zhang et al., 2012), temporal and causal reasoning over instructional narratives (Takechi et al., 2003; Regneri et al., 2010; Tandon et al., 2020; Rajagopal et al., 2020), script and schema induction from large corpora (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008; Balasubramanian et al., 2013; Weber et al., 2018; Rudinger et al., 2015), and the construction of task-oriented knowledge bases using wikiHow or similar resources (Jung et al., 2010; Chu et al., 2017; Park and Motahari Nezhad, 2018; Koupaee and Wang, 2018).
Recent datasets target fine-grained procedural reasoning, but trade off scale for annotation depth, limiting generalization in open-domain settings.
Crowdsourced collections have been influential yet typically small after adjudication (Singh et al., 2002; Regneri et al., 2010; Li et al., 2012; Wanzare et al., 2016; Rashkin et al., 2018).
Large-scale acquisition from community instruction portals promises coverage but raises challenges in normalization, step alignment, and link reliability.
Efforts to leverage wikiHow for knowledge-base construction demonstrate feasibility at scale (Jung et al., 2010; Chu et al., 2017; Park and Motahari Nezhad, 2018), while leaving open questions about intrinsic link quality and downstream task impact.
Our study addresses these gaps by coupling retrieval-augmented linking with human validation and task-based evaluation.
We further quantify how search-then-rerank improves recall without sacrificing precision, and we analyze link utility for intent reasoning and subtask transfer.
Finally, we release a normalized, cross-article graph with broader topical coverage, enabling reproducible experimentation and future extensions.

spans: Recent efforts further broaden this landscape with block-sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019), routing-based sparsity in Switch Transformers (Fedus et al., 2021), linearized attention kernels such as Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021), kernelized low-rank projections in Linformer (Wang et al., 2020), and long-context pretraining strategies exemplified by LED (Beltagy et al., 2020) and UL2 (Tay et al., 2023).
document:
Sparse attention mechanism The full attention mechanism has a quadratic memory cost. Prior research works have proposed different sparse attention mechanisms to reduce the memory cost. Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) uses a dilated sliding window of blocks and global attention patterns. BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020) employs sliding window and random blocks. Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) uses the locality-sensitive hashing. In addition to optimizing the encoder self-attention, Huang et al. (2021) proposes head-wise positional strides to reduce the cost of the encoder-decoder attention. Recent efforts further broaden this landscape with block-sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019), routing-based sparsity in Switch Transformers (Fedus et al., 2021), linearized attention kernels such as Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021), kernelized low-rank projections in Linformer (Wang et al., 2020), and long-context pretraining strategies exemplified by LED (Beltagy et al., 2020) and UL2 (Tay et al., 2023). However, sparse attention diminishes the benefits of pretraining and sacrifices parts of the receptive field.

Extract-then-generate method The model first extracts salient text snippets from the input, followed by generating a concise overall summary. Most two-stage summarization approaches (Zhang et al., 2019;Lebanoff et al., 2019;Xu and Durrett, 2019;Bajaj et al., 2021) are trained separately, which suffer from information loss due to the cascaded errors. Some approaches attempt to reduce that loss by bridging the two stages. Chen and Bansal (2018) adopts reinforcement learning with a sentence-level policy gradient method. Bae et al. (2019) proposes summary-level policy gradient. In addition to the drawbacks explained in Section 2.3, our model is different as we jointly train an extractthen-generate model for summarization using latent variables.

Divide-and-conquer approach A common approach in long input summarization is divide-andconquer (Gidiotis and Tsoumakas, 2020;Grail et al., 2021). This approach breaks a long input into multiple parts, which are summarized separately and combined to produce a final complete summary. However, these models do not capture the contextual dependencies across parts and assumes a certain structure of the input (such as paper sections).

Hierarchical models Various hierarchical models have been proposed to handle the longer inputs. Cohan et al. (2018) models the document discourse structure with a hierarchical encoder and a discourse-aware decoder to generate the summary. HAT-Bart (Rohde et al., 2021) proposes a new Hierarchical Attention Transformer-based architecture that attempts to capture sentence and paragraphlevel information. HMNet (Zhu et al., 2020) builds a hierarchical structure that includes discourselevel information and speaker roles. However, these models focus mainly on model performance and not on reducing the memory and computational cost.

spans: Sentence rewriting IUR can be considered to be similar to the sentence rewriting task (Xu and Veeramachaneni, 2021;Lin et al., 2021;Chen and Bansal, 2018;Cao et al., 2018), and related advances also include controllable paraphrasing (Iyyer et al., 2018;Krishna et al., 2020), style transfer for text (Shen et al., 2017;Li et al., 2018), edit-based revision (Guu et al., 2018;Dong et al., 2019), and constrained decoding with lexical control (Hu et al., 2019;Post and Vilar, 2018).
document: Sentence rewriting IUR can be considered to be similar to the sentence rewriting task (Xu and Veeramachaneni, 2021;Lin et al., 2021;Chen and Bansal, 2018;Cao et al., 2018), and related advances also include controllable paraphrasing (Iyyer et al., 2018;Krishna et al., 2020), style transfer for text (Shen et al., 2017;Li et al., 2018), edit-based revision (Guu et al., 2018;Dong et al., 2019), and constrained decoding with lexical control (Hu et al., 2019;Post and Vilar, 2018). Recent studies have been addressed the IUR task with various sophisticated methods. Text generation IUR can be formulated as text generation by using the seq2seq model (Pan et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2021). For the generation, several well-known pre-trained models have been applied (Lewis et al., 2020;Brown et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2020) with promising results. We employ the T5 model (Raffel et al., 2020) as the main component to rewrite utterances. To address the problem of missing important tokens in model's rewriting, we enhance T5 by introducing a Picker and two labeling methods (Section 3.2). The learning of Θ is composed by only using utterance generation (the generator) or the combination of two tasks: important token identification (the picker) and utterance generation (the generator). To situate IUR within broader dialog modeling, prior work has explored context-aware rewriting for ASR repair and disfluency removal (Rao et al., 2020;Cho et al., 2021), user-intent preserving paraphrase generation (Zhang et al., 2020), and slot-focused reformulation in task-oriented systems (Elgohary et al., 2019). Compared to classification-style IUR approaches that detect and tag utterance issues (Kim et al., 2021), our focus remains on direct generation with explicit token guidance. We assume access to dialogue context and system schema, and we condition the generator on these signals to improve factual retention. The picker identifies salient tokens that must be preserved or emphasized during rewriting, thereby reducing omissions of entity names, slot values, and negations. We further introduce two supervision strategies for the picker, one derived from alignment heuristics and one from gradient-based salience, to balance precision and coverage. The generator is a T5-based encoder–decoder with constrained copy bias, while the picker is a lightweight token scorer that outputs hard and soft masks. Training proceeds in two stages, starting with generator pretraining on synthetic rewrites and followed by joint finetuning with picker guidance. Inference uses a constrained beam search that respects hard masks and prioritizes soft-mask tokens through a modified length-normalized score. We evaluate on multi-domain dialog benchmarks with entity-rich utterances and report improvements in content preservation and factuality over strong pretrained baselines. Ablations demonstrate that both picker supervision strategies are complementary and that the constrained decoding contributes most to recall of critical tokens.

spans: Recent neural and probabilistic approaches to unsupervised word and morpheme segmentation span Bayesian models with hierarchical priors (Goldwater et al., 2009;Mochihashi et al., 2009;Teh, 2006b), heuristic or constraint-based algorithms evaluated on morph-rich languages (Creutz and Lagus, 2005;Virpioja et al., 2013), subword modeling for ASR and MT using BPE and unigram LM segmentation (Sennrich et al., 2016;Kudo, 2018), and evaluation on benchmark corpora such as Brent and CHILDES for English as well as Mboshi and Japhug for low-resource settings (Brent, 1999;MacWhinney, 2000;Boito et al., 2018;Jacques, 2016).
document:
Going from audio recordings to fully annotated transcripts implies two successive segmentation steps: the first segments words and happens during the production of phonemic or orthographic transcripts; the second further splits words into morphs, which are then annotated with syntactic information and glosses. We mostly focus on the former task, assuming a two-step process: first, the computation of a phonemic transcript, that we assume are given; then the segmentation into words for which we consider two settings: batch and online learning. The word and morpheme segmentation tasks are closely related and rely on similar tools: using the Japhug corpus, which contains both levels of segmentations, we also study the implications of using lists of words vs. morphemes as weak supervision.

In its baseline form, the word segmentation process is fully unsupervised, and the only training material is a set of transcribed sentences (see Fig.). Prior work has emphasized that purely unsupervised models benefit from simple priors when data are scarce. Recent neural and probabilistic approaches to unsupervised word and morpheme segmentation span Bayesian models with hierarchical priors (Goldwater et al., 2009;Mochihashi et al., 2009;Teh, 2006b), heuristic or constraint-based algorithms evaluated on morph-rich languages (Creutz and Lagus, 2005;Virpioja et al., 2013), subword modeling for ASR and MT using BPE and unigram LM segmentation (Sennrich et al., 2016;Kudo, 2018), and evaluation on benchmark corpora such as Brent and CHILDES for English as well as Mboshi and Japhug for low-resource settings (Brent, 1999;MacWhinney, 2000;Boito et al., 2018;Jacques, 2016). This landscape motivates a closer examination of probabilistic assumptions under limited supervision.

We rely on Bayesian non-parametric approaches to word segmentation (see (Cohen, 2016) for a thorough exposition), and our baselines are the unigram version of the dpseg model (Goldwater et al., 2009) and a variant where the underlying Dirichlet Process is replaced by a Pitman-Yor Process as in (Neubig, 2014). We selected unigram models for their simplicity, which (a) makes them amenable to the processing of very small sets of sentences; (b) makes the online learning setting tractable. While using higher order models or more sophisticated models of the same family (Teh, 2006b;Mochihashi et al., 2009) may improve the performance (see for an experimental comparison), we believe that in our low-resource conditions these variations would be small, and would not change our main conclusions.

Word segmentation models fundamentally rely on probabilistic models for word sequences defining P(w = w1 . . . wT); word sequences can also be viewed as segmented sequences of characters y = y1 . . . yL, so that the same model can be used for the joint probability of (y, b), with b = b1 . . . bL representing the vector of boundary locations where value bt = 1 (resp. bt = 0) denotes a boundary (resp. no boundary) after symbol yt. In an unsupervised setting, these boundaries are hidden and are latent variables in the model. Such models lend themselves well to Gibbs sampling, which repeatedly produces samples of each boundary given all the other boundaries in the corpus.

In dpseg, the underlying sequence model is a unigram model: P(w1 . . . wT) = ΠT t=1 P(wt). The probability of individual words corresponds to a Dirichlet Process with parameters α, the concentration parameter, and P0, the base distribution, and yields the following formulation for the conditional probability of wt given the past words w<t:

where nw(w<t) counts the number of times w has occurred in the past. With lower values of α, the most frequent words tend to be generated more (hence, concentration), while with higher values, the words are more smoothly distributed. P0, the base distribution, assigns scores to arbitrary character strings; Goldwater et al. (2009) use a length model and a uniform character model. For word w made of characters y1, ..., ym, P0 is computed as:

where p# is the probability to end the word. For this model, Gibbs sampling compares at each position t two sequences of words wt=0 (no boundary at position t) and wt=1 (a boundary is inserted). As these sequences only differ minimally, terms such as P(bt = 0|y, b−t) are readily derived (see eg. (Goldwater et al., 2009)). Gibbs sampling is performed for a number of iterations that are sufficient to reach convergence, and we use the last iteration to uncover the resulting segmentation. To speed up mixing, Goldwater et al. (2009) also use annealing, so that a larger search space is explored.

An extension of dpseg, denoted pypseg, uses a Pitman-Yor Process (PYP) instead of the DP and generalises equation (1) with an additional discount parameter, which enables better control of the generation of new words. PYPs are introduced in (Teh, 2006b;Mochihashi et al., 2009); a fast implementation is in (Neubig, 2014). For our experiments, both models have been re-implemented in Python. This implementation will be publicly released. We also adopt a consistent tokenization pipeline across corpora to ensure comparability of results.

We consider both batch and online learning regimes to highlight trade-offs between convergence stability and adaptation speed. In batch mode, we process shuffled sentence sets and tune α and p# on a held-out split. In online mode, we process sentence streams with periodic hyperparameter updates using marginal likelihood estimates. For weak supervision, we experiment with lexicon priors derived from either word lists or morpheme inventories. These priors are injected into P0 via a mixture with a lexicon-backed distribution controlling the mass on attested units. We further analyze how lexicon noise impacts boundary decisions under different concentration settings. Finally, we report segmentation quality using boundary F1 and lexicon precision and recall on the Japhug and Mboshi corpora.

Figure: The sentence segmentation task illustrated with a sentence from the Mboshi corpus: 'báa ámikúndá poo yá kala' ('they found the old village'). The two possible segmentations only differ in one boundary at position t = 6, one (w6=0) where 'ámikúndá' is one single unit and one (w6=1) where it is split in two.

spans: Several works have since shifted focus toward the more challenging phoneme discovery problem by formulating it as a self-supervised learning problem where the semantics of the speech are known, such as from translation, phonemelevel language models or other sensory modalities such as vision. (Jansen, 2013) has studied the use of pairwise word identity labels for training phoneme discovery models based on Gaussian mixture models (GMM); (Harwath and Glass, 2019) analyzes the hidden layers of a two-branch neural network trained to retrieve spoken captions with semantically related images and finds strong correlation between segment representation and phoneme boundaries. (Harwath et al., 2020) adds hierarchical vector quantization (VQ) layers in the same retrieval network and is able to find a much smaller codebook than the unsupervised neural approach (Baevski et al., 2020), and achieve high correlation with the phoneme inventory. (Godard et al., 2018;Boito et al., 2019) has studied the possibility of learning semantic units using an attention-based speech-to-text translation system, though the units appear to correlate more with words.

Due to the challenge of learning phonemes, early works on unsupervised speech representation learning (Park and Glass, 2005;Lee and Glass, 2012;Ondel et al., 2016) focus on learning speech segments sharing similar acoustic properties, or phones, without taking into account the meaning of the speech they are part of. There are two main approaches in this direction. One approach is to learn discrete phone-like units without any textual labels by modeling phone labels of the speech segments as latent variables. In particular, (Park and Glass, 2005;Jansen et al., 2010) first detect segments with recurring patterns in the speech corpus followed by graph clustering using the similarity graph formed by the segments. (Lee and Glass, 2012;Ondel et al., 2016;Kamper et al., 2016) develop probabilistic graphical models to jointly segment and cluster speech into phone-like segments. An extension to the latent variable approach is to introduce additional latent variables such as speaker identity (Ondel et al., 2019) or language identity (Yusuf et al., 2020) and develop mechanisms to disentangle these variables.

With the advance of deep learning, neural network models have also been proposed to learn unsupervised phone-level representation either by first learning a continuous representation (Chung et al., 2019;Feng et al., 2019;Nguyen et al., 2020) followed by off-line clustering, or by learning a discrete representation end-to-end with Gumbel softmax (Eloff et al., 2019b;Baevski et al., 2020) or vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) (van den Oord et al., 2017;Chorowski et al., 2019;. However, codebooks learned by the neural approaches tend to be much larger than the number of phonemes (Baevski et al., 2020), leading to low scores in standard phoneme discovery metrics. The second approach utilizes weak supervision such as noisy phone labels predicted by a supervised, multilingual ASR system trained on other languages. Along this direction, ( Żelasko et al., 2020;Feng et al., 2021a) systematically study the performance of zero-shot crosslingual ASR on 13 languages trained with international phonetic alphabet (IPA) tokens and found that the system tends to perform poorly on unseen languages. Instead, (Feng et al., 2021b) is able to discover phone-like units by clustering bottleneck features (BNF) from a factorized time-delay neural network (TDNN-f) trained with phone labels predicted by a crosslingual ASR (Feng et al., 2021a).

Several works have since shifted focus toward the more challenging phoneme discovery problem by formulating it as a self-supervised learning problem where the semantics of the speech are known, such as from translation, phonemelevel language models or other sensory modalities such as vision. (Jansen, 2013) has studied the use of pairwise word identity labels for training phoneme discovery models based on Gaussian mixture models (GMM); (Harwath and Glass, 2019) analyzes the hidden layers of a two-branch neural network trained to retrieve spoken captions with semantically related images and finds strong correlation between segment representation and phoneme boundaries. (Harwath et al., 2020) adds hierarchical vector quantization (VQ) layers in the same retrieval network and is able to find a much smaller codebook than the unsupervised neural approach (Baevski et al., 2020), and achieve high correlation with the phoneme inventory. (Godard et al., 2018;Boito et al., 2019) has studied the possibility of learning semantic units using an attention-based speech-to-text translation system, though the units appear to correlate more with words. Works on unsupervised speech recognition  attempt to learn to recognize phonemes by leveraging the semantic information from a phoneme language model unpaired with the speech, typically by matching the empirical prior and posterior distributions of phonemes either using cross entropy (Yeh et al., 2019) or adversarial loss Baevski et al., 2021). Throughout the paper, we use P{•} to denote probability. We use capital letters to denote random variables and lower-case letters to represent samples of random variables. We use P X := P{X = x} to denote both probability mass and density functions of random variable X, depending on whether it is continuous or discrete. Further, denote P Y |X (y|x) := P{Y = y|X = x} as the true conditional probability distribution of random variable Y = y given random variable X = x. The probability simplex in R d is denoted as ∆ d .

This section provides background and motivation for task and domain adaptability applied to concern detection in the 2017 French Election domain ( §2.1, §2.2), including concern values induced from Moral Foundations Theory (MFT) ( §2.3).
We focus on the challenge of detecting moral concern categories in short, noisy political texts under distribution shift across topics and sources.
Task adaptability refers to transferring a concern detector trained on one label schema or annotation guideline to another without extensive relabeling.
Domain adaptability addresses generalization from one electoral context, medium, or time period to another where lexical and pragmatic cues differ.
We ground our study in MFT, which posits a set of moral dimensions (e.g., Care/Harm, Fairness/Cheating, Loyalty/Betrayal, Authority/Subversion, Sanctity/Degradation) that can be operationalized as concern values at the utterance level.
Prior work on political text classification spans lexicon-based approaches (e.g., LIWC; Pennebaker et al., 2015), neural encoders pre-trained on general corpora (Mikolov et al., 2013; Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019), election-specific stance and ideology datasets (SemEval-2016 Task 6; Iyyer et al., 2014; Johnson and Goldwasser, 2016; Conover et al., 2011), and domain adaptation methods including adversarial invariance (Ganin et al., 2016), parameter-efficient adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020), and prompt tuning (Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023).
Concern detection has been explored in the context of moral rhetoric and persuasion, with early studies employing hand-crafted features and later work leveraging contextual representations.
However, most existing resources are centered on English-language debates or social media, limiting direct transfer to French political discourse.
The 2017 French Election provides a natural stress test for adaptability due to rapid agenda shifts, multilingual media ecosystems, and evolving party coalitions.
We curate a corpus that interleaves campaign speeches, televised debates, and Twitter posts, annotated with MFT-derived concern values.
To examine task adaptability, we consider mappings between fine-grained moral cues and coarser concern categories used by different annotator groups.
To examine domain adaptability, we analyze shifts across platforms and time segments within the campaign timeline.
Our methodology compares label-space alignment techniques with representation-level adaptation under controlled data budgets.
We report performance under zero-shot, few-shot, and continual adaptation regimes to assess robustness and data efficiency.
Finally, we discuss implications for building portable concern detectors that remain stable under policy salience changes and linguistic variability.
We release data splits, annotation guidelines, and code to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

spans: Recent works (Petroni et al., 2019;Davison et al., 2019;Jiang et al., 2020) design prompts to probe the factual and commonsense knowledge encoded within a PLM. Recent works (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b;Gao et al., 2021) demonstrate that even smaller PLMs have similar few-shot learning capacity. Le Scao and Rush (2021) analyzes the effect of prompting and concludes that a single prompt may be worth 100 training examples in fine-tuning. In parallel, studies on cloze-style prompting and calibration report sensitivity to template wording and verbalizers (Zhao et al., 2021;Holtzman et al., 2021;Min et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022). Additional lines of work examine prompt transfer across tasks and domains (Vu et al., 2021;Logan IV et al., 2022;Lester et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2023). Efforts on prompt ensembling and selection explore stability and robustness (Shin et al., 2020;Ben-David et al., 2023;Zhong et al., 2021;Rubin et al., 2022).

Gao et al. (2021) uses re-ranking to find the best label words by fine-tuning a RoBERTa model on the candidates searched by RoBERTa, and using an external generation model for data augmentation of prompt templates; AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020) uses a gradient-based search to determine both prompts and label words. However, these methods require parameter updates with gradient descent, which is infeasible without access to the model weights (e.g., GPT-3). PET and its variants also require a large unlabeled set and need to be fine-tuned multiple times. AutoPrompt uses discretization techniques to approximately map a continuous vector back to tokens in the vocabulary (i.e., "vocablization"). These searched prompts and labels are often uninterpretable by humans. Different from these prior studies, our proposed AMuLaP is a simple and interpretable method for few-shot prompting that can work well with and without access to model weights. Concurrently to our work,  propose a method that exploits an external knowledge base to find label mapping. Other contemporaneous efforts include calibration strategies for verbalizer selection (Zhao et al., 2021;Holtzman et al., 2021), soft-prompt tuning and prefix methods for parameter-efficient adaptation (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021;Prefix-Tuning), retrieval-augmented prompting that conditions on external corpora (Lewis et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021;Borgeaud et al., 2022), and evaluation frameworks for prompt sensitivity across datasets (Perez et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2023;Sanh et al., 2022;Mishra et al., 2022).

Discrete Prompts The release of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) has led to interest in prompting, a new way to leverage pretrained language models (PLM). Brown et al. (2020) proposes an intuitive in-context learning paradigm by concatenating a few input and output examples and feeding them to the language model and let the model autoregressively generate answers for new examples. Recent works (Petroni et al., 2019;Davison et al., 2019;Jiang et al., 2020) design prompts to probe the factual and commonsense knowledge encoded within a PLM. Recent works (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b;Gao et al., 2021) demonstrate that even smaller PLMs have similar few-shot learning capacity. Le Scao and Rush (2021) analyzes the effect of prompting and concludes that a single prompt may be worth 100 training examples in fine-tuning. In parallel, studies on cloze-style prompting and calibration report sensitivity to template wording and verbalizers (Zhao et al., 2021;Holtzman et al., 2021;Min et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022). Additional lines of work examine prompt transfer across tasks and domains (Vu et al., 2021;Logan IV et al., 2022;Lester et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2023). Efforts on prompt ensembling and selection explore stability and robustness (Shin et al., 2020;Ben-David et al., 2023;Zhong et al., 2021;Rubin et al., 2022).

Instead of manually designing prompts (i.e., prompt engineering), some recent studies also explore automatic prompt generation. PETAL (Schick et al., 2020) augments Pattern Exploiting Training (PET, Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b) with automatically identified label words; Gao et al. (2021) uses re-ranking to find the best label words by fine-tuning a RoBERTa model on the candidates searched by RoBERTa, and using an external generation model for data augmentation of prompt templates; AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020) uses a gradient-based search to determine both prompts and label words. However, these methods require parameter updates with gradient descent, which is infeasible without access to the model weights (e.g., GPT-3). PET and its variants also require a large unlabeled set and need to be fine-tuned multiple times. AutoPrompt uses discretization techniques to approximately map a continuous vector back to tokens in the vocabulary (i.e., "vocablization"). These searched prompts and labels are often uninterpretable by humans. Different from these prior studies, our proposed AMuLaP is a simple and interpretable method for few-shot prompting that can work well with and without access to model weights. Concurrently to our work,  propose a method that exploits an external knowledge base to find label mapping. Other contemporaneous efforts include calibration strategies for verbalizer selection (Zhao et al., 2021;Holtzman et al., 2021), soft-prompt tuning and prefix methods for parameter-efficient adaptation (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021;Prefix-Tuning), retrieval-augmented prompting that conditions on external corpora (Lewis et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021;Borgeaud et al., 2022), and evaluation frameworks for prompt sensitivity across datasets (Perez et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2023;Sanh et al., 2022;Mishra et al., 2022).

spans: Recent neural methods for idiom and multiword expression processing span contextualized representation learning and task-specific architectures, including sequence tagging with BiLSTMs and CRFs (Ma and Hovy, 2016;Gharbieh et al., 2016), transformer-based encoders fine-tuned for idiom token identification (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Reddy et al., 2021), probing studies of idiomaticity in BERT-like models (Nivre et al., 2020;Ettinger, 2020;Liu et al., 2022), and contrastive learning frameworks for compositionality (Wu et al., 2020;Chiang et al., 2022;Zeng and Bansal, 2023); complementary resources include PARSEME MWE corpora (Ramisch et al., 2018;Baldwin and Kim, 2010), the VNC-Tokens dataset (Cook et al., 2008), and idiom lexica such as IDIX and SemEval-2013 Task 5 (Korkontzelos et al., 2013).

Many idiom detection models build on insights about unconventional meaning in metaphor. A number of approaches use distributional models, such as Kintsch (2000), Utsumi (2011), Sa-Pereira (2016), and Shutova et al. (2012, the latter of which was one of the first to implement a fully unsupervised approach for encoding relationships between words, their contexts, and their dependencies. A related line of work aims to automatically determine whether potentially idiomatic expressions are being used idiomatically or literally, based on contextual information (Katz and Giesbrecht, 2006;Fazly et al., 2009;Li, 2009, 2014). Our measure of conventionality is inspired by the insights of these models; as described in Section 3.2, our measure uses differences in embeddings across contexts. Meanwhile, approaches to collocation detection have taken a probabilistic or informationtheoretic approach that seeks to identify collocations using word combination probabilities. PMI is a frequently-used quantity for measuring cooccurrence probabilities (Fano, 1961;Church and Hanks, 1990). Other implementations include selectional association (Resnik, 1996), symmetric conditional probability (Ferreira and Pereira Lopes, 1999), and log-likelihood (Dunning, 1993;Daille, 1996).

While much of the literature in NLP recognizes that idioms share a cluster of properties, including semantic idiosyncrasy, syntactic inflexibility, and institutionalization (e.g., Sag et al., 2002;Fazly and Stevenson, 2006;Fazly et al., 2009), our approach is novel in attempting to characterize idioms along two orthogonal dimensions that correspond to specific proposals from the cognitive science literature. To situate these choices, we first review recent work that operationalizes idiomaticity within neural language models. Recent neural methods for idiom and multiword expression processing span contextualized representation learning and task-specific architectures, including sequence tagging with BiLSTMs and CRFs (Ma and Hovy, 2016;Gharbieh et al., 2016), transformer-based encoders fine-tuned for idiom token identification (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Reddy et al., 2021), probing studies of idiomaticity in BERT-like models (Nivre et al., 2020;Ettinger, 2020;Liu et al., 2022), and contrastive learning frameworks for compositionality (Wu et al., 2020;Chiang et al., 2022;Zeng and Bansal, 2023); complementary resources include PARSEME MWE corpora (Ramisch et al., 2018;Baldwin and Kim, 2010), the VNC-Tokens dataset (Cook et al., 2008), and idiom lexica such as IDIX and SemEval-2013 Task 5 (Korkontzelos et al., 2013). In Section 4 we describe how we instantiate our dimensions in a model-agnostic scoring pipeline. We also clarify how our dataset selection controls for lexical frequency and topical confounds. Our measures may offer a new avenue for tackling the problem of automatic idiom detection. We report empirical results on three benchmarks to quantify the contribution of each dimension. Finally, we outline limitations and opportunities for broader generalization beyond English idioms. We conclude by discussing implications for cognitively informed representations in NLP.

The state of NLP in South Asia. So far, initiatives for improving language technology in South Asia have largely focused on languages with official status and some degree of standardisation. These include cross-lingual projects such as Indic-NLPSuite (Kakwani et al., 2020), the EMILLE corpus (McEnery et al., 2000), and iNLTK (Arora, 2020), and workshops like DravidianLangTech (Chakravarthi et al., 2021) and WILDRE (Jha et al., 2020). As table 1 shows, only a select few languages benefit from NLP research-even fewer benefit from (commericialised) products like Google Translate or OCR tools.

NLP/CL has proven to be an expansive field as of late. Computational historical linguistics is inextricably linked with computational approaches to fundamental linguistic tasks: corpus building, POS tagging and dependency parsing, morphological analysers, and lexical databases. Work on these has progressed fast for the big languages. For example, Hindi, the highest-resourced South Asian language, has massive hand-annotated dependency treebanks , state-of-the-art neural distributional semantic transformer models (Jain et al., 2020;Khanuja et al., 2021), and machine translation models to and from English (Saini and Sahula, 2018). This is not to say that there are no resources at all for the languages (Joshi et al., 2020) terms "the Left-Behinds". Linguists, for example, have compiled rudimentary treebanks for many languages, simply waiting to be digitised and converted to a multilingual format like Universal Dependencies; these include Palula (Liljegren and Haider, 2015) and Toda (Emeneau, 1984), which are yet to be the subject of any NLP research work. There are also new treebanks in Universal Dependencies for Kangri, Mandeali, Bhojpuri (Ojha and Zeman, 2020), and Magahi.

Comparative linguistics in South Asia. The study of historical and comparative linguistics has a long history in South Asia, beginning well before similar threads of inquiry in the Western linguistic tradition, with grammarians like Pān . ini (c. 5th century BCE) and Hēmacandra (1088Hēmacandra ( -1173 analysing historical and dialectal language from a comparative perspective.

Following the recognition by Western philologists of an Indo-European language family that includes Sanskrit, comparative study of the languages of South Asia began in earnest. As a result, several comprehensive comparative grammars featuring the Dravidian (Caldwell, 1856;Andronov, 2003;Krishnamurti, 2003) and Indo-Aryan families (Beames, 1872;Hoernlé, 1880;Bloch, 1934;Masica, 1993) have appeared in the years since. Emeneau (1956) was the first to posit a South Asian zone of language contact and convergence spanning multiple families. Subsequent work on micro-areal zones has yielded many insights into the nature of linguistic interactions in the region (Peterson, 2017;Liljegren et al., 2021;Toulmin, 2006).

The sole South Asia-wide linguistic data collection effort to ever be undertaken was the Linguistic Survey of India, completed about a century ago (Grierson, 1903(Grierson, -1928. To date, there has been no comparable centralised data resource on South Asian languages of its magnitude-covering typological features, the lexicon, and sociolinguistic phenomena.

Data in the earliest comparative works was frequently sourced from high-prestige standard varieties like Delhi Hindi, with progress on studying and collecting data from more localised lects largely proceeding in isolation. Compilation of comparative data continued sporadically throughout the 20th century, resulting in works such as the Comparative Dictionary of the Indo-Aryan Languages (Turner, 1962(Turner, -1966 and the Dravidian Etymology Dictionary (Burrow and Emeneau, 1984) which attempt at a more diverse spectrum of language data. Meanwhile, progress on documentation and comparative analysis of the Austroasiatic (Anderson, 2008) ies drawing upon their data for purposes such as substrate analysis often lack nuance and familyinternal consistency.

spans: Recent work spans diverse paradigms for structured-to-text and text-to-structure supervision, including TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020), TaBERT (Yin et al., 2020), TURL (Deng et al., 2020), T5-based table pretraining (Liu et al., 2022), GrailQA and Grail-KG reasoning (Gu et al., 2021), Spider for text-to-SQL (Yu et al., 2018), WikiTableQuestions (Pasupat and Liang, 2015), and TOTTO (Parikh et al., 2020), as well as augmentation and prompt-based pipelines for compositionality (Furrer et al., 2020; Min et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021).

Template-based data generation has been previously used for data augmentation, for example to inject numerical skills (Geva et al., 2020), and to improve consistency (Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020), and zero-shot accuracy (Zhao et al., 2019). In addition, templates were used for dataset construction (Talmor and Berant, 2018;Thorne et al., 2021), and to analyse model generalization (Rozen et al., 2019). In this work, we automatically generate examples by instantiating templates using structured data. Since our method relies solely on tables as input, it is highly scalable, has rich lexical diversity, and can be easily extended to new skills and domains.

Recently, Thorne et al. ( 2021) introduced the WIKINLDB dataset, which includes queries that require reasoning over a set of textual facts. Queries are instantiated with values from a knowledge graph (KG), and facts are generated by a LM. Unlike this work, WIKINLDB is focused on evaluating reasoning skills. We, on the other hand, show that generated examples can be used to endow a pretrained LM with new reasoning skills. Moreover, tables are much easier to collect at scale compared to KGs, which tend to have limited coverage. Data augmenatation techniques have been extensively explored in RC, QA, and dialogue (Feng refers to all questions that do not require reasoning over the image modality.     Khashabi et al., 2020;Alberti et al., 2019;Puri et al., 2020;Bartolo et al., 2021). Here, we focus on tables as a valuable source for data generation.

Pre-training over tables has focused in the past on reasoning over tables and knowledge-bases Yin et al., 2020;Herzig et al., 2020;Müller et al., 2021;Yu et al., 2021;Neeraja et al., 2021b). Here, we use pre-training over tables to improve reasoning over text. We leave evaluation on tasks beyond RC to future work.

Recent work spans diverse paradigms for structured-to-text and text-to-structure supervision, including TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020), TaBERT (Yin et al., 2020), TURL (Deng et al., 2020), T5-based table pretraining (Liu et al., 2022), GrailQA and Grail-KG reasoning (Gu et al., 2021), Spider for text-to-SQL (Yu et al., 2018), WikiTableQuestions (Pasupat and Liang, 2015), and TOTTO (Parikh et al., 2020), as well as augmentation and prompt-based pipelines for compositionality (Furrer et al., 2020; Min et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021).

Error-driven sampling has been considered in the past in the context of active learning (Sharma et al., 2018), reinforcement learning (Graves et al., 2017;Glover and Hokamp, 2019;Xu et al., 2019), transfer learning Pilault et al., 2021), and distributionally robust optimization (Oren et al., 2019;Sagawa et al., 2020), where the goal is to perform well over a family of distributions. Similar to Gottumukkala et al. (2020), we compute heterogeneous batches based on error rates, and show that this improves efficiency and performance.

spans: Across both EGEC and CGEC, prior studies have explored diverse modeling and evaluation setups without a unifying perspective, including neural sequence-to-sequence architectures with attention (Sutskever et al., 2014;Bahdanau et al., 2015;Luong et al., 2015), Transformer-based encoders and decoders (Vaswani et al., 2017;Zhao and Wang, 2020;Tang et al., 2021), sequence editing frameworks such as LaserTagger and GECToR (Malmi et al., 2019;Omelianchuk et al., 2020;Liang et al., 2020), pretrained language models adapted to grammatical error correction (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2020), and evaluation protocols spanning M2, ERRANT, and span-based F-scores (Dahlmeier and Ng, 2012;Bryant et al., 2017;Felice et al., 2016).

EGEC resources. There is a lot of work on EGEC data construction. As the two earliest EGEC datasets, FCE (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011) and NU-CLE (Dahlmeier et al., 2013) adopt the error-coded annotation paradigm. In contrast, JFLEG (Napoles et al., 2017) collects sentences from TOFEL exams and adopts the direct rewriting paradigm. W&I (Bryant et al., 2019) also chooses the direct rewriting paradigm, and for each original sentence additionally provides the language proficiency level of the writer. All four datasets are composed of essays from non-native English speakers and provide multiple references.

Recently, researchers start to annotate small-scale EGEC data for texts written by native English speakers, including AESW (Daudaravicius et al., 2016), LOCNESS (Bryant et al., 2019), GMEG (Napoles et al., 2019) and CWEB (Flachs et al., 2020). In the future, we plan to extend this work to texts written by native Chinese speakers. CGEC resources. Compared with EGEC, progress in CGEC data construction largely lags behind. As thoroughly discussed in Section 1, NLPCC18 (Zhao et al., 2018) and CGED (Rao et al., 2018(Rao et al., , 2020 are the only two evaluation datasets for CGEC research. Besides them, there are also a few resources for training CGEC models, e.g., Lang8 corpus (Zhao et al., 2018) and HSK corpus (Xun, 2018).

Recent progress in CGEC. In the NLPCC-2018 shared task (Zhao et al., 2018), many systems adopt Seq2Seq models, based on RNN/CNN. Recent work mainly utilizes Transformer Zhao and Wang, 2020;Tang et al., 2021). Hinson et al. (2020) first employ a Seq2Edit model for CGEC, and achieve comparable performance with the Seq2Seq counterparts. Some systems in the CGED-2020 shared task (Rao et al., 2020) directly employ the open-source Seq2Edit model, i.e., GECToR (Liang et al., 2020;Fang et al., 2020). Besides the above two mainstream models, Li and Shi (2021) for the first time apply a non-autoregressive neural machine translation model to CGEC.

Besides modeling optimization, techniques like data augmentation (Zhao and Wang, 2020;Tang et al., 2021) and model ensemble (Hinson et al., 2020) have also been proved very useful for CGEC. Across both EGEC and CGEC, prior studies have explored diverse modeling and evaluation setups without a unifying perspective, including neural sequence-to-sequence architectures with attention (Sutskever et al., 2014;Bahdanau et al., 2015;Luong et al., 2015), Transformer-based encoders and decoders (Vaswani et al., 2017;Zhao and Wang, 2020;Tang et al., 2021), sequence editing frameworks such as LaserTagger and GECToR (Malmi et al., 2019;Omelianchuk et al., 2020;Liang et al., 2020), pretrained language models adapted to grammatical error correction (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2020), and evaluation protocols spanning M2, ERRANT, and span-based F-scores (Dahlmeier and Ng, 2012;Bryant et al., 2017;Felice et al., 2016).

Code-switching in NLP has seen a rise of interest in recent years, including a dedicated workshop starting in 2014 (Diab et al., 2014) and still ongoing (Solorio et al., 2021). CS in machine translation also has a long history (Le Féal, 1990;Climent et al., 2003;Sinha and Thakur, 2005;Johnson et al., 2017;Elmadany et al., 2021;Xu and Yvon, 2021), but has seen a rise of interest with the advent of large multilingual models such as mBART (Liu et al., 2020) or mT5 (Xue et al., 2020;Gautam et al., 2021;Jawahar et al., 2021). Due to the lack of available CS data and the ease of single-word translation, most of these recent related MT works have synthetically created CS data for either training or testing by translating one or more of the words in a sentence (Song et al., 2019;Nakayama et al., 2019;Xu and Yvon, 2021). We differ from those works by using naturally occurring CS data (Section 3) which models the real-world CS distribution rather than arbitrary language mixing.

For spoken input, as present in ASR and ST, synthetically creating realistic CS data is more challenging than it is for MT. However, dedicated ASR corpora that contain natural CS exist, including the Bangor Miami (Deuchar et al., 2014), SEAME (Zeng et al., 2018), and the recent largescale ASRU 2019 task. These corpora generally do not contain translations of the ASR annotations, since they were designed for the ASR task only. However, there exist two exceptions, which we leverage to derive our ST CS data set, described in Section 3.

There also exists a wide range of prior modeling work on CS in ASR models, for a variety of strategies (Lyu et al., 2006;Ahmed and Tan, 2012;Seki et al., 2018;Luo et al., 2018;Lu et al., 2020;Du et al., 2021). However, the recently introduced large multilingual models for speech, such as Wav2Vec, Wav2Vec 2.0, Schneider et al. (2019); Baevski et al. (2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021), are still underexplored with regards to their CS performance. Recent efforts also examine multilingual pre-training and adaptation for code-switching using a variety of corpora and methods without a unified framing of objectives (Conneau et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2020;Xue et al., 2021;Pires et al., 2019;Artetxe et al., 2020;Pratap et al., 2020;Zhou et al., 2021;Rijhwani et al., 2017).

Handling mixed languages also requires understanding what languages are being spoken. Systems that support mixed language input therefore require some form of automatic LID -either as an explicit component on the utterance (Mabokela et al., 2014;Xu and Yvon, 2021) or word-level (Lyu and Lyu, 2008a;Nakayama et al., 2019), or implicitly learned by the underlying model(s) via a multi-task learning setup (Lyu and Lyu, 2008b;Watanabe et al., 2017;Hou et al., 2020). In our work, we leverage both, exploring utterance-level LID components as well as implicit learning of utterance and word level LID.

In both MT and ASR, prior publications have also included the study of intra-word mixing of languages (Yılmaz et al., 2018;Mager et al., 2019), a phenomenon we do not explore in our work.

Text Question Answering. In recent years, the natural language processing research community has devoted substantial efforts to text question answering tasks (Huang et al., 2018a;Zhu et al., 2018;Xu et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020;Gong et al., 2020). Within the growing body of work on machine reading comprehension, an important sub-task of text question answering, two signature attributes have emerged: the availability of large benchmark datasets (Choi et al., 2018;Elgohary et al., 2018;Reddy et al., 2019) and pre-trained language models (Devlin et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2020). However, these existing works typically focus on modeling the complicated context dependency in text form. In contrast, we focus on enabling the machine to build the capability of language recognition and dialogue modeling in both speech and text domains. Spoken Question Answering. In parallel to the recent works in natural language processing (Huang et al., 2018a;Zhu et al., 2018), these trends have also been pronounced in the speech field Haghani et al., 2018;Lugosch et al., 2019;Palogiannidi et al., 2020;You et al., 2021), where spoken question answering (SQA), an extended form of QA, has explored the prospect of machine comprehension in spoken form. Previous work on SQA typically includes two separate modules: automatic speech recognition (ASR) and text question answering. It involves transferring spoken content to ASR transcriptions, and then employs NLP techniques to handle speech tasks.

Existing methods (Tseng et al., 2016;Serdyuk et al., 2018;Su and Fung, 2020) focus on optimizing each module in a two-stage manner, where errors in the ASR module would result in severe performance loss. Recent advances in multimodal QA and speech-language modeling provide a broad context for this direction, with works on end-to-end SQA pipelines (Haghani et al., 2018; Lugosch et al., 2019), joint speech-text pre-training (Baevski et al., 2020; Chung et al., 2021), conversational QA over text (Reddy et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2018), and robustness to ASR noise via data augmentation and confidence modeling (Khurana et al., 2021; Parthasarathi and Strom, 2019) all illustrating complementary threads without explicit unification. SpeechBERT (Chuang et al., 2019) cascaded the BERT-based models as a unified model, and then trained it in a joint manner of audio and text. However, the existing SQA methods aimed at solving a single question given the related passage, without building and maintaining the connections of different questions in the human conversations. In addition, we compare our Spoken-CoQA with existing SQA datasets (See Table ). Unlike existing SQA datasets, Spoken-CoQA is a multi-turn conversational SQA dataset, which is more challenging than single-turn benchmarks.

Knowledge Distillation. Hinton et al. (Hinton et al., 2015) introduced the idea of Knowledge Distillation (KD) in a teacher-student scenario. In other words, we can distill the knowledge from one model (massive or teacher model) to another Dataset Conversational Spoken Answer Type TOEFL (Tseng et al., 2016) × √ Multi-choice S-SQuAD  × √ Spans ODSQA ) (small or student model). Previous work has shown that KD can significantly boost prediction accuracy in natural language processing and speech processing (Kim and Rush, 2016;Hu et al., 2018;Huang et al., 2018b;Hahn and Choi, 2019), while adopting KD-based methods for SQA tasks has been less explored. In this work, our goal is to handle the SCQA tasks. More importantly, we focus the core nature property in speech and text: Can spoken conversational dialogues further assist the model to boost the performance? Finally, we incorporate the knowledge distillation framework to distill reliable dialogue flow from the spoken contexts, and utilize the learned predictions to guide the student model to train well on the noisy input data.

Current self-explaining models often enforce shortest yet sufficient rationales, with the assumption that short rationales are more intuitive to humans (Lei et al., 2016;Bastings et al., 2019). Paranjape et al. (2020) proposes an information bottleneck approach to enable the rationale length control. However current studies only assessed the methods with auto-metrics and did not evaluate human understanding on different rationale lengths.

On the other hand, a line of studies measure the "human rationales alignment" (Paranjape et al., 2020), which compares how well the model generated rationales are agreeing with human grounded annotations (DeYoung et al., 2019). There are also studies involving human-in-the-loop to evaluate the explanations, such as asking humans to choose a better model Ribeiro et al. (2016). However, there is a lack of human evaluations on validating the effect of rationale length on human understanding. Beyond these, prior work has examined extractive sufficiency losses (Jain et al., 2020), post-hoc faithfulness diagnostics (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), counterfactual sufficiency and comprehensiveness tests (DeYoung et al., 2020), perturbation-based rationale generators (Chang et al., 2020), and prompt-driven attribution methods for large language models (Sun et al., 2022; Wiegreffe et al., 2022), as well as datasets with human-marked evidence spans such as ERASER, FEVER, MultiRC, and e-SNLI, and modeling recipes including sparsity-inducing selectors, entropy regularization, and confidence calibration. In this work, we conduct a human-subject study to directly test how rationale length influences comprehension and trust. We design a controlled annotation task where participants answer questions with or without model-provided rationales of varying lengths. Our study measures task accuracy, response time, perceived clarity, and perceived helpfulness as primary outcomes. We further analyze whether rationale length interacts with instance difficulty and annotator expertise. To isolate length from content quality, we normalize for semantic coverage using matched-span paraphrases. We also assess downstream calibration by comparing confidence reports to correctness across length conditions. Finally, we discuss implications for constructing explanation policies that trade off brevity and informativeness in applied settings.

Introduction

Measuring bias in NLP systems remains a central challenge for both methodological rigor and societal impact. Several studies have investigated the prevalence of bias in NLP models (Caliskan et al., 2017; May et al., 2019; Bordia and Bowman, 2019; Davidson et al., 2019; Magee et al., 2021), with many focusing on cases of models exhibiting stereotyping behavior. Though Blodgett et al. (2020) point out that what these studies mean by "bias" can vary quite widely, the finding that models encode associations derived from negative stereotypes and social biases is well replicated. In defining bias for this study, our design aligns most closely with the definition of representational harms by Crawford (2017) as harms that "occur when systems reinforce the subordination of some groups along the lines of identity." When constructing data to measure this bias, contrasting groups of people rather than just relevant attributes highlights the difference in outcomes and impact on groups targeted by a given stereotype (Dev et al., 2021).

Related Work

Early measurement efforts emphasized word-level associations using distributional semantics and embedding geometry. Subsequent work extended these paradigms to sentence encoders and contextual models, revealing that debiasing techniques often reduce measured associations without improving downstream fairness. Dataset construction has therefore shifted toward templates and minimal pairs to stress-test group contrasts while controlling for lexical content. Crowdsourced annotations and synthetic generation techniques have been combined to probe both localized bias triggers and broader discourse effects. Beyond intrinsic evaluations, researchers have introduced a range of extrinsic and diagnostic protocols spanning sentiment analysis debiasing benchmarks (Kiritchenko and Mohammad, 2018), toxicity and hate speech classification audits (Waseem and Hovy, 2016;Davidson et al., 2017;Sap et al., 2019), counterfactual fairness test suites (Garg et al., 2019;Maudslay et al., 2019), representational association probes such as WEAT and SEAT (Caliskan et al., 2017;May et al., 2019), and dataset-level diversity assessments including BOLD, CrowS-Pairs, and StereoSet (Dhamala et al., 2021;Nangia et al., 2020;Nadeem et al., 2021). These tools vary in their operationalization of bias, coverage of protected attributes, and sensitivity to distributional shifts. However, methodological choices about context, label space, and calibration can confound interpretation of results across settings.

Problem Formulation

We focus on representational harms that manifest as asymmetric associations between social groups and evaluative attributes. Our setting assumes access to a pretrained model and a collection of templated and naturally occurring sentences with minimal confounds. We target group-contrast designs where hypotheses can be tested via counterfactual swaps and controlled context perturbations. Our primary measurement objective is to disentangle lexical priors from compositional effects and pragmatic cues. To that end, we construct evaluation strata that isolate polarity, negation, and intensity, and we report bias estimates with uncertainty quantification.

Contributions

We present a unified measurement protocol that integrates group-contrast templates, natural text probes, and counterfactual augmentation. We introduce calibration-aware scoring that normalizes for base-rate variation across prompts. We release a corpus annotated for pragmatic operators relevant to bias elicitation. We provide a diagnostic analysis comparing measurement stability under domain shift and prompt paraphrase. We discuss ethical considerations and responsible reporting practices to guide interpretation and deployment.

Introduction

The game of Mafia is particularly well-suited for the goal of determining whether the deceptive participants in a conversation can be identified from the contents of their utterances. In Mafia, speakers have asymmetric access to the ground truth, strategically manage information over multiple rounds, and face incentives to mislead without contradicting shared public evidence. These properties create a natural laboratory for studying deception in interactive, high-stakes, and adversarial settings. Prior work in deception detection has largely focused on monologic statements or dyadic interviews, leaving open questions about group interaction and evolving strategies. Prior work spans several strands, including deception detection from linguistic cues using lexical and psycholinguistic markers (Bond and DePaulo, 2006; Ott et al., 2011; Newman et al., 2003; Mihalcea and Strapparava, 2009), multimodal analyses that integrate prosody and facial dynamics (Perez-Rosas et al., 2015; Krishnamurthy et al., 2018; Carriero et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2022), conversational games as testbeds such as Werewolf and Diplomacy (Kashima et al., 2015; Niculae and Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil, 2016; Bakhtin et al., 2022; Hughes et al., 2023), and computational pragmatics for modeling implicature and belief states (Goodman and Frank, 2016; Potts, 2012; Andreas and Klein, 2016; Fried et al., 2018). However, debates persist over whether deception is primarily signaled by surface-level markers (e.g., pronoun shifts, hedging) or by higher-order discourse moves that manipulate common ground. In interactive games like Mafia, utterances must be interpreted relative to dynamically updated beliefs and publicly observable votes, complicating the modeling assumptions of static classifiers. Recent advances in conversational representation learning enable modeling of speaker roles, turn-taking structure, and temporal dependencies over long horizons. At the same time, the availability of large language models suggests opportunities to infer latent intentions from contextualized evidence. The combination of structured game mechanics and rich linguistic behavior provides leverage for causal probing of deception cues. We consider how public commitments, contradictions, and justificatory burden interact with role knowledge to alter predictive signals. By aligning utterances to event timelines (e.g., accusations, defenses, and votes), we can test whether patterns of framing, entailment, and uncertainty correlate with deceptive roles. We further explore whether deception cues are stable across players or heavily individualized. Our empirical setting draws on annotated multi-session Mafia transcripts with role labels and turn-level metadata. The dataset supports both discriminative analyses of cue prevalence and generative analyses of strategy adaptation across days. We additionally discuss methodological controls to avoid overfitting to idiosyncratic game jargon or community norms. Finally, we outline evaluation protocols that emphasize generalization across groups and sessions rather than in-sample accuracy.

Related Work

Work on deception in dialog has begun to examine multi-party interaction, but benchmarks remain limited in scale and in the granularity of role annotations. Studies in argumentation mining highlight the importance of tracking claims and evidence, suggesting analogs for accusation-defense cycles in Mafia. Research in social deduction games offers insights into coordination and lying incentives while underscoring the need for transparent, reproducible corpora.

document: Relation Extraction. Relation extraction aims at extracting relation between entities within a given sentence. Many relation extraction methods (Qian et al., 2008;Zeng et al., 2014;Zhou et al., 2016) are supervised model. Recently, some studies focus on pre-training language model (Devlin et al., 2019;Lan et al., 2020) because of its powerful capability of semantic representation. Wu and He (2019) propose R-BERT that uses BERT to extract relation features and incorporates entity information to perform relation extraction. Soares et al. (2019) propose a relation representation learning method based on BERT and have shown promising results. However, these models require labeled data. Unsupervised relation extraction (Yu et al., 2017;Saha et al., 2018;Stanovsky et al., 2018) seeks to discover relations without annotated pairs by clustering or leveraging patterns from large corpora. Distant supervision (Mintz et al., 2009) alleviates annotation costs by aligning text with knowledge bases but introduces label noise. Noise-robust training and multi-instance learning have been developed to mitigate misalignment issues in distant supervision (Surdeanu et al., 2012;Riedel et al., 2010;Lin et al., 2016). Contextualized encoders further improve extraction by modeling entity-aware representations and capturing long-range dependencies across sentences (Zhang et al., 2017;Wang et al., 2019). Prior distant supervision and open information extraction lines also offer alternatives, including Mintz et al. (2009), Hoffmann et al. (2011), Riedel et al. (2010), Angeli et al. (2015), OpenIE (Banko et al., 2007; Stanovsky and Dagan, 2016), and TACRED and SemEval datasets (Zhang et al., 2017; Hendrickx et al., 2010), as well as neural architectures such as PCNN (Zeng et al., 2015), multi-instance learning with attention (Lin et al., 2016), and span-based transformers for relation classification (Baldini Soares et al., 2019). Despite these advances, cross-domain generalization remains a central challenge due to vocabulary shift and schema mismatch between source and target corpora (Alt et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2021). Recent work explores domain-adaptive pretraining and instance reweighting to improve robustness under domain shift (Gururangan et al., 2020;Jiang and Zhai, 2007). Zero-shot and few-shot RE further reduce annotation requirements by exploiting descriptions of relations or external knowledge (Levy et al., 2017;Huang et al., 2022). Prompt-based and instruction-tuned language models offer a promising alternative by mapping natural language prompts to relation labels (Gao et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2022;Liu et al., 2023). Nevertheless, prompt brittleness and label verbalization ambiguity can degrade transfer performance when prompts are underspecified (Scao and Rush, 2021;Min et al., 2022). In parallel, multi-sentence and document-level RE investigate reasoning across multiple evidence sentences, often integrating coreference and discourse cues (Yao et al., 2019;Nan et al., 2020). Knowledge-augmented approaches enrich representations with graph structures and typed constraints to improve factual consistency (Yao et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2019). Evaluation remains challenging due to label noise, partial matches, and the need to assess calibration in low-resource regimes (Bekoulis et al., 2018;Zhou and Chen, 2022). To address reliability, some studies measure confidence estimates and selective prediction for relation predictions under distribution shift (Kamath et al., 2020;Lei et al., 2022).

spans: Prior work on entity linking spans a broad methodological spectrum, including collective graph-based inference and local context ranking, knowledge-base aware priors, and neural architectures for mention representations and candidate scoring (Milne and Witten, 2008; Cucerzan, 2007; Ganea and Hofmann, 2017; Le and Titov, 2018; Yamada et al., 2020; Logeswaran et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2021). Systems leverage surface form statistics from Wikipedia anchors, type constraints, and coherence signals that jointly select entities across a document (Hoffart et al., 2011; Pershina et al., 2015; Ratinov et al., 2011; Guo and Barbosa, 2018). Contextualized embeddings and cross-encoder scoring further improved disambiguation accuracy under in-KB assumptions (Kolitsas et al., 2018; Broscheit, 2019; Poerner et al., 2020; De Cao et al., 2021). Zero-shot or open-world extensions incorporate description encoders and retrieval over dense indices to handle unseen entities and tail mentions (Logan IV et al., 2019; Gillick et al., 2019; Botha et al., 2020; Orr et al., 2021). Benchmarks commonly used in this line include CoNLL-YAGO, TAC-KBP, AIDA, and WNED, alongside Wikipedia-derived splits (Hoffart et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2010; Guo and Barbosa, 2018; Wu et al., 2020).

Related strands on cross-document coreference and entity discovery explore clustering-based objectives, graph partitioning, and neural span linking over mentions distributed across a corpus (Bagga and Baldwin, 1998; Gooi and Allan, 2004; Singh et al., 2011; Barhom et al., 2019; Cattan et al., 2020; Caciularu et al., 2021; Ravenscroft et al., 2021). Recent work integrates retrieval-augmented representations, pairwise scoring with pruning, and constrained decoding for consistent entity assignments (Lee et al., 2017; Joshi et al., 2019; Kantor and Globerson, 2019; Yu et al., 2020). Discovery-oriented approaches adopt weak supervision, bootstrapped mention expansion, and deduplication over web-scale corpora (Dalvi et al., 2014; Balog and Ramampiaro, 2013; Zhiltsov et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2017). Training regimes vary from contrastive learning over entity descriptions to multi-task setups mixing typing, linking, and span coreference (Zhang et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2020; Orr et al., 2021; De Cao et al., 2021). Datasets and evaluations often rely on TAC KBP, ECB+, Wikilinks, and recently curated open-entity testbeds (Beckett et al., 2020; Barhom et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2012; Ganea and Hofmann, 2017).
