Introduction

Entity linking remains a central problem for building coherent knowledge-centric applications across domains and modalities. Classical pipelines decompose the task into candidate generation and candidate ranking, often injecting priors from surface forms, types, and document-level coherence. However, recent neural methods blur this boundary by learning dense representations that support both retrieval and scoring within a unified embedding space. Despite progress, scalability and generalization to tail entities and noisy contexts continue to challenge deployed systems.

Prior work on entity linking spans a broad methodological spectrum, including collective graph-based inference and local context ranking, knowledge-base aware priors, and neural architectures for mention representations and candidate scoring (Milne and Witten, 2008; Cucerzan, 2007; Ganea and Hofmann, 2017; Le and Titov, 2018; Yamada et al., 2020; Logeswaran et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2021). Systems leverage surface form statistics from Wikipedia anchors, type constraints, and coherence signals that jointly select entities across a document (Hoffart et al., 2011; Pershina et al., 2015; Ratinov et al., 2011; Guo and Barbosa, 2018). Contextualized embeddings and cross-encoder scoring further improved disambiguation accuracy under in-KB assumptions (Kolitsas et al., 2018; Broscheit, 2019; Poerner et al., 2020; De Cao et al., 2021). Zero-shot or open-world extensions incorporate description encoders and retrieval over dense indices to handle unseen entities and tail mentions (Logan IV et al., 2019; Gillick et al., 2019; Botha et al., 2020; Orr et al., 2021). Benchmarks commonly used in this line include CoNLL-YAGO, TAC-KBP, AIDA, and WNED, alongside Wikipedia-derived splits (Hoffart et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2010; Guo and Barbosa, 2018; Wu et al., 2020).

A parallel thread studies cross-document coreference and its interface with entity discovery. These efforts often model mention similarity, temporal signals, and discourse structure while constraining cluster consistency at scale. Advances in hierarchical encoders and memory-augmented architectures have further enabled robust aggregation of sparse evidence across long documents. Nevertheless, a tension remains between the expressivity of pairwise models and the tractability of global inference over large graphs.

Our approach targets this tractability–expressivity trade-off by designing clustering-aware dual encoders that accommodate efficient retrieval and consistent assignment. We derive training signals from soft cluster structure discovered over unlabeled corpora, and we use those signals to shape the embedding geometry for linking. The method supports both standard in-KB linking and discovery of out-of-KB entities without modifying the backbone retriever. We evaluate the approach on established datasets and on a new benchmark emphasizing tail-entity coverage.

Related strands on cross-document coreference and entity discovery explore clustering-based objectives, graph partitioning, and neural span linking over mentions distributed across a corpus (Bagga and Baldwin, 1998; Gooi and Allan, 2004; Singh et al., 2011; Barhom et al., 2019; Cattan et al., 2020; Caciularu et al., 2021; Ravenscroft et al., 2021). Recent work integrates retrieval-augmented representations, pairwise scoring with pruning, and constrained decoding for consistent entity assignments (Lee et al., 2017; Joshi et al., 2019; Kantor and Globerson, 2019; Yu et al., 2020). Discovery-oriented approaches adopt weak supervision, bootstrapped mention expansion, and deduplication over web-scale corpora (Dalvi et al., 2014; Balog and Ramampiaro, 2013; Zhiltsov et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2017). Training regimes vary from contrastive learning over entity descriptions to multi-task setups mixing typing, linking, and span coreference (Zhang et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2020; Orr et al., 2021; De Cao et al., 2021). Datasets and evaluations often rely on TAC KBP, ECB+, Wikilinks, and recently curated open-entity testbeds (Beckett et al., 2020; Barhom et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2012; Ganea and Hofmann, 2017).

We position our contribution within efficient alternatives to cross-encoders for large-scale linking. Cross-encoders remain the accuracy leader but exhibit quadratic cost in candidate size and limited batching throughput. Dual encoders and bi-encoders offer sub-linear retrieval via ANN search while enabling end-to-end training with negative mining. Our findings indicate that clustering-informed objectives can bridge a portion of the accuracy gap without sacrificing latency.

Alternatives to Cross-Encoders Our work demonstrates how clustering-based training and prediction improves dual-encoder based models for linking and discovery. If prediction efficiency, and not training efficiency, was the only concern, one could use model distillation (Hinton et al., 2015;Izacard and Grave, 2021, inter alia). We could also consider models such as poly-encoders as an alternative to dual-encoders (Humeau et al., 2020).

spans: Neural coherence research has also expanded beyond entity-centric views to include discourse relations, attention-based sequence encoders, and pre-trained representations (Li and Hovy, 2014; Li and Jurafsky, 2017; BERTScore, Zhang et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020; Mohiuddin et al., 2021; Moon et al., 2019; Mesgar and Strube, 2018; Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019), with additional benchmarks and evaluation protocols appearing in work on sentence ordering and summary coherence (Barzilay and Lee, 2004; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2019).
document:
Entity-based modeling has been the prevailing approach to model coherence in pre-neural models. The entity grid is its most well-known implementa-tion (Barzilay and Lapata, 2008). It represents entities in a two-dimensional array to track their transitions between sentences. Many variations have been proposed to improve this model, e.g., projecting the grid into a graph representation (Guinaudeau and Strube, 2013) or converting the grid to a neural model (Tien Nguyen and Joty, 2017).

However, the neural version of the entity grid (Tien Nguyen and Joty, 2017) has two limitations. First, Lai and Tetreault (2018) state that entity grids applied to downstream tasks are often extremely sparse. In their evaluation, it is difficult to find meaningful entity transitions between sentences in the grids. Accordingly, this model performs worse than other neural models. More importantly, this neural model cannot provide any clues of how this model works since Tien Nguyen and Joty (2017) apply a convolutional layer on the entity grid. The feature map of the convolutional layer is not interpretable. They cannot examine which entity is assigned more importantly than others by their model. In contrast, we constrain our model to capture focus on entities using noun phrases. Then our model tracks the changes of focus. Hence, it provides us with an interpretable focus (Section 5).

More recently, Moon et al. (2019) propose a neural coherence model to exploit both local and structural aspects. They evaluate their model on an artificial task only, the shuffle test, which determines whether sentences in a document are shuffled or not. However, recent studies (Pishdad et al., 2020) claim that this artificial task is not suitable to evaluate coherence models. Lai and Tetreault (2018) show that the neural coherence models, which achieve the best performance on this task, do not outperform non-neural models on downstream tasks. More recently, Mohiuddin et al. (2021) find a weak correlation between the model performance in artificial tasks and downstream tasks. Neural coherence research has also expanded beyond entity-centric views to include discourse relations, attention-based sequence encoders, and pre-trained representations (Li and Hovy, 2014; Li and Jurafsky, 2017; BERTScore, Zhang et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020; Mohiuddin et al., 2021; Moon et al., 2019; Mesgar and Strube, 2018; Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019), with additional benchmarks and evaluation protocols appearing in work on sentence ordering and summary coherence (Barzilay and Lee, 2004; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2019). In our evaluation, we compare Moon et al. (2019) with ours in an artificial task as well as in three downstream tasks. Moon et al. (2019) perform the best in the artificial task, but do not outperform our model in three downstream tasks (Section 4).

Learning self-supervised tasks on large-scale multilingual texts has proven to be effective for pretraining cross-lingual language models. Masked language modeling (MLM; Devlin et al. 2019) is typically used to learn cross-lingual encoders such as multilingual BERT (mBERT; Devlin et al. 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020). The crosslingual language models can be further improved by introducing external pre-training tasks using parallel corpora. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) introduces the translation language modeling (TLM) task that predicts masked tokens from concatenated translation pairs. ALM utilizes translation pairs to construct codeswitched sequences as input. InfoXLM (Chi et al., 2021b) considers an input translation pair as crosslingual views of the same meaning, and proposes a cross-lingual contrastive learning task. Several pre-training tasks utilize the token-level alignments in parallel data to improve cross-lingual language models (Cao et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2021;Hu et al., 2020a;Chi et al., 2021c). Prior work has also explored multilingual denoising autoencoding, multilingual machine translation pretraining, and retrieval-augmented cross-lingual objectives in diverse settings, including mBART (Liu et al., 2020), MASS (Song et al., 2019), m2m-100 (Fan et al., 2021), mBART50 (Tang et al., 2021), and mRAG-style approaches (Lewis et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2021), along with alignment-enhanced variants such as ERNIE-M (Ouyang et al., 2021) and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), as well as multilingual adapters (Pfeiffer et al., 2020) and parameter-efficient finetuning methods like LoRA (Hu et al., 2022).

In addition, parallel data are also employed for cross-lingual sequence-to-sequence pre-training. XNLG (Chi et al., 2020) presents cross-lingual masked language modeling and cross-lingual autoencoding for cross-lingual natural language generation, and achieves the cross-lingual transfer for NLG tasks. VECO (Luo et al., 2020) utilizes crossattention MLM to pretrain a variable cross-lingual language model for both NLU and NLG. mT6 (Chi et al., 2021a) improves mT5 (Xue et al., 2021) by learning the translation span corruption task on parallel data. ∆LM (Ma et al., 2021) proposes to align pretrained multilingual encoders to improve cross-lingual sequence-to-sequence pre-training.

Here we first review the related work of DA for NLP, then expound the differences between our method and the major existing ones. According to the methodology of data generation, existing methods can be categorized into three types: rule-based, interpolation-based, and model-based, respectively.

Rule-based DA approaches typically manipulate surface forms to preserve labels while introducing lexical or syntactic diversity. Common transformations include token-level substitutions, phrase reordering, and template-driven paraphrasing designed to respect task constraints. Back-translation is often considered a hybrid of rule-based and model-based, but it is generally grouped with model-driven paraphrasing when neural MT is used. Interpolation-based approaches operate either in the input space or in a learned representation space to create convex combinations of examples. For classification, these methods often interpolate both inputs and labels to encourage linear decision boundaries. For sequence labeling, alignment-aware mixing strategies are sometimes required to avoid label drift across spans. Model-based methods synthesize data by conditioning language models on labels, prompts, or partial inputs, optionally filtering with task-specific validators. They can target rare phenomena by sampling under explicit controllable attributes.

Rule-based DA relies on handcrafted heuristics and transformation templates such as synonym replacement, back-translation pipelines, and paraphrase rules (Zhang et al., 2015; Sennrich et al., 2016; Wei and Zou, 2019; Xie et al., 2020), interpolation-based DA constructs synthetic examples in representation or input space via mixup, manifold mixup, and vicinal risk methods (Zhang et al., 2018; Verma et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020), and model-based DA leverages pretrained generators, masked language modeling, and conditional text synthesis, including EDA variants, back-translation with neural MT, contextual augmentation with BERT-style models, and prompt-based sampling (Kobayashi, 2018; Edunov et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021).

Despite the breadth of techniques, the choice among these paradigms depends on task sensitivity to semantic drift, calibration needs, and compute constraints. For low-resource classification, simple rule-based edits can be competitive when regularization is the primary goal. In structured prediction, preserving span alignment and label schema consistency is often paramount, which motivates constrained generators and alignment-aware mixing. Interpolation in representation space can mitigate sample scarcity while maintaining label compatibility under convexity assumptions. Model-based generators can be guided by uncertainty estimates to focus synthesis on underrepresented regions.

The present work focuses on reliability under distribution shift and controllability of augmentation targets. We consider settings where naive transformations can induce confounding correlations that degrade calibration. We therefore examine augmentation strategies with explicit semantic fidelity checks and coverage guarantees. Our method integrates a selection mechanism that prioritizes difficult but label-consistent augmentations. We also analyze sensitivity to augmentation scale and demonstrate stable gains without extensive hyperparameter tuning. Extensive ablations isolate the contributions of selection, constraint enforcement, and diversity control.

In contrast to prior DA systems that optimize a single objective, we introduce a multi-criterion framework balancing fidelity, diversity, and hardness. We provide theoretical insight into when interpolation preserves decision margins for linear and margin-based classifiers. Empirically, we show improvements across sentiment analysis, natural language inference, and slot filling under covariate and label shift. Our evaluation protocol includes out-of-domain test sets and calibration metrics, highlighting robustness rather than in-distribution accuracy alone. We release code and datasets to facilitate reproducibility and further study.

Unsupervised POS tagging. In addition to HMMs and the CRF-AE, other approaches for unsupervised POS tagging are as follows.

(1) Clustering. The clustering approach, as a mainstream unsupervised learning technique, is also investigated for unsupervised POS tagging Yatbaz et al. (2012); Yuret et al. (2014); Gupta et al. (2020). All three works adopt k-means algorithm to divide word tokens into different groups. The difference among them is how to represent words. Yatbaz et al. (2012) propose to learn context-free word embeddings by minimizing the distance between each word and its substituted words. Substituted words are selected according to a n-gram language model. It is noteworthy that Yatbaz et al. ( 2012   Stratos (2019), and G'20 for Gupta et al. (2020).

than He et al. (2018). Yuret et al. (2014) extend their previous work to produce context-sensitive word embeddings. Gupta et al. (2020) adopt a deep clustering approach that uses a feed-forward neural network to transform word representations from mBERT into a lower-dimension clustering-friendly space. Transformation with reconstruction loss and clustering are jointly trained. Their model achieves much better performance on the multilingual UD data than previous works. Unfortunately, all three works have not released their source code.

(2) Mutual information maximization. Inspired by Brown et al. (1992), Stratos (2019) propose a mutual information maximization approach for unsupervised POS tagging. The idea is that we can predict POS tags in two ways (using the words themselves or their context), and predictions from these two ways should agree as more as possible.

Utilizing PLMs for unsupervised tagging or parsing. There exist few works exploiting PLMs for unsupervised POS tagging and parsing so far. The above-mentioned clustering proposed by Gupta et al. (2020) is the only work using PLMs for unsupervised POS tagging. As for unsupervised parsing, Wu et al. (2020) propose a perturbed masking technique to estimate inter-word correlations and then induce syntax trees from those correlations. Inspired by Shen et al. (2018), Kim et al. (2020) extract constituency trees from the PLMs through capturing syntactical proximity between representations of two adjacent words (or subwords). If the proximity is loose, then it is likely that the middle position of the two words corresponds to some constituent boundary. Cao et al. (2020) successfully exploit PLMs for unsupervised constituency parsing based on constituency test, achieving SOTA performance.

Recent studies on leveraging large pretrained language models for structure induction span a broad set of techniques and tasks, including masked-language-model probing for part-of-speech discovery (Peters et al., 2018;Devlin et al., 2019;Tenney et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019), attention-based syntax induction from Transformers (Clark et al., 2019;Manning et al., 2020;Hewitt and Manning, 2019;Goldberg, 2019), cross-lingual transfer of token-level categories via multilingual encoders (Pires et al., 2019;Wu and Dredze, 2019;Conneau et al., 2020;Artetxe et al., 2020), and unsupervised constituency or dependency parsing driven by representational geometry (Shen et al., 2018;Kim et al., 2020;Cao et al., 2020;Wu et al., 2020).

Utilizing CRF-AE. As mentioned above, Ammar et al. ( 2014) first proposes CRF-AE for unsupervised sequence labeling. Cai et al. (2017) apply CRF-AE to unsupervised dependency parsing. Similarly, they use the encoder to generate a most likely dependency tree and then force the decoder to reconstruct the input sentence from the tree. CRF-AE was initially designed for unsupervised learning. However, recent works show it is useful under the semi-supervised learning setting. For instance, Zhang et al. (2017) propose a neural CRF-AE for semi-supervised learning on sequence labeling problems (including POS tagging) and Jia et al. (2020) adopt a neural CRF-AE for semi-supervised semantic parsing.

Our approach draws insights from multi-label classification (MLC) (Tsoumakas and Katakis, 2007;Zhou, 2006, 2014). One of the earliest approaches for MLC was to transform the problem into multiple binary classification problems while ignoring the correlations between labels (Boutell et al., 2004). More recent work has modeled MLC in the sequence-to-sequence framework with a decoder that generates the labels sequentially, thus preserving the inter-label correlations (Yang et al., 2018). Most prior work in MLC focuses on classification and is not directly applicable to MT. In contrast, our training strategy is tailored for sequence-to-sequence problems. Unlike prior work (Yang et al., 2018), SCONES allows us to perform MLC style training with any underlying NMT architecture by simply changing the loss function. By jointly training all label-specific binary classifiers, our strategy is able to account for label correlations.  used an MLC objective to improve machine translation. Unlike our approach, they attempted to predict all words in the target sentence with a bag-of-words loss function. We formulate the next word prediction at each time step as a MLC problem to handle intrinsic uncertainty, but our models are predicting ordered target sequences, not bags of words.

Recent neural MT architectures and training strategies span a wide range of paradigms, including transformer-based sequence modeling (Vaswani et al., 2017), non-autoregressive decoding for efficiency (Gu et al., 2018; Ghazvininejad et al., 2019), minimum risk and margin-based objectives to align training with evaluation metrics (Shen et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018), and auxiliary losses such as bag-of-words or future n-gram prediction to enrich target-side supervision (Zhou et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019; Du and Ji, 2019).

The speed-ups from SCONES can be partially attributed to avoiding the normalization of the output over the full vocabulary. The same idea motivated earlier work on self-normalized training Mnih and Teh, 2012;Devlin et al., 2014;Goldberger and Melamud, 2018). As described in Sec. 2, unlike work on self-normalization, SCONES does not try to ap-proximate a distribution over the full vocabulary. Rather, its output consists of multiple binary classifiers that do not share probability mass by design to be able to better represent intrinsic uncertainty.

We focus on the most relevant subset of this literature below. Language similarity The methods developed by (Sen et al., 2019;Kong et al., 2021) use language proximity to design parameter sharing strategies. The authors propose a multi-decoder model sharing the same encoder among languages and routing languages in different families to different decoders. These approaches share the same interest in expressing the proximity between tasks in the selection of task-specific parameters as our approach. However, our method learn the selection from a latent commonality in data instead of using a predefined selection such as "One language family per decoder" in (Kong et al., 2021). Language-specific sub-networks. Frankle and Carbin (2019);  study techniques to identify the most important parameters for the current task, so that masking the less important parameters during training does not hurt performance. Lin et al. (2021) adapts this idea for multilingual NMT, trying to identify language dependent subsets of parameters by pruning a fine-tuned model.

In multidomain translation, Pham et al. (2021) recently demonstrated the brittleness of adapters against fuzzy domain separations, outof-domain distributions, and erroneous domain tags. Several subsequent studies have aimed to mitigate this weakness through a mixture of expert mechanism (e.g. (Pfeiffer et al., 2021)). Zhang et al. (2021) propose to learn to route between shared and language-specific representations with a conditional language-specific routing while training the parameters of the underlying Transformer. This method is related to the Fusion-Adapters of Pfeiffer et al. (2021). Both approaches aim to select between shared and task-specific representations. The proximity between tasks is not taken into account in the routing mechanism.

Multidomain and multilingual translation systems have received considerable attention in the recent years, and a exhaustive survey is beyond the goal of this paper. Domain adaptation for neural MT is surveyed in (Chu et al., 2017), while multidomain MT systems are notably studied in (Saunders, 2021;Pham et al., 2021); for multilingual MT, the reader is referred eg. to (Chu and Dabre, 2018;Dabre et al., 2020). We focus on the most relevant subset of this literature below.

Language similarity The methods developed by (Sen et al., 2019;Kong et al., 2021) use language proximity to design parameter sharing strategies. The authors propose a multi-decoder model sharing the same encoder among languages and routing languages in different families to different decoders. These approaches share the same interest in expressing the proximity between tasks in the selection of task-specific parameters as our approach. However, our method learn the selection from a latent commonality in data instead of using a predefined selection such as "One language family per decoder" in (Kong et al., 2021).

Language-specific sub-networks. Frankle and Carbin (2019);  study techniques to identify the most important parameters for the current task, so that masking the less important parameters during training does not hurt performance. Lin et al. (2021) adapts this idea for multilingual NMT, trying to identify language dependent subsets of parameters by pruning a fine-tuned model. Our approach also aims to map sub-networks to tasks: we do so by masking the output of each layer, rather than masking parameters. Furthermore, Lin et al. (2021) computes the masks via a heuristic selection; while our approach learns the masks with variational techniques.

Sparse Transformer The idea of adaptive sparsity is studied in several works. For instance, Li et al. (2020) propose to use a variable depth for different tasks. The authors aimed to match the depth of the sub-network to the complexity of the task. Gong et al. (2021b,a) also take an interest in the adaptive sparse Transformers, in which differ each task triggers the selection of specific heads in multihead attention, layers, and blocks in feedforward matrices. Mixture-of-experts (MoE) constitute another effective approach to achieve sparsity. Using the Transformer architecture, the GShard model replaces a single feedforward (FFN) sub-layer with an MoE module consisting of multiple FFN sublayers (Lepikhin et al., 2021;Fedus et al., 2021).

Adapter modules Adapters have proven to be very efficient for multi-task NLP (Houlsby et al., 2019;Bapna and Firat, 2019;Pham et al., 2020;Pfeiffer et al., 2020). In a nutshell, this technique consists in plugging several so-called adapter modules to the intermediate layers of a pretrained Transformer and finetuning these adapters on the downstream tasks. Adapters can also be trained without supervision for multilingual translation (Philip et al., 2020). However, the hard-coded separation between the domains of different tasks may lead to a catastrophic forgetting effect (Pfeiffer et al., 2021), which is a common problem in multi-task modeling using neural networks (McCloskey and Cohen, 1989). In multidomain translation, Pham et al. (2021) recently demonstrated the brittleness of adapters against fuzzy domain separations, outof-domain distributions, and erroneous domain tags. Several subsequent studies have aimed to mitigate this weakness through a mixture of expert mechanism (e.g. (Pfeiffer et al., 2021)). Zhang et al. (2021) propose to learn to route between shared and language-specific representations with a conditional language-specific routing while training the parameters of the underlying Transformer. This method is related to the Fusion-Adapters of Pfeiffer et al. (2021). Both approaches aim to select between shared and task-specific representations. The proximity between tasks is not taken into account in the routing mechanism. We propose a different approach to the problem of multi-task routing in the underlying network.

Introduction

Probing knowledge in pretrained language models seeks to characterize the extent to which parameters encode factual associations and how such information can be elicited at inference time. Early studies demonstrated that masked language models answer cloze prompts with nontrivial accuracy on entity–relation facts, suggesting implicit storage of world knowledge. However, questions remain about the reliability and interpretability of these behaviors across architectures, training regimes, and evaluation protocols.

Prior efforts span diverse paradigms, including cloze-style probing with masked language models (Petroni et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020b), closed-book question answering at scale (Roberts et al., 2020), consistency analyses for factual prediction (Elazar et al., 2021; Ravichander et al., 2020), causal probing and representation interventions (Vig et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2022), and evaluations on curated factual benchmarks such as LAMA, LAMA-UHN, and TREx (Petroni et al., 2019; Poerner et al., 2020; Elsahar et al., 2018).

Cloze-style prompts operationalize knowledge as the probability mass assigned to masked tokens conditioned on templated relation statements. While informative, this approach is sensitive to prompt surface form and paraphrase, motivating subsequent work on template mining, paraphrasing, and calibration. Closed-book question answering reframes the problem as parametric retrieval, decoupling inference from access to external context. Consistency-focused analyses further test whether models provide stable answers under semantically equivalent perturbations, uncovering brittleness and contradictions. Collectively, these lines of inquiry highlight both the promise and the limits of output-based probes.

Despite progress, output probing alone provides limited visibility into the internal mechanisms by which models represent and manipulate factual associations. Interpretability techniques that intervene on activations or trace information flow can complement behavioral evaluations by localizing functional substructures. Building upon this perspective, we consider whether knowledge can be attributed to sparse units whose modulation predictably alters factual predictions.

We therefore examine knowledge representations within Transformer layers using targeted interventions and circuit analysis. Our methodology identifies units whose activations are causally implicated in producing fact-specific outputs, evaluates their stability across paraphrases and contexts, and quantifies transfer across related relations. By integrating behavioral metrics with mechanistic evidence, we aim to bridge the gap between what models output and how they compute it.

Our contributions are threefold. First, we introduce a scalable procedure for discovering candidate knowledge units via gradient-guided search and validating them with controlled activation patching. Second, we design evaluation protocols that jointly assess factual accuracy, paraphrase robustness, and intervention efficacy on established benchmarks. Third, we release code and annotated unit catalogs to facilitate reproducibility and further study of knowledge localization.

We situate our approach within the broader literature on mechanistic interpretability and parametric knowledge, and we discuss implications for model editing, robustness, and continual learning. Finally, we outline limitations related to spurious localization, dataset coverage, and potential distributional artifacts, and we propose avenues for refining both probes and intervention techniques.

In this section we mainly discuss and compare related work in the two most relevant directions: the assessment benchmarks to the general narrative comprehension skills; and the tasks specifically designed for character-centered predictions over narratives. Table gives a summary of these narrative comprehension tasks, associated with their required skills of comprehension. We also reviewed studies on character-centered tasks over non-narrative texts like synopses and chit-chat (i.e., not story-related) conversations. Detailed rationales of the required skills for each task are discussed in Appendix A.

We organize the literature into three clusters that map to our evaluation axes. First, general narrative comprehension datasets emphasize story-level inference, long-range dependencies, and discourse cohesion. Second, character-centered tasks emphasize tracking entities, modeling intentions, and attributing beliefs under partial observability. Third, hybrid benchmarks combine plot understanding with fine-grained social reasoning signals. Recent narrative comprehension benchmarks span commonsense story understanding and plot inference (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016;Roemmele et al., 2011;Lai et al., 2017;Bhagavatula et al., 2020), multi-hop narrative reasoning (Yang et al., 2018;Welbl et al., 2018;Khashabi et al., 2018), temporal and causal coherence (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008;Rashkin et al., 2018;Do et al., 2011;Zhou et al., 2020), and long-form reading with discourse phenomena (Kočiský et al., 2018;Hermann et al., 2015;Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019;Zhu et al., 2015). Additional datasets consider character-centric annotations such as coreference and entity tracking (Pradhan et al., 2012;Clark and Manning, 2016;Bamman et al., 2014;Liu et al., 2019), dialogic and social dynamics in stories (Iyyer et al., 2016;Choi et al., 2014;Zhou and Wang, 2019;Sap et al., 2019), and narrative question answering or cloze tests (NarrativeQA, Kočiský et al., 2018;StoryCloze, Mostafazadeh et al., 2016;RACE, Lai et al., 2017;MCTest, Richardson et al., 2013). To situate character-centered prediction, we next review modeling approaches that interface with these benchmarks.

A central thread concerns how models encode characters and their evolving states. Early neural architectures rely on recurrent encoders with entity memory to capture cross-sentence references. More recent approaches add contrastive objectives that bind mentions across scenes while maintaining temporal order. Complementary work integrates social commonsense resources to enrich character motivations during narrative progression.

Character-centered prediction tasks further vary by supervision granularity. Some datasets annotate fine-grained spans for coreference and attribution, while others only provide end-task labels such as next-action or outcome plausibility. This variation influences metrics, with token-level F1 and B³ used for entity resolution, and ranking metrics or exact match applied to cloze-style evaluation. We briefly outline representative task formulations before turning to methodological choices.

Methodologically, two families dominate: span-centric pipelines that detect mentions then aggregate entity states, and end-to-end transformers that jointly model text and latent character graphs. The former provide interpretability via explicit clusters, whereas the latter achieve stronger performance on long-context reasoning through global attention and memory compression. Hybrid systems increasingly combine mention proposals with graph-aware transformers for scalable tracking.

Evaluation remains challenging due to narrative length, shifting focalization, and implicit causal structure. Beyond accuracy, recent works report calibration and robustness under adversarial paraphrases. Cross-domain transfer from synopses to full stories highlights brittleness in surface-form reliance. We therefore delineate dataset splits that test extrapolation to unseen character constellations and settings.

spans: Early neural and graph-based models for scientific document understanding and generation have explored citation intent classification, keyphrase extraction, and multi-document summarization without shared formulations (Cohan et al., 2018; Kardas et al., 2020; Yasunaga et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Collins et al., 2017; DeYoung et al., 2021; Fabbri et al., 2021). Work on leveraging heterogeneous citation graphs for content planning emphasizes structural signals and influence propagation (Liu et al., 2020; Sachan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2020). Pretrained language models have been adapted to scientific discourse with domain-specific corpora and objectives (Beltagy et al., 2019; Gururangan et al., 2020; Lo et al., 2020; Phang et al., 2020; Cohan et al., 2020). Prompting and controllable generation techniques have been examined for scholarly text drafting and reference-aware summarization (Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Dou et al., 2022; Goyal et al., 2022).

Concurrent efforts have cataloged datasets for citation recommendation, scientific summarization, and long-context language modeling, including SciCite, TLDR, S2ORC, arXivSumm, and LongSumm, alongside methods such as LED, Longformer, BigBird, PEGASUS, and BART (Cohan et al., 2020; Lo et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; Chandrasekaran et al., 2020; Cachola et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2022). Evaluations frequently rely on ROUGE, METEOR, and BERTScore with occasional human assessments for coherence and factuality (Lin, 2004; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005; Zhang et al., 2019). Several studies also consider content planning and discourse coherence via rhetorical structure and section-aware decoding (Li et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022; Iso et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021).

Extractive Related Work Generation. Early related work generation systems employed the extractive summarization approach. Hoang and Kan (2010) pioneered the task, developing rules to select sentences following a topic hierarchy tree that was assumed to be given as input. Hu and Wan (2014) grouped sentences into topic-biased clusters with PLSA, modeled sentence importance with SVR and applied a global optimization framework to select sentences. Chen and Zhuge (2019) se-lected sentences from papers that co-cited the same cited papers as the target paper in order to cover a minimum Steiner tree constructed from a paper's keywords. Wang et al. (2019) extracted Cited Text Spans (CTS), the matched text spans in the cited paper that are most related to a given citation. However, these extractive approaches aim to maximally cover the citation texts with the extracted sentences, thus mostly ignoring the reference type citations that are concise and abstractive ( §3.1.3).

Abstractive Related Work Generation. Recently, Xing et al. (2020) extend the pointergenerator (See et al., 2017) to take two text inputs, allowing them to recover a masked citation sentence given its neighboring context sentences. Ge et al. (2021) encode the citation context, cited paper's abstract, and citation network and train their model with multiple objectives: sentence salience score regression of the cited paper's abstract, functional role classification of the citation sentence, and citation sentence generation. Chen et al. (2021) propose a relation-aware, multi-document encoder to generate a related work paragraph given a set of cited papers. Luu et al. (2021) fine-tune GPT2 (Radford et al., 2019) on scientific texts and explore several techniques for representing documents, such as using extracted named entities. Early neural and graph-based models for scientific document understanding and generation have explored citation intent classification, keyphrase extraction, and multi-document summarization without shared formulations (Cohan et al., 2018; Kardas et al., 2020; Yasunaga et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Collins et al., 2017; DeYoung et al., 2021; Fabbri et al., 2021). Work on leveraging heterogeneous citation graphs for content planning emphasizes structural signals and influence propagation (Liu et al., 2020; Sachan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2020). Pretrained language models have been adapted to scientific discourse with domain-specific corpora and objectives (Beltagy et al., 2019; Gururangan et al., 2020; Lo et al., 2020; Phang et al., 2020; Cohan et al., 2020). Prompting and controllable generation techniques have been examined for scholarly text drafting and reference-aware summarization (Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Dou et al., 2022; Goyal et al., 2022).

All of the works described above focus on the generation aspect, while neglecting dataset collection; their datasets are mostly extracted automatically. Moreover, the datasets are not reused, though they are publicly available, because these works all use slightly different problem definitions, and thus the models are not directly comparable (Li and Ouyang, 2022). Concurrent efforts have cataloged datasets for citation recommendation, scientific summarization, and long-context language modeling, including SciCite, TLDR, S2ORC, arXivSumm, and LongSumm, alongside methods such as LED, Longformer, BigBird, PEGASUS, and BART (Cohan et al., 2020; Lo et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; Chandrasekaran et al., 2020; Cachola et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2022). Evaluations frequently rely on ROUGE, METEOR, and BERTScore with occasional human assessments for coherence and factuality (Lin, 2004; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005; Zhang et al., 2019). Several studies also consider content planning and discourse coherence via rhetorical structure and section-aware decoding (Li et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022; Iso et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). In this work, we focus on collecting a dataset that is widely applicable to various related work generation settings, rather than proposing another incomparable approach.

Introduction

Prompting has emerged as a unifying interface for steering large language models across tasks, modalities, and domains. Despite rapid progress, our understanding of why certain prompts succeed while others fail remains limited, especially when moving between discrete templates and continuous embeddings. This paper examines interpretability and transfer in prompt representations with a focus on connecting learned continuous prompts to human-readable discrete prompts.

Background on continuous prompts. There is a line of work focused on tuning continuous prompts (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). A recurring theme in this line of work is the strength of continuous prompts in producing compact models that rival or surpass task-specific fine-tuning. Continuous parameterizations are attractive because they can be optimized with gradient-based methods and amortized across multiple tasks. However, the semantics of the learned vectors are opaque and their behavior can vary across architectures and scales.

Background on discrete prompts. The release of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) sparked interest in natural language prompts that elicit capabilities without task-specific training. Subsequent studies explored manual template crafting, automatic template search, and verbalizer engineering for classification and generation (Petroni et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Le Scao and Rush, 2021). Discrete prompts are interpretable and portable across settings but are sensitive to wording and often require brittle search procedures. Prompt calibration and instruction tuning have partially mitigated these issues but raised questions about robustness and generality.

Related lines of inquiry. Recently, several complementary strands have examined prompt initialization strategies, transfer across tasks and models, and robustness interventions without converging on a single recipe for bridging prompt formats (Vu et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023; Min et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Sanh et al., 2022; Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Rubin et al., 2022; Schick and Schütze, 2021), and additional work has compared instruction-tuned checkpoints, mixture-of-experts parameterizations, and prompt ensembling in both zero-shot and few-shot regimes (Ouyang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Mishra et al., 2021a; Gao et al., 2021), alongside studies on calibration and verbalizer design for prompt-based classification and extraction tasks (Zhao et al., 2021; Holtzman et al., 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021), as well as investigations into the stability of discrete templates across domains and languages and the sensitivity of soft prompts to initialization seeds and optimizer choices (Lester et al., 2021; Scao and Rush, 2021; Logan IV et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021).

Problem statement. Despite the promise of both paradigms, there remains a mismatch between the geometry of learned continuous embeddings and the compositional structure of natural language templates. This mismatch complicates efforts to translate between representations and to reason about transfer.

Our focus. We study whether structure in continuous prompt space aligns with interpretable token-level patterns and whether such structure can be exploited for controlled editing. We further analyze cross-model portability and sensitivity to scaling when moving from base to instruction-tuned checkpoints.

Contributions. First, we formalize a mapping interface that casts discrete templates into a subspace comparable to soft prompts. Second, we introduce diagnostics that probe locality, linearity, and compositionality of prompt edits. Third, we evaluate cross-task transfer with constrained budgets and ablate initialization, optimizer, and regularization choices.

Empirical scope. We consider classification, extraction, and generative tasks with heterogeneous verbalizers and evaluate across English and multilingual settings. Our experiments span encoder-decoder and decoder-only families at multiple parameter scales. We report efficiency, stability, and robustness metrics in addition to task accuracy.

Implications. By clarifying how continuous and discrete prompts align and diverge, our analysis informs the design of prompt learning algorithms and the interpretation of their behavior. We close by discussing open challenges for bridging representation formats and for principled prompt search.

Pretrained Language Models. Owing to the great achievements Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) has made, the paradigm of pretrained language models (PLMs) is thriving (Radford et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019b;Lewis et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2020). It is widely recognized that PLMs can learn massive knowledge from corpus, leading to significant progress in various language tasks. Giving such results in extensive NLP tasks, now it has come to the point to systematically evaluate the abilities of PLMs, which can further deepen our understanding of PLMs and facilitate their application to more fields.

Language Model Evaluation. Many efforts have studied the evaluation on language model performance. Liu et al. (2019a) evaluate BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT (Radford et al., 2018), and ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) on a variety of linguistics tasks. Their results suggest that the features generated by PLMs are sufficient for high performance on a board set of tasks but fail on tasks requiring fine-grained linguistics knowledge. Tenney et al. ( 2019) evaluate similar models on a variety of sub-sentence linguistic analysis tasks, showing that PLMs encode both syntax and semantics into parameters. Zhou et al. ( 2020) is in line in the sense that PLMs can learn rich knowledge but focus on evaluating the commonsense. However, these work just focus on one dimension of PLMs ability evaluation. Other work such GLUE (Wang et al., 2019b) and CLUE (Liang Xu, 2020) just consider a simple mixture of multiple tasks lacking comprehensive evaluation. Recent studies catalog a broad spectrum of evaluation perspectives and resources for PLMs, including probing tasks for syntax and semantics (Tenney et al., 2019;Hewitt and Manning, 2019;Liu et al., 2019a), commonsense and world knowledge benchmarks (Zhou et al., 2020;Talmor et al., 2019;Sap et al., 2019), general-purpose leaderboards such as GLUE, SuperGLUE, and CLUE (Wang et al., 2019b;Wang et al., 2019a;Liang Xu, 2020), multilingual and cross-lingual suites (Conneau et al., 2018;Hu et al., 2020;Ruder et al., 2021), as well as robustness and adversarial stress tests (Ettinger, 2020;Nie et al., 2020;Ribeiro et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to systematically evaluate PLMs by defining various kinds of ability and performing extensive comparison.

Introduction

In this section, we analyze the literature on the two main evaluation problems tackled in this paper: multi-labeling and class hierarchies, keeping the focus on extreme scenarios (numerous and unbalanced classes).

Extreme multi-label classification (XMLC) poses unique evaluation challenges because tail labels dominate error profiles while head labels skew aggregate metrics. Conventional metrics such as micro-averaged precision and recall emphasize frequent labels, whereas macro-averaged scores can be unstable given sparse support for rare classes.

Prior studies have examined extreme multi-label classification with thousands to millions of labels under severe class imbalance, including one-vs-rest linear models (Babbar and Schölkopf, 2017), tree-based partitioning methods (Jain et al., 2019), embedding-based approaches such as SLEEC and AnnexML (Bhatia et al., 2015; Tagami, 2017), deep architectures like XML-CNN, AttentionXML, and XR-Transformer (Liu et al., 2017; You et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020), and benchmark datasets including Wikipedia-500K, Amazon-670K, and Amazon-3M (McAuley et al., 2015; Sharma et al., 2015; Dahiya et al., 2021).

Evaluation protocols for XMLC frequently incorporate propensity-scored metrics to counteract label-frequency bias, with propensity estimates derived from empirical label distributions or hierarchical surrogates. These adjustments aim to provide a more faithful view of performance on tail labels while remaining comparable across methods.

A related line of work studies calibration in multilabel settings, highlighting that overconfident scores on rare labels can inflate micro-averaged gains without improving decision quality. Post-hoc calibration techniques, such as temperature scaling and class-wise Platt scaling, have been adapted to the multilabel regime with mixed success on extreme datasets.

Turning to hierarchical classification, evaluation is further complicated by taxonomic relations among classes, where partial credit for near-miss predictions can better reflect semantic proximity. Metrics like hierarchical precision and loss functions weighted by ancestor distance have been proposed to capture graded correctness along paths in a DAG or tree.

However, real-world hierarchies often contain inconsistencies, missing links, or evolving structures, which undermines the reliability of fixed-path scoring. Recent work explores robustness by averaging over multiple plausible hierarchies or by learning soft taxonomies directly from data.

Cross-domain evaluations reveal that domain shift exacerbates tail-label brittleness and increases hierarchical misalignment. Methods that couple label embeddings with structure-aware regularizers tend to retain more utility under shift, although improvements vary with hierarchy depth and branching factor.

From a systems perspective, large-scale evaluation pipelines must balance metric fidelity against computational cost. Approaches based on approximate nearest neighbor search, shard-wise evaluation, and stratified sampling offer substantial speedups while preserving metric estimates within tight confidence intervals.

Prior benchmarks frequently report only aggregate metrics, making it difficult to diagnose per-stratum performance. Recent reporting standards advocate disaggregating results across head/mid/tail bins and hierarchy levels, enabling more actionable comparisons and ablation studies.

Despite progress, open questions remain on how to align evaluation with deployment goals. For decision-support applications, utility-aware metrics that weight labels by downstream cost have shown promise, but require careful elicitation from stakeholders.

Finally, reproducibility in extreme settings depends on consistent preprocessing of labels, pruning of spurious nodes in hierarchies, and transparent handling of missing or deprecated classes. Community-shared scripts that materialize canonical label spaces are a practical step toward comparable evaluations.

In summary, the literature underscores that evaluating extreme multilabel and hierarchical models demands metrics and protocols that are frequency-aware, structure-aware, and resource-conscious, motivating principled choices tailored to data and application constraints.

Fake News Detection: Early work for fake news detection uses hand-crafted features to conduct document classification (Rubin et al., 2016;Wang, 2017;Rashkin et al., 2017;Pérez-Rosas et al., 2018;Sarkar et al., 2018;Atanasova et al., 2019). Recent work uses neural network such as RNN (Karimi et al., 2018;Nasir et al., 2021) andTransformer (Zellers et al., 2019) to encode the news document.

To model the internal structure of a news document, Karimi and Tang (2019)  compares the news with external knowledge base (KB) to check for inconsistencies. However, the correlation between news and KB is not as close as the correlation between related news documents due to the incompleteness of these KBs. Other work utilizes additional information such as user engagements and behaviors on social media (Shu et al., 2019;Nguyen et al., 2020) and multi-modal features (Khattar et al., 2019;Tan et al., 2020;. However, to the best of our knowledge, no published work has considered using cross-document inference for misinformation detection.

In addition to document-level detection, the task of fine-grained detection is also important but rarely explored. The most relevant work detects fake knowledge triplets extracted from each individual news article .

Another related task is fact verification which aims to verify a statement based on retrieved evidence. Fact verification has been explored in multiple domains such as general domain (Thorne et al., 2018), climate change (Diggelmann et al., 2020) and COVID-19 (Wadden et al., 2020;Saakyan Figure : An overview of our approach. We first construct a within-document KG for each document based on IE output, where represents an entity and represents an event. Then, we construct a cross-document KG by (1) adding a node for each cross-document event cluster and connecting it with events in the cluster, and (2) introducing a document node for each document and connecting it with all entities and events in the given document. Finally, we use GNN to encode the cross-document KG, and use the event and document features to conduct misinformation detection at two levels. The two detectors are trained and deployed in a pipeline fashion, where event-level detection results are leveraged to improve document-level detection. et al., 2021). However, fact verification focuses on short single-sentence statements, and cannot model the complicated internal structure of a news document.

Fake News Datasets: The main difficulty in constructing a fake news dataset is to obtain annotations. Rashkin et al. (2017) and Rubin et al. (2016) obtain labels from the source information, and consider news from reliable sources as real news, and unreliable sources as fake news. A potential issue is that the detector may only learn to distinguish the style of different news sources, rather than the authenticity of the content. Shu et al. (2020) collects annotations from fact-checking websites, and Pérez-Rosas et al. (2018) collects annotations via crowd-sourcing. These approaches produce datasets of higher quality, but require extensive manual efforts. With the development of powerful generative models capable of mimicking humanwritten news (Zellers et al., 2019), recent work has constructed datasets by using generative models to generate fake news (Tan et al., 2020;.  further generates fake news from manipulated KG, which we follow to construct our dataset.

spans: Pretraining for information retrieval has explored diverse objectives and resources across both supervised and unsupervised regimes, including ICT for pseudo-query construction (Lee et al., 2019), Wikipedia-specific pretraining with hyperlink prediction and section-aware tasks (Chang et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021), end-to-end retriever–reader pretraining for open-domain QA (Guu et al., 2020), contrastive representation learning frameworks adapted from vision such as MoCo and SimCLR (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020), and large-scale synthetic QA generation for domain matching and coverage (Lewis et al., 2021; Oguz et al., 2021).

Dense Retrieval DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) initializes a bi-encoder model with BERT and achieves better results than earlier dense retrieval methods. RocketQA (Qu et al., 2021) exploits a trained retriever to mine hard negatives and then re-train a retriever with the mined negatives. ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021) dynamically mines hard negatives throughout training but requires periodic encoding of the entire corpus. TAS-B is a bi-encoder trained with balanced topic-aware sampling and knowledge distillation from a cross-encoder and a Col-BERT model (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), in addition to in-batch negatives. xMoCo (Yang et al., 2021) adapt MoCo (He et al., 2020), a contrastive learning algorithm that is originally proposed for image representation, to text retrieval by doubling its fast and slow encoders. Although these dense retrieval systems demonstrate effectiveness on some datasets, the BEIR benchmark (Thakur et al., 2021) highlights a main drawback of these dense retrieval systems—failure to generalize to out-of-domain data. This motivates pretraining as a solution for better domain generalization (Gururangan et al., 2020).

Pretraining for Retrieval first propose to pretrain a bi-encoder retriever with an Inverse Cloze Task (ICT), which constructs a training pair by randomly selecting a sentence from a passage as the query and leaving the rest as the document. Subsequently, several studies propose two pretraining tasks for Wikipedia and attempt to combine them with ICT and masked language modeling (MLM). Guu et al. (2020) pretrain a retriever and a reader together for end-to-end question answering (QA). Pretraining for information retrieval has explored diverse objectives and resources across both supervised and unsupervised regimes, including ICT for pseudo-query construction (Lee et al., 2019), Wikipedia-specific pretraining with hyperlink prediction and section-aware tasks (Chang et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021), end-to-end retriever–reader pretraining for open-domain QA (Guu et al., 2020), contrastive representation learning frameworks adapted from vision such as MoCo and SimCLR (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020), and large-scale synthetic QA generation for domain matching and coverage (Lewis et al., 2021; Oguz et al., 2021).

Very recently, DPR-PAQ (Oguz et al., 2021) highlight the importance of domain matching by using both synthetic and crawled QA data to pretrain and then fine-tune the model on downstream datasets for dialogue retrieval. Condenser is a new Transformer variant for MLM pretraining. It exploits an information bottleneck to facilitate learning for information aggregation. On top of that, coCondenser adds an unsupervised corpus-level contrastive loss to warm up the passage embedding space. Different from these works, LaPraDoR is the first pretrained retriever that does not require fine-tuning on a downstream dataset and can perform zero-shot retrieval.

3 Methodology

spans: Prior work spans a wide range of modeling and data perspectives in fusion, including graph-based compression and lattice methods for multi-sentence compression (Filippova, 2010; Filippova and Altun, 2013), integer linear programming for fusion and summarization (Woodsend and Lapata, 2012; Thadani and McKeown, 2013), neural encoder-decoder approaches leveraging copy and coverage mechanisms (See et al., 2017; Nallapati et al., 2016; Lebanoff et al., 2019), discourse-informed and RST-guided fusion strategies (Marsi and Krahmer, 2005; Louis et al., 2010; Hirao et al., 2013), datasets for disparate and union-style fusion (Elsner and Santhanam, 2011; Geva et al., 2019; Lebanoff et al., 2020; Bingel and Søgaard, 2016), and recent pretraining-based formulations that cast fusion as conditional generation with content planning (Zhu et al., 2021; Iso et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020; Laban et al., 2020).

The sentence fusion task deals with combining multiple sentences with overlapping content into a single summary sentence that represents the shared information across the inputs (Barzilay and McKeown, 2005;Filippova and Strube, 2008;Marsi and Krahmer, 2005;McKeown et al., 2010;Thadani and McKeown, 2013). Several other variants of sentence fusion have also been explored, such as sentence union -fusing the union of information in the input (Marsi and Krahmer, 2005). In another variant, "disparate" sentence fusion (Elsner and Santhanam, 2011;Geva et al., 2019;Lebanoff et al., 2019Lebanoff et al., , 2020, the input sentences do not exhibit considerable content overlap but are rather related in discourse. Such sentences often originate in a single document and pose a different kind of challenge to generate the right discourse structure that will fluently connect the inputs.

For pragmatic purposes, a "loose" variant of sentence intersection is desired, since redundant content is most likely salient, yet additional important but non-overlapping information may be relevant for a final summarized sentence. For this reason, our extended dataset follows the fusion as "loose" intersection approach applied by Barzilay andMcKeown (2005), McKeown et al. (2010) and Thadani and McKeown (2013). The latter comprises templates that prioritize content units repeatedly expressed across inputs while allowing the inclusion of ancillary facts when they contribute to informativeness. We construct our resources by annotating sentence sets with varying degrees of lexical overlap and by supplying references that demonstrate conservative merging with limited abstraction. We further operationalize this design by instructing annotators to avoid speculative paraphrase unless it is licensed by explicit cross-sentence entailments. Our sampling procedure targets news and encyclopedic domains to capture both event-centric and definitional fusion. Prior work spans a wide range of modeling and data perspectives in fusion, including graph-based compression and lattice methods for multi-sentence compression (Filippova, 2010; Filippova and Altun, 2013), integer linear programming for fusion and summarization (Woodsend and Lapata, 2012; Thadani and McKeown, 2013), neural encoder-decoder approaches leveraging copy and coverage mechanisms (See et al., 2017; Nallapati et al., 2016; Lebanoff et al., 2019), discourse-informed and RST-guided fusion strategies (Marsi and Krahmer, 2005; Louis et al., 2010; Hirao et al., 2013), datasets for disparate and union-style fusion (Elsner and Santhanam, 2011; Geva et al., 2019; Lebanoff et al., 2020; Bingel and Søgaard, 2016), and recent pretraining-based formulations that cast fusion as conditional generation with content planning (Zhu et al., 2021; Iso et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020; Laban et al., 2020). We also include inter-annotator agreement protocols that quantify the stability of loose-intersection references under synonymy and ordering variations.

Methodologically, we adopt a controllable generation framework that decouples selection from surface realization via a two-stage pipeline. In the first stage, a selector estimates a compact set of atomic content units derived from predicate-argument structures and coreference chains. In the second stage, a generator linearizes these units under discourse constraints learned from reference orderings. We instantiate selectors with contrastive objectives that penalize inclusion of singleton units absent corroboration across sentences. To mitigate hallucination, we regularize the generator with extractive coverage penalties and entailment-based constraints over candidate outputs. The training mixture interleaves gold references with minimally edited negative candidates created through unit perturbations. We report results across automatic and human evaluation, emphasizing factual consistency, redundancy reduction, and grammaticality.

Our experiments compare extractive and abstractive baselines, including ILP-based compressors, pointer-generator models, and pretrained sequence-to-sequence architectures fine-tuned on fusion corpora. We conduct ablations on the selector thresholding, discourse ordering priors, and the strength of entailment regularization. The evaluation suite comprises ROUGE, BERTScore, and content-unit F1 derived from semantic role labels aligned to references. Human raters judge faithfulness and coherence on a five-point Likert scale with adjudication for ties. Results indicate that selection precision is the primary driver of factuality under loose intersection. We observe complementary effects between corroboration-aware selection and discourse-aware realization in reducing redundancy. Error analysis shows that residual issues stem from appositive compression and coordination scope. Finally, we release the dataset, annotation guidelines, and code to support reproducibility and further research on controlled sentence fusion.

Vanilla Domain Adaptation. Several works employ domain adaptation to improve performance. For example, Du et al. (2020) approach the sentiment analysis task by using a BERT-based (Devlin et al., 2019)   (Klimaszewski and Andruszkiewicz, 2019), mixup synthesis training (Tang et al., 2020), and effective regularization (Vernikos et al., 2020).

Cross-Lingual Domain Adaptation. Chen et al. (2018) proposed ADAN, an architecture based on a feed-forward neural network with three main components, namely: a feature extractor, a sentiment classifier, and a language discriminator. The latter has the purpose of supporting the adversarial training setup, thus covering the scenario where the model is unable the detect whether the input language is from the source dataset or the target one. A similar cross-lingual approach is adopted by Zhang et al. (2020), who developed a system to classify entries from the target language, while only labels from the source language are provided. Keung et al. (2019) employed the usage of multilingual BERT (Pires et al., 2019) and argued that a language-adversarial task can improve the performance of zero-resource cross-lingual transfers. Moreover, training under an adversarial technique helps the Transformer model align the representations of the English inputs.

Under a Named Entity Recognition (NER) training scenario, Kim et al. (2017) use features on two levels (i.e., word and characters), together with Recurrent Neural Networks and a language discriminator used for the domain-adversarial setup. Similarly, Huang et al. (2019) use target language discriminators during the process of training models for low-resource name tagging.

Word Complexity Prediction. Gooding and Kochmar (2019) base their implementation for CWI as a sequence labeling task on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) networks, inasmuch as the context helps towards proper identification of complex tokens. The authors used 300-dimensional pretrained word embeddings as inputs for the LSTMs. Also adopting a sequence labeling approach, Finnimore et al. ( 2019) consider handcrafted features, including punctuation or syllables, that can properly identify complex structures.

The same sequence labeling approach can be applied under a plurality voting technique (Polikar, 2006), or even using an Oracle (Kuncheva et al., 2001). The Oracle functions best when applied to multiple solutions, by jointly using them to obtain a final prediction.

At the same time, Zaharia et al. (2020) explore the power of Transformer-based models (Vaswani et al., 2017) in cross-lingual environments by using different training scenarios, depending on the scarcity of the resources: zero-shot, one-shot, as well as few-shot learning.

Moreover, CWI can be also approached as a probabilistic task. For example, De Hertog and Tack (2018) introduce a series of architectures that combine deep learning features, as well as handcrafted features to solve a regression problem.

Recent advances across adjacent areas also report strong results with pre-trained encoders, task-specific fine-tuning, and data augmentation, including domain-adaptive pretraining for sentiment classification (Gururangan et al., 2020), back-translation and self-training for low-resource transfer (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2020), multilingual alignment via adversarial objectives (Conneau et al., 2018; Lample and Conneau, 2019), and consistency-based semi-supervision (Miyato et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020), as well as transformer-based sequence labeling frameworks for multilingual named entity recognition (Pires et al., 2019; Rahimi et al., 2019; Wu and Dredze, 2019) and word complexity estimation datasets and shared tasks (Paetzold and Specia, 2016; Yimam et al., 2018; Shardlow et al., 2021).

Despite these developments, cross-lingual CWI remains challenging due to domain shift, annotation sparsity, and morphology-driven variation across languages. To address this gap, we outline a unified perspective on model, data, and objective design in the next section.

This section describes prior arts in back-translation for NMT, data augmentation, and semi-supervised machine translation.

Back-translation NMT Bojar and Tamchyna (2011) firstly proposed back-translation, then Bertoldi and Federico (2009); Lambert et al. (2011) apply back translation to solve the domain adaptation problems in phrase-based NMT systems. Sennrich et al. (2016a) further extend the back translation for training NMT models integrally.

For understanding the back-translation synthetic corpus, Currey et al. (2017) use a copy of target as a pseudo source , and find that NMT model performance can still be improved under the low resource settings. Caswell et al. (2019) propose tagged back-translation to indicate to the model that the given source is synthetic. To further find an optimum back-translation corpus decoding method, Imamura et al. (2018) firstly use sampling based synthetic corpus and find such a stochastic decoding method outperforms beam search on boosting NMT model performance, and Edunov et al. (2018) broaden the investigation of a number of backtranslation generation methods for synthetic source sentences. Their contribution shows that sampling or noisy synthetic data gives a much stronger training signal. Graça et al. (2019) reformulate backtranslation in the context of optimization and clarifying to improve sampling based decoding method search space, thus proposing N best list sampling. Recently, Nguyen et al. (2020) diversify the training data by multiple forward and backward models translations and combine them with the original datasets.

Recent work extends these ideas with diverse strategies that alter either the generator, the decoding policy, or the filtering of synthetic pairs (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Edunov et al., 2018; Imamura et al., 2018; Caswell et al., 2019; Graça et al., 2019; Hoang et al., 2018; Kim and Rush, 2016; Poncelas et al., 2018; Ng et al., 2019), and multilingual and domain-adaptive variants have also been explored (Burlot and Yvon, 2018; Freitag et al., 2019; Marie et al., 2020; Currey et al., 2019), alongside filtering techniques and quality estimation for synthetic data (Junczys-Dowmunt, 2018; Fomicheva et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018; Ott et al., 2018).

Data Augmentation for NMT NMT researchers are the pioneers of data augmentation studies since back-translation is a natural type of data augmentation method. (Sennrich et al., 2016b;Norouzi et al., 2016;Zhang and Zong, 2016).

To balance the token frequency in NMT corpus, Fadaee et al. (2017) create new sentences contain low-frequency words. However, as observed by , the improvement across different translation tasks is not consistent, and they invent SwitchOut data augmentation policy. Recht et al. (2018Recht et al. ( , 2019; Werpachowski et al. (2019) also observe such an inconsistency of variance between training corpus and testing set as well as in the generation tasks Recently, Li et al. (2019) try to understand data augmentation from input sensitivity and prediction margin, thus obtaining relatively low variance in generation.

Semi-supervised Machine Translation However, as high quality bitext is always limited and costly to collect, Gulcehre et al. (2015) study methods for effectively leveraging monolingual data in NMT systems. He et al. (2016) develop a duallearning mechanism, under such a learning objective, a NMT system is able to automatically learn from unlabeled data, thus improving NMT performance iteratively. Based on iterative learning, Lample et al. (2018) investigates how to learn NMT systems when only large monolingual corpora can be used in each language.

For supervision of models, Gulcehre et al. (2017) employ the target language model hidden states into NMT decoder to further improve performance. Edunov et al. (2020) show that back-translation improves translation quality of both naturally occurring text and translationese according to professional human translators. For supervision of learning corpus, Wu et al. (2019) study both the source-side and target-side monolingual data for NMT.

Beyond architecture design, efficiency-focused work in neural IR has emphasized approximate nearest neighbor indexing for dense vectors (Johnson et al., 2019;Dou et al., 2022;Gao et al., 2021), dynamic routing or token pruning to reduce per-query computation (Kaya et al., 2019;Kim and Cho, 2021;Yu et al., 2022), quantization and product quantization for compact embeddings (Jegou et al., 2011;Mao et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2020), and mixed-precision or sparsity-inducing training to lower inference latency (Micikevicius et al., 2018;Narang et al., 2017;Lagunas et al., 2021;Frantar and Alistarh, 2023).

Neural text ranking. A number of so-called cross-attention models concatenate a query and a candidate document into a string and feed it into the model (Han et al., 2020;, which allows the attention mechanism of the model to capture interactions across query and document terms. However, deploying such models to millions or billions of documents is usually intractable due to the exorbitant computational cost. To combat this cost, other studies have explored more efficient models, e.g., dual-encoder models (Karpukhin et al., 2020;, BERT with late interaction (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), or using contextual language models to improve term weighting in traditional inverted indexes Dai and Callan, 2020;. Neural text ranking has also been explored through a variety of retriever–reader pipelines, sparse–dense hybrids, and prompt-augmented retrievers that aim to balance effectiveness and efficiency across large corpora, with studies examining dense dual-encoders trained with in-batch negatives (Karpukhin et al., 2020;Xiong et al., 2021;Hofstätter et al., 2021), late-interaction schemes for token-level matching (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020;Santhanam et al., 2021;Formal et al., 2021), sparse lexical models derived from pretrained transformers (Formal et al., 2021;Mallia et al., 2021;Zhuang and Zuccon, 2021), and retrieval-augmented generation frameworks that treat ranking as conditional text generation or scoring via language model likelihoods (Lewis et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021;Wang et al., 2022;Ni et al., 2021).

A few studies that are most closely related to this work focus on leveraging the generative nature of pretrained encoder-decoder language models. A natural practice is to directly use the likelihood of generating the query given a document to rank the documents (Zhuang and Zuccon, 2021;Zhuang et al., 2021b;Lesota et al., 2021). However, these methods mostly perform substantially worse than cross-attention ranking models. Another work (dos Santos et al., 2020) transforms the likelihood of generating the query into a discriminative loss, where an "unlikelihood" loss is introduced for negative query-document pairs. Despite relatively better performance than using vanilla maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), we found that their method still underperforms cross-attention ranking models. Our proposed method uses a combination of query generation loss and a cross-entropy loss on a specific token, which is capable of achieving comparable performance to cross-attention models.

Other work (Ju et al., 2021) uses query generation as an auxiliary task during training and shows improved performance. However, the proposed model still takes both a query and a document as input in the main ranking task and hence would be as costly as cross-attention ranking models during inference.

Efficient neural IR. Due to the excessive computational cost of inference in pretrained language models, there is a series of studies aiming to improve the efficiency.

A major trend is to distill expensive models into cheaper ones (Hinton et al., 2015;Sanh et al., 2019). Some distillation approaches have specifically focused on text ranking applications (Zhang et al., 2020;Zhuang et al., 2021a;Chen et al., 2021a;Hofstätter et al., 2021).

Another trend is to improve model efficiency by modifying the model architecture. A typical approach used by ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) and PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020) defer query-document interactions to upper layers so that part of the model can be pre-computed. Our model can be categorized into this class of models, except that the late interaction is naturally aligned with the decomposition of encoder-decoder models. This alignment allows us to better leverage knowledge learned by the model during pretraining, and can be the reason behind our stronger performance compared to ColBERT and PreTTR.

There are a couple of other efficient model structures, such as early exiting (Soldaini and Moschitti, 2020;Xin et al., 2020), Transformer-Kernel (TK) model (Hofstätter et al., 2020), and contextualized offline relevance weighting (Chen et al., 2021b). Beyond architecture design, efficiency-focused work in neural IR has emphasized approximate nearest neighbor indexing for dense vectors (Johnson et al., 2019;Dou et al., 2022;Gao et al., 2021), dynamic routing or token pruning to reduce per-query computation (Kaya et al., 2019;Kim and Cho, 2021;Yu et al., 2022), quantization and product quantization for compact embeddings (Jegou et al., 2011;Mao et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2020), and mixed-precision or sparsity-inducing training to lower inference latency (Micikevicius et al., 2018;Narang et al., 2017;Lagunas et al., 2021;Frantar and Alistarh, 2023). In terms of storage cost, Cohen et al. (2021) proposed the succinct document representation which reduces the dimension of token representation to compress document representations. These techniques are orthogonal to our study and can be combined with our work to further improve the time and storage efficiency.
