Prompt Tuning GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) demonstrates remarkable few-shot performance by prepending textual prompts before the inputs and thus help the PLM to generate desired outputs of NLP tasks directly. Motivated by this, many works have tried to improve various NLP tasks by creating manually-crafted (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b;Mishra et al., 2021) or automaticallysearched (Jiang et al., 2020;Shin et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2021) hard prompts, which are discrete tokens but not necessarily human-readable. Furthermore, soft prompts (Li and Liang, 2021;Hambardzumyan et al., 2021;Zhong et al., 2021; are proposed, which are tuneable embeddings rather than tokens in the vocabularies and can be directly trained with task-specific supervi-sion. Recent advances span manual prompt engineering and automatic prompt induction as well as continuous prompt learning, with representative efforts including PET and Pattern-Exploiting Training (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b), prompt source and example-based prompting (Mishra et al., 2021), LM-BFF and AutoPrompt-style search (Jiang et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021), and soft-prompt or prefix-based tuning variants such as Prompt Tuning, P-Tuning, and Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). demonstrate that prompt tuning (PT) method can match the performance of full-parameter fine-tuning when the PLM has billions of parameters. This suggests that PT is promising to utilize extremely large PLMs. However, the much more training time needed to reach the convergence makes PT inefficient. In this work, we show that prompt transfer can remedy, improve the effectiveness to some extent with knowledge transfer, and empirically analyze the transferability of prompts across tasks and PLMs.

Knowledge Transfer Cross-task knowledge transfer (Ruder, 2017) has been a long-standing way to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of NLP systems. In the PLM era, some works propose to tune the PLMs on intermediate tasks (Phang et al., 2018;Pruksachatkun et al., 2020;Gururangan et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2019a;Vu et al., 2020;Poth et al., 2021) before fine-tuning on specific target tasks to achieve certain benefits. Vu et al. (2020) empirically analyze the transferability between tasks in this setting.

These explorations are all for fine-tuning. Considering the potential of PT, we believe the transferability and knowledge transfer methods for PT are worth exploring. As a prior attempt,  demonstrate that PT's cross-domain transferability is stronger than fine-tuning. Similar to our work, concurrent work (Vu et al., 2021) explores the cross-task transferability of PT and improves performance with transfer initialization. Differently, we attempt to improve the efficiency of PT and further analyze what decides the prompt transferability by exploring various transferability indicators. Additionally, we also attempt cross-model transfer, which is inspired by previous cross-model knowledge transfer works such as Net2Net (Chen et al., 2016), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015) and knowledge inheritance (Qin et al., 2021).

Sentence Representation Learning. Existing works for learning sentence embeddings can be categorized into supervised (Conneau et al., 2017;Cer et al., 2018;Reimers and Gurevych, 2019;Wieting et al., 2020) and unsupervised approaches (Li  Carlsson et al., 2020;Su et al., 2021;Kim et al., 2021;Gao et al., 2021;Yan et al., 2021). Supervised approaches mostly utilize supervision from annotated natural language inference data or parallel data. By adjusting the training objective to STS tasks, unsupervised approaches are able to make use of the intrinsic semantic information embedded in the natural language text corpus, thereby eliminating the need for a costly annotation process.

In particular, contrastive learning objective (Carlsson et al., 2020;Kim et al., 2021;Gao et al., 2021;Yan et al., 2021) regularizes the embedding space by pulling positive (i.e., semantically similar) sentences closer and pushing apart negatives, showcasing great effectiveness in capturing the semantic similarity among sentences. Our approach adopts the contrastive learning framework and is built on top of the current SOTA approach (Gao et al., 2021), further pushing the frontier of STS by leveraging multimodal semantic information.

Visually Grounded Representation Learning. We revisit the literature that grounds language in vision to inform representation design. A large body of multimodal research explores aligning images and text through joint objectives and cross-modal encoders, including image–sentence embedding models based on contrastive ranking losses (Kiros et al., 2014;Frome et al., 2013;Faghri et al., 2018;Mithun et al., 2018), vision–language pretraining with region-level grounding (Lu et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020;Zhou et al., 2020), end-to-end masked multimodal transformers (Tan and Bansal, 2019;Li et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2020), and datasets that pair captions with images for representation learning (Lin et al., 2014;Young et al., 2014;Krishna et al., 2017;Sharma et al., 2018). Prior work shows that vision can enrich textual semantics for downstream tasks. We extend this line by asking how visual grounding can regularize sentence-level similarity geometry.

Recently, Tan and Bansal (2020) and Tang et al. (2021) train large scale language models with multimodal supervision from scratch, with the goal of improving general language understanding. Different from the aforementioned works, we focus on learning grounded sentence embeddings by fine-tuning pre-trained models in a contrastive learning framework. We compare against strong unimodal and multimodal baselines under identical data and compute budgets. We also analyze failure modes related to shortcut cues in captions and image biases. Our experiments demonstrate consistent improvements on standard STS benchmarks. Finally, we discuss implications for robust semantic retrieval in visually rich domains.

Knowledge Selection for Dialog. Knowledge selection can be tightly coupled with the response generator (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018) or performed separately prior to response generation. Some approaches adapted question answering models (Moghe et al., 2018;Qin et al., 2019;Wu et al., 2021) or summarization models (Meng et al., 2020a) for knowledge selection. With a pool of knowledge candidates, knowledge selection has been commonly set up as a sentence classification or ranking problem (Dinan et al., 2019;Lian et al., 2019;Kim et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020b;Zhao et al., 2020). Some work has modeled the underlying knowledge as a latent variable (Lian et al., 2019;Kim et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2020). Others have explored modeling the knowledge transition over dialog turns to improve selection accuracy (Kim et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020b;Zhan et al., 2021). In comparison, we model knowledge selection as a node selection task on the document semantic graph. Recent advances also consider leveraging structured and semi-structured resources alongside neural retrievers and encoders in dialogue and related conditional generation tasks, including retrieval-augmented generation frameworks and dense passage retrievers (Lewis et al., 2020;Karpukhin et al., 2020), sentence-level and span-level selectors trained with contrastive or margin-based objectives (Dinan et al., 2019;Zhao et al., 2020), hierarchical selectors over multi-turn contexts (Lian et al., 2019;Kim et al., 2020), and graph-enhanced selectors that incorporate entity linking or discourse coherence signals (Liu et al., 2019b;Xu et al., 2020;Zhan et al., 2021;Lee et al., 2021).

Graph-based Knowledge Sources. Knowledge graphs are popular choices for integrating knowledge into dialog systems (Liu et al., 2018;Moon et al., 2019;Xu et al., 2020;Jung et al., 2020;. However, their applicability is limited by the coverage of both entities and relations. For example in (Moon et al., 2019), for books and movies, the knowledge base only contains metadata such as title and genre, making it impossible to conduct conversation about the actual content. The closest work to ours is AKGCM (Liu et al., 2019b), which starts from an existing general knowledge graph and then augments the knowledge graph with unstructured text by performing entity linking on the sentences. In comparison, our document semantic graph is created from knowledge documents and during knowledge selection we select both sentences and concept nodes.

Application of Document Graphs. Documentlevel AMR graphs have been used for summarization (Liu et al., 2015;Dohare et al., 2018;Hardy and Vlachos, 2018;Lee et al., 2021) and document generation (Fung et al., 2021). Graphs constructed using OpenIE (Banko et al., 2008) have been applied to long-form question answering and multidocument summarization .

Neural Code Generation With the rapid development of Deep Learning (DL), some researchers attempt to use DL for code generation tasks.  proposed a neural architecture for code completion task with multi-task learning based on the architecture of Transformer-XL Dai et al. (2019) and BiLSTM (Schuster and Paliwal, 1997).  presented several ways of feeding the code structure to Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) and further improved the accuracy of the code prediction (next token prediction) task. Wei et al. (2019) adopted an encoder-decoder architecture and utilized the relations between code generation and code summarization to improve the performance of both tasks.  proposed a retrieve-and-edit framework for seq2seq model on code autocomplete task.

Benefiting from the strong power of pre-training techniques in natural language processing (NLP), such as GPT (Radford et al., 2018), BART , and T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), some recent works attempt to pre-train language models on the corpus of source code for generation. Svyatkovskiy et al. (2020) proposed CodeGPT follows the architecture of GPT-2 , which is pre-trained with a causal language modeling (CLM) objective on large-scale source codes.  proposed CugLM, which adopts the same architecture as CodeGPT, and is pretrained by Masked bidirectional Language Modeling (MLM), Next Code segment Predicting (NCP), and Unidirectional Language Modeling (ULM) objectives.  proposed PLBART follows the architecture of BART , which is pre-trained on large-scale Java and Python functions paired with natural language comments via denoising autoencoding. Wang et al. (2021b) proposed CodeT5 based on the T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) architecture, which is employs denoising sequence-to-sequence pre-training on multiple programming languages.

Reinforced Text Generation Reinforcement learning (Sutton and Barto, 1998) has shown great success in various tasks. It focuses on how agents ought to take actions in an environment to maximize the cumulative reward, is well suited for decision-making tasks. Ranzato et al. (2016) were among the first to apply REINFORCE algorithm (Williams, 2004) to train recurrent neural networks on sequence generation tasks, suggesting that directly optimizing the metric used at the test phase can lead to better results. Chen and Bansal (2018) proposed a hybrid extractive-abstractive architecture with policy-based reinforcement learning. They used an extractor agent to select salient sentences and then employed an abstractor network to rewrite these extracted sentences. Wan et al. (2018) incorporated the tree structure and sequential content of code snippets and designed a deep reinforcement learning framework optimized by the metric of BLEU to improve the performance of the code summarization task. Yao et al. (2019) proposed a reinforcement learning framework, which encourages the code annotation model to generate annotations that can be used for code retrieval tasks. Korbak et al. (2021) proposed an energy-based model with an imposed constraint of generating only compilable sequences to improve compilation rates of generated codes.

Introduction

The correctness of summarization systems' outputs has long been intertwined with content selection, where measures such as the Pyramid method assess alignment with salient source content units (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004). However, neural abstractive summarizers have altered the error profile of generated summaries, amplifying concerns about factual consistency with the source (Kryscinski et al., 2020). A large-scale human evaluation across multiple architectures reported that hallucinations are common and diverse, ranging from unsupported claims to entity conflations (Maynez et al., 2020).

Despite these findings, measuring factuality remains challenging due to the interplay between faithfulness, salience, and linguistic variability. Early automatic metrics that correlate with content coverage do not sufficiently capture fine-grained factual errors that require source-grounded verification (Pagnoni et al., 2021). As a result, community attention has shifted toward evaluation protocols that explicitly target factual agreement with the source document.

Recent efforts span automatic factuality scoring and correction with entailment-style verifiers and QA-based checks (Zhou et al., 2021; Kryscinski et al., 2020; Maynez et al., 2020; Durmus et al., 2020), claim-level and entity-level evaluation frameworks (Goyal and Durrett, 2021; Laban et al., 2022; Pagnoni et al., 2021; Nan et al., 2021), datasets targeting hallucination analysis and benchmark protocols (FRANK; FactCC; QAGS; SummEval), and decoding-time or training-time interventions such as constrained decoding, faithfulness-aware losses, and retrieval-augmented summarization (Keskar et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2022).

At the same time, system-level mitigation strategies have expanded beyond post-hoc verification to include architectural and objective modifications. Source-conditioned decoding heuristics attempt to bound generations to source-supported spans while calibration techniques temper overconfident predictions. Retrieval-augmented generators can decrease unsupported synthesis by grounding token probabilities in retrieved passages.

Yet, even with specialized evaluators, agreement across metrics and with humans remains imperfect. Entailment models may over-penalize paraphrases, while QA-based metrics depend on question coverage and span extractability. This discrepancy motivates a more nuanced view of factuality that separates atomic claim verification from discourse-level coherence.

From a data perspective, the prevalence of loosely aligned references in summarization corpora can induce spurious correlations that reinforce model hallucinations. Noisy alignment between source and reference encourages models to learn shortcuts that ignore grounding constraints. Increasing annotation granularity and coupling references with verified claim sets are promising directions to address this issue.

Practical constraints also shape the adoption of factuality methods in real applications. Newsrooms and scientific curation pipelines demand predictable latency, transparency, and actionable error signals. Lightweight verifiers and selective regeneration policies can be more deployable than monolithic end-to-end retraining.

Finally, the evaluation ecosystem is evolving toward multi-perspective benchmarks that integrate faithfulness, usefulness, and calibration. As summarization expands to multi-document, multi-modal, and interactive settings, factuality assessment must remain robust under distribution shift. This landscape sets the stage for approaches that connect verification signals with controllable generation to reduce hallucinations while preserving abstraction.

Learning from instructions. There is recent literature on the extent to which models follow language instructions (Hase and Bansal, 2021;Ye and Ren, 2021;Gupta et al., 2021;Zhong et al., 2021). For example, Efrat and Levy (2020) examine if language models can follow crowdsourcing instructions with no further training. On the contrary, our work is pursuing a fundamentally different goal: creating a dataset of crowdsourcing instructions and task instances and formulating cross-task generalization by training models on seen tasks and measuring generalization to the remaining unseen ones. Weller et al. (2020) construct a crowdsourced dataset with short question-like task descriptions. Compared to this work, our instructions are longer, more complex and natural since they were used to collect datasets through crowdsourcing.

PromptSource and FLAN (Wei et al., 2021;Sanh et al., 2021) are two concurrent works that pursue a similar goal as ours. A key difference between our work to these works is in terms of data collection strategy. Our work uses natural instructions created by NLP researchers before the dataset instances were created by crowd workers, and hence it contains the complete definition of each task (definition, things to avoid, negative examples, etc.). On the other hand, instructions in the concurrent work are collected retroactively based on the alreadyavailable task instances. Our natural instructions enable evaluating models on how they learn tasks given different elements of task descriptions. (See §A.5 for further comparisons.) Nevertheless, we believe that all these approaches to constructing instructions and task categories are complementary and the community will benefit from considering both towards solving the challenging problem of cross-task generalization.

Prompt engineering. Constructing effective discrete prompts for language models to perform NLP tasks is an active area of research (Schick and Schütze, 2020;Reynolds and McDonell, 2021;Liu et al., 2021). Such prompts are often extremely short and may not include a complete definition of complex tasks. In contrast, our instructions encode detailed instructions as they were used to collect the datasets. Moreover, the goals are different: Most prompt-engineering approaches seek prompts with higher performance on a particular task, typically through assumptions about their target task which make them non-trivial to generalize to any other task. However, our introduced meta dataset enables the measurement of generalization to unseen tasks.

Beyond standard multi-task learning. Multitask learning is a long-standing goal for AI (Caruana, 1997) and has led to successful models that can support a wider range of tasks (McCann et al., 2018;Khashabi et al., 2020;Aghajanyan et al., 2021;Ye et al., 2021;Raffel et al., 2020). Recent efforts on instruction tuning and task collections span large-scale mixtures and prompt libraries, including T0 and FLAN (Sanh et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2021), BIG-bench (Srivastava et al., 2022), Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022), HELM (Liang et al., 2022), instruction induction methods (Honovich et al., 2022), and prompt repositories such as PromptSource and PromptBench (Bach et al., 2022;Liu et al., 2023), alongside studies on in-context learning and meta-learning with task descriptions (Brown et al., 2020;Min et al., 2022;Xie et al., 2022). Most of the conventional setups in the multi-tasking literature evaluate on instances that belong to the tasks that are seen, i.e., their labeled instances were observed during training (1st column of Table ). We augment this setup by introducing natural language instructions which enable our models to bridge to tasks that were not seen during training.

Relation extraction (RE) can be categorized by its granularity, such as sentence-level (Doddington et al., 2004;Xu et al., 2016; and document-level (Gupta et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2019). Early work mainly focuses on sentencelevel relation extraction. Pantel and Pennacchiotti (2006) propose a rule-based approach, and Mintz et al. (2009) design features for classifying relations. In the past several years, neural networks have become a prevailing approach for relation extraction (Xu et al., 2015;Song et al., 2019).

Document-level relation extraction (DocRE) is attracting increasing attention in the community, as it considers the interactions across entity mentions expressed in different sentences Yao et al., 2019). Compared with the sentence level, DocRE requires the model collecting and integrating inter-sentence information effectively. Recent efforts design sequence-based and graphbased models to address such a problem.

Sequence-based DocRE models encode a document by the sequence of words and/or sentences, for example, using the Transformer architecture (Devlin et al., 2019). Zhou et al. (2021) argue that the Transformer attentions are able to extract useful contextual features across sentences for DocRE, and they adopt an adaptive threshold for each entity pair. Zhang et al. (2021) model DocRE as a semantic segmentation task and predict an entity-level relation matrix to capture local and global information.

Graph-based DocRE models abstract a document by graphical structures. For example, a node can be a sentence, a mention, and/or an entity; their co-occurrence is modeled by an edge. Then graph neural networks are applied to aggregate inter-sentence information (Quirk and Poon, 2017;Christopoulou et al., 2019;Zeng et al., 2020). Zeng et al. (2020) construct double graphs, applying graph neural networks to mention-document graphs and performing path reasoning over entity graphs. Xu et al. (2021a) explicitly incorporate logical reasoning, commonsense reasoning, and coreference reasoning into DocRE, based on both sequence and graph features.

Recent DocRE studies have introduced specialized architectures and training regimes spanning heterogeneous supervision and multi-hop reasoning, including HeterGAT (Tan et al., 2022), ATLOP (Zhou et al., 2021), SSAN (Xu et al., 2021b), DocuNet (Zhang et al., 2021), GAIN (Zeng et al., 2020), GLRE (Wang et al., 2020), LSR (Nan et al., 2020), SciREX (Luan et al., 2018), DocRED (Yao et al., 2019), and DWIE (Zaporojets et al., 2021), alongside inference mechanisms based on path enumeration (Sorokin and Gurevych, 2017), latent evidence modeling (Eberts and Ulges, 2021), and document-level attention pooling (Wadden et al., 2019).

Different from previous work, our paper proposes SIEF as a general framework that can be combined with various sequence-based and graph-based DocRE models. In our approach, we propose a sentence importance score and a sentence focusing loss to encourage the model to focus on evidence sentences, improving the robustness and the overall performance of DocRE models.

Detection of adversarial examples is a wellexplored field in the image domain. Earlier works have tackled in various ways such as input transformation (Xu et al., 2018), statistical analysis (Grosse et al., 2017), or training a separate binary classifier (Metzen et al., 2017). However, Carlini and Wagner (2017) has shown that an adversary with par- tial knowledge of the detector can easily nullify it. Meanwhile, early works in novelty detection have shown that a generative model can detect anomaly samples (Bishop, 1994). Following this line of research,  have proposed a method to detect out-of-distribution samples by using features of a neural network for maximum likelihood estimation.

In the NLP domain, few efforts have been made in detecting word-level adversarial examples. Zhou et al. (2019, DISP) utilize a detector trained on adversarial samples for a joint detect-defense system. FGWS (Mozes et al., 2021) outperforms DISP in detection by building on the observation that attacked samples are composed of rare words. FGWS experiements on 2 proposed attack methods. Le et al. (2021) tackle a particular attack method called UniTrigger (Wallace et al., 2019), which pre-pends or appends an identical phrase in all sentences. While the performance is impressive, applying this method to other attacks requires significant adjustment due to the distinct characteristics of UniTrigger. Meanwhile, Pruthi et al. (2019)

Meanwhile, related research on textual robustness and adversarial detection surveys and methods spans HotFlip (Ebrahimi et al., 2018), TextFooler (Jin et al., 2020), BERT-Attack (Li et al., 2020), DeepWordBug (Gao et al., 2018), checklist-style perturbation testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020), universal adversarial triggers (Wallace et al., 2019), certified robustness approaches (Jia et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019), and robust pretraining objectives (Si et al., 2021), as well as out-of-distribution and confidence calibration baselines like ODIN (Liang et al., 2018), Energy-based OOD detection (Liu et al., 2020), and Mahalanobis distance detectors (Lee et al., 2018). This body of work highlights the diversity of attack and defense paradigms pertinent to text classifiers. However, there remains a practical need for detectors that operate without access to adversarial training data and that generalize across attack families. We therefore focus on detection signals that are intrinsic to token distributions and model-internal activations, rather than attack-specific cues. Concretely, we study whether lexical rarity, subword fragmentation, and activation consistency across layers can expose imperceptible manipulations. We also examine the interaction between frequency-based indicators and confidence calibration under distribution shift. To this end, we assemble a benchmark spanning synonym substitution, character-level noise, paraphrastic rewriting, and trigger-based attacks across sentiment and natural language inference tasks. Our evaluation isolates the contribution of token-level features versus representation-level metrics using ablations. We further analyze threshold stability under varying base model sizes and decoding settings. Finally, we report cross-attack generalization and data-efficiency results to assess deployment viability.

spans: Beyond token-level substitution, document- and sentence-level augmentation has also been explored across diverse architectures and tasks, including paraphrase generation with back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018), sequence-level perturbations via mixup and manifold mixup in NLP settings (Zhang et al., 2018; Verma et al., 2019), masked-span infilling for style-agnostic rewriting (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020), and contrastive learning with dropout or view-based augmentations for representation robustness (Fang et al., 2020; Giorgi et al., 2021; Carlsson et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2021).

Various NLP data augmentation techniques have been proposed and they are mainly divided into two categories: one is to modify raw input directly, and the other interferes with the embedding (Miyato et al., 2016;. The most commonly used method to modify the raw input is the token replacement: randomly select tokens in a sentence and replace them with semantically similar tokens to synthesize a new sentence. (Wei and Zou, 2019) directly uses the synonym table WordNet(Miller, 1998) for replacement. (Kobayashi, 2018) proposes contextual augmentation to predict the probability distribution of replacement tokens with two causal language models.  extends contextual augmentation with BERT's (Devlin et al., 2018) masked language modeling (MLM) to consider bi-directional context. (Gao et al., 2019) softly augments a randomly chosen token in a sentence by replacing its one-hot representation with the distribution of the vocabulary provided by the causal language model in machine translation. Unlike (Gao et al., 2019), we use MLM to generate smoothed representation, which considers the deep bi-directional context more adequately. And our method has better parallelism, which can efficiently obtain the smoothed representation of the entire sentence in one forward process. Moreover, we propose to constrain smoothed representation more controllable through interpolation for classification tasks.

Beyond token-level substitution, document- and sentence-level augmentation has also been explored across diverse architectures and tasks, including paraphrase generation with back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018), sequence-level perturbations via mixup and manifold mixup in NLP settings (Zhang et al., 2018; Verma et al., 2019), masked-span infilling for style-agnostic rewriting (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020), and contrastive learning with dropout or view-based augmentations for representation robustness (Fang et al., 2020; Giorgi et al., 2021; Carlsson et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2021). These studies broaden the design space from lexical changes to distributional and structural variations that may reduce overfitting in low-resource regimes. However, most prior approaches face a tension between semantic fidelity and diversity when perturbing inputs or intermediate features. In addition, methods that rely on autoregressive proposals can suffer from exposure bias or cascading errors during multi-token edits. Embedding-space perturbations, while label-preserving in expectation, may drift if not aligned with task-relevant subspaces. These challenges motivate approaches that incorporate stronger contextual constraints while maintaining efficiency.

3 Our Method

Introduction

In this part, we first present a formal definition of the few shot slot tagging task in Section 2.1, followed by an introduction of the conventional sequence labeling approaches in Section 2.2 and Sequence Labeling with Prompts in Section 2.3.
Few-shot slot tagging aims to identify semantic spans under a constrained annotation budget, where a model must generalize from only a handful of labeled examples per intent and slot type.
We focus on the setting where a base ontology is partially observed and new domains appear with limited supervision, requiring robust inductive biases.
Conventional methods rely on conditional random fields (CRFs) or neural CRFs atop contextual encoders to model label dependencies and enforce valid tag transitions.
Self-attentive encoders such as BiLSTM-CRF and Transformer-CRF variants have demonstrated strong performance when sufficient labeled data are available.
However, these approaches degrade in the few-shot regime due to sparse coverage of slot realizations and domain-specific surface forms.
Representation transfer with pre-trained language models mitigates this issue by providing contextual priors that capture cross-domain regularities.
Prior work on few-shot sequence labeling has surveyed meta-learning strategies such as MAML (Finn et al., 2017), Prototypical Networks (Snell et al., 2017), Matching Networks (Vinyals et al., 2016), and Relation Networks (Sung et al., 2018), data augmentation via back-translation and paraphrasing (Sennrich et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2020), transfer from multilingual encoders like mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), span classification and tagging with pointer networks (Zhang et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2021), and prompt-based adapters including LM-BFF, P-Tuning, and Prefix-Tuning (Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021).
Despite progress, a central challenge remains the alignment between label semantics and input spans when slot definitions are terse or ambiguous.
This motivates formulations that inject label descriptions, examples, or definitions into the model input to reduce semantic drift.
Sequence labeling with prompts reframes tagging as conditional text completion where label tokens or verbalizers steer the decoder or masked language model toward consistent predictions.
Verbalizers map slot labels to words or phrases that are likely under the model’s pre-training distribution, thereby connecting label space and token space.
When combined with calibration or ensembling, prompt-based inference can stabilize predictions across domains with varying label priors.
Nevertheless, prompt design is sensitive to template wording, verbalizer selection, and the interaction between label markers and tokenization.
To reduce these sensitivities, recent work explores automatic template search and continuous prompt embeddings that are optimized with supervision.
We also consider the interplay between span boundary detection and label realization, as prompts can bias models toward headwords rather than full multi-token spans.
For evaluation, we adopt benchmarks that include new domains with disjoint slot ontologies and report both span-level and token-level metrics.
We further analyze generalization under label-space shift, measuring performance when unseen slots share partial lexical overlap with seen ones.
Our analysis includes ablations on the number of support examples per slot and the degree of paraphrastic variation in the support set.
We compare against strong CRF baselines, nearest-neighbor prototype methods, and prompt-tuned encoders that differ in how they integrate label semantics.
To assess robustness, we stress-test under synonym substitution, domain-specific jargon, and noisy transcriptions common in voice assistants.
Finally, we discuss practical considerations for deployment, including latency implications of multiple verbalizers and the memory footprint of support-conditioned encoders.

spans: Recent studies survey textual and multimodal explanation techniques that attribute importance to tokens, spans, or regions and introduce perturbation- and gradient-based methods across tasks such as sentiment analysis, natural language inference, and vision-language reasoning (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017; Shrikumar et al., 2017; Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018; Jain and Wallace, 2019; Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019; Serrano and Smith, 2019), while separate lines examine stability, faithfulness, and human factors through perturbation tests, randomization controls, and user studies in both classification and sequence modeling (Adebayo et al., 2018; Wallace et al., 2019; Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020; Pruthi et al., 2020; Slack et al., 2020; Agarwal et al., 2022).

Parallel efforts in data-centric counterfactuals cover causal data augmentation, factual-contrastive training, and evaluation via targeted edits across tasks including question answering, summarization, and dialogue, drawing on benchmarks and toolkits that operationalize minimal changes and contrast sets for systematic stress testing (Gardner et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2020; Ribeiro et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2021; Ross et al., 2021; Gardner et al., 2021; Uttamchandani et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022).
document: There has been a great deal of recent interest in providing explanations of blackbox machine learning models, focusing on explaining why the model makes an individual prediction (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Lei et al., 2016;Ribeiro et al., 2018;Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018;Liu et al., 2018), or achieving better understanding of the limitations of models (Wallace et al., 2019;Ribeiro et al., 2020). Recent studies survey textual and multimodal explanation techniques that attribute importance to tokens, spans, or regions and introduce perturbation- and gradient-based methods across tasks such as sentiment analysis, natural language inference, and vision-language reasoning (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017; Shrikumar et al., 2017; Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018; Jain and Wallace, 2019; Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019; Serrano and Smith, 2019), while separate lines examine stability, faithfulness, and human factors through perturbation tests, randomization controls, and user studies in both classification and sequence modeling (Adebayo et al., 2018; Wallace et al., 2019; Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020; Pruthi et al., 2020; Slack et al., 2020; Agarwal et al., 2022). In contrast, our goal is to explain how the input can be changed to achieve a desired outcome, which is called a counterfactual explanation (Wachter et al., 2017;Ustun et al., 2019). Counterfactual explanations in NLP seek minimally edited inputs that flip a model decision while satisfying fluency and plausibility constraints, often formalized through sparse edit sets, semantic similarity bounds, and classifier-consistent constraints. Foundational work in vision and tabular domains has inspired NLP-specific formulations that trade off edit costs and classifier responses using differentiable surrogates or combinatorial search. Current approaches in text differ in whether they operate in the input space of tokens and characters, the latent space of encoders, or the discrete space of structured outputs for tasks like parsing and translation. Methods such as gradient-guided token replacement, masked-language-model proposals, and constrained beam search have been adapted to maintain grammaticality while pursuing decision flips. However, guarantees of faithfulness, robustness to rephrasing, and user comprehensibility remain open challenges. There has been work on leveraging natural language descriptions to help generate counterfactual explanations for image classifiers (Hendricks et al., 2018), but not tailored at counterfactual predictions for natural language tasks. Recent work has also connected counterfactual explanations to causal reasoning, framing edits as interventions and outcomes as potential responses under do-operations, which motivates evaluation protocols that measure counterfactual validity across perturbation neighborhoods. Parallel efforts in data-centric counterfactuals cover causal data augmentation, factual-contrastive training, and evaluation via targeted edits across tasks including question answering, summarization, and dialogue, drawing on benchmarks and toolkits that operationalize minimal changes and contrast sets for systematic stress testing (Gardner et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2020; Ribeiro et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2021; Ross et al., 2021; Gardner et al., 2021; Uttamchandani et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). Finally, there has been interest in improving the performance of semantic parsers through interaction (Wang et al., 2016(Wang et al., , 2017; our approach is complementary to this line of work, since it aims to make the system more transparent to the user. To situate our contribution, we formalize counterfactual generation as a constrained optimization that respects linguistic well-formedness and domain validity while minimizing edit distance. We extend recent constrained decoding techniques with task-specific semantic constraints learned from weak supervision. We evaluate on intent classification and semantic parsing benchmarks where small edits can yield large changes in predicted structure. Our experiments test whether counterfactuals transfer across model families and whether human-verified edits retain utility under model retraining. We report both flip rate and plausibility metrics, and we conduct user studies to assess interpretability. We further analyze distributional shifts induced by counterfactual augmentation and their effects on calibration. Our results highlight the interaction between constraint strength and edit minimality. We release code and annotation guidelines to facilitate reproducibility and future comparisons. We conclude by discussing open questions around interactive counterfactual generation and personalized constraints.

Introduction

Transfer learning with large pretrained language models has catalyzed substantial gains across diverse NLP tasks. Yet practitioners still face a practical choice between multi-task learning (MTL) and sequential transfer via intermediate labeled tasks (STILTs). While both strategies promise gains over naïve fine-tuning, their relative benefits depend on factors that remain poorly characterized in a task- and data-dependent manner. This paper asks a simple operational question: when should one choose MTL versus STILTs for a new target task under realistic constraints.

Background and motivation

A large body of recent work (Søgaard and Bingel, 2017;Vu et al., 2020;Bettgenhäuser et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Poth et al., 2021) exists that examines when these transfer learning methods are more effective than simply fine-tuning on the target task. Oftentimes, these explanations involve recognizing catastrophic forgetting (Phang et al., 2018;Pruksachatkun et al., 2020b;Wang et al., 2018a) although recent work has called for them to be reexamined (Chang and Lu, 2021). This paper is orthogonal to those, as we examine when you should choose MTL or STILTs, rather than when they are more effective than the standard fine-tuning case (in fact, these strategies could be combined to predict transfer and then use the best method). As our task is different, theoretical explanations for how these methods work in relation to each other will need to be explored in future work. Potential theories suggested by our results are discussed in Appendix C, and are left to guide those efforts.

Related work

Prior explorations of multi-task learning, intermediate-task transfer, and curriculum-style pretraining span diverse settings and objectives, including adversarial or uncertainty-based task weighting (Kendall et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2018), gradient surgery and conflict mitigation (Yu et al., 2020; Sener and Koltun, 2018; Chen et al., 2020), task grouping and hierarchical transfer (Ruder et al., 2019; Standley et al., 2020), linguistic-probing or syntax-informed auxiliaries (Tenney et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019), and intermediate-task pipelines such as STILTs, multitask-to-single-task adaptation, and adapter-based transfer (Phang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). In parallel, data-centric perspectives have examined how label space structure, class imbalance, and annotation noise impact transfer behavior across tasks. Complementary lines assess domain shift sensitivity, robustness to spurious correlations, and calibration under distributional drift. Methodologically, scaling laws and compute-aware evaluations have begun to quantify when additional auxiliary tasks yield diminishing returns. Several benchmarks aggregate heterogeneous tasks to probe negative transfer, interference, and sample efficiency under constrained budgets.

Problem formulation

We consider a practitioner who must select between MTL and STILTs given limited compute, limited target labels, and a pool of candidate auxiliary tasks. We operationalize the decision as predicting which strategy maximizes validation performance at a fixed budget. Our analysis decomposes the choice into measurable properties of the target task, the auxiliary pool, and the model scale. We focus on encoder-only transformer backbones but anticipate similar patterns for encoder–decoder models.

Contributions

We provide a simple decision framework grounded in budgeted validation performance that predicts the preferable strategy under observed task–auxiliary statistics. We instantiate diagnostics for auxiliary–target affinity, interference risk, and representation reuse that are computable before full training. We offer actionable guidance on when to allocate compute to breadth via MTL or depth via STILTs in low-resource regimes. We release code and metadata to facilitate reproducibility and to enable downstream meta-learning extensions.

Vision-and-Language Navigation.  proposed the first Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) benchmark combining real imagery (Chang et al., 2017) and natural language navigation instructions. To solve this task,  proposed a novel SERL model to learn reward functions from the expert distribution. And combining imitation learning and reinforcement learning (Wang et al., 2019) has been proved to be beneficial for VLN. Since the VLN dataset is relatively small-scale, some works propose augmentation approaches (Fried et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2021a) to improve robustness. Auxiliary losses (Majumdar et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2020) is used to take advantage of the additional training signals derived from the semantic information. Some pretraining methods Hao et al., 2020) have been proposed to learn generic cross-modal representations. This is further extended to a recurrent model that significantly improves sequential ac-tion prediction (Hong et al., 2021). However, the limited number of environments in pretraining constrain the generalization ability to unseen scenarios. Most related to this work, VLN-BERT (Majumdar et al., 2020) transfers knowledge from abundant, but out-of-domain image-text data to improve path-instruction matching. In contrast, we not only propose an effective method to build an in-domain dataset by sampling trajectory and generating instructions with templates, but also present a promptbased pretraining strategy to improve VLN.

Vision-and-Language Pretraining. Vision-andlanguage pretraining has made great progress in recent years. Inspired by BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), much work has extended it to process visual tokens and pretrain on large-scale image-text pairs for learning generic visio-linguistic representations. Previous research introduces one-stream BERT models and two-stream BERT models. The former directly perform inter-modal grounding (Li et al., 2019;Su et al., 2019;Alberti et al., 2019;Li et al., 2020a;Zhou et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020b), while two-stream models process both visual and textual inputs in separate streams, and then fuse the two modalities in a later stage . These models are often pretrained with self-supervised objectives akin to those in BERT: masked language modeling, masked object classification, and sentence-image alignment. In this work, the architecture of the ProbES model is structural similar to ViLBERT . We make several VLN-specific adaptations to ViLBERT so that pretrained weights can be transferred to initialize large portions of the model. Different from VLN-BERT which finetunes a ViLBERT on instruction-trajectory pairs to measure their compatibility in beam search setting, we introduce prompt tuning, which only tunes the continuous prompts.

Prompting. Natural language prompting freezes pretrained models and reformats the natural language input with example prompts. GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) introduces in-context learning, using manually designed and discrete text prompts. Sun et al. (Sun and Lai, 2020) also leverage prompts as keywords to control the sentiment or topic of the generated sentence. AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020) searches for a sequence of discrete trigger words and concatenates it with each input to elicit sentiment or factual knowledge from a masked LM. Different from the discrete text prompt, some methods examine continuous prompts (a.k.a. soft prompts) that perform prompting directly in the embedding space of the model. Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) prepends a sequence of continuous task-specific vectors as virtual tokens to the input. Following early progress on prompt-based tuning, numerous studies explore discrete and continuous prompting across diverse architectures and tasks, including GPT-3 style in-context prompting (Brown et al., 2020), AutoPrompt for eliciting factual and sentiment knowledge (Shin et al., 2020), prefix-based continuous conditioning (Li and Liang, 2021), prompt ensembling and calibration (Zhong et al., 2021), gradient-based soft prompt initialization (Qin and Eisner, 2021), reparameterized prompt tuning for stability (Hambardzumyan et al., 2021), P-tuning with deep virtual tokens (Liu et al., 2021c), and compositional template assembly with logic rules (Han et al., 2021). Ptuning (Liu et al., 2021c) uses continuous prompts which are learned by inserting trainable variables into the embedded input. Ptr (Han et al., 2021) adopts manually crafted sub-templates and generates complete templates by logic rules. In ProbES, we prepend continuous task-specific vectors to the embedding of the input instruction and directly tune the embeddings of these vectors. After prompt tuning, the model can be adapted to VLN and REVERIE tasks.

spans: Early work on visual narrative generation studies a range of modeling strategies spanning sequence-to-sequence encoders with attention, hierarchical decoders for multi-sentence outputs, and multimodal fusion of CNN and RNN features, with notable systems including AREL (Wang et al., 2018a), GLAC Net (Kim et al., 2018), KG-Story (Hsu et al., 2020), and XE-SS (Huang et al., 2019), alongside pretraining on COCO Captions (Chen et al., 2015), Flickr30k (Young et al., 2014), and visual-semantic alignment objectives (Kiros et al., 2014).

Beyond VIST, automatic evaluation in open-ended generation spans machine translation metrics such as BLEU and chrF (Popović, 2015), summarization metrics like ROUGE and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019), dialogue metrics including ADEM (Lowe et al., 2017) and USR (Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020), learned quality estimators such as COMET (Rei et al., 2020) and BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020), and reference-free variants including MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019), BaryScore (Colombo et al., 2021), and QuestEval (Scialom et al., 2021).

Visual Storytelling (VIST) Visual storytelling was introduced by Huang et al. (2016) as the task of generating a coherent story given five images. They provided a dataset, Sequential Images Narrative Dataset (SIND), containing images and references in which references are human-written short stories describing images. For every image prompt (one sequence of photos), there are 2 to 5 references. VIST requires deeper understanding of the photo events to prevent descriptions in isolation (i.e., image captions). Researchers have proposed various methods for this task. Early work on visual narrative generation studies a range of modeling strategies spanning sequence-to-sequence encoders with attention, hierarchical decoders for multi-sentence outputs, and multimodal fusion of CNN and RNN features, with notable systems including AREL (Wang et al., 2018a), GLAC Net (Kim et al., 2018), KG-Story (Hsu et al., 2020), and XE-SS (Huang et al., 2019), alongside pretraining on COCO Captions (Chen et al., 2015), Flickr30k (Young et al., 2014), and visual-semantic alignment objectives (Kiros et al., 2014). Knowledge graphs are often integrated in models to encourage diversity of terms and plots in the stories (Hsu et al., 2020(Hsu et al., , 2021a. Some studies use reinforcement learning to reward models that generate stories that contain fewer errors and are more topically-focused Hu et al., 2020a). However, existing evaluation methods are unable to capture the true quality of the generated stories. Thus we examine automatic metrics to devise a better way for machines to evaluate stories.

VIST-Human Evaluation Several VIST generation models use human evaluation to evaluate model performance. Recent studies apply aspectbased rating evaluation. Hu et al. (2020b) and Wang et al. (2020b) ask workers to rate stories based on pre-defined aspects. 1 However, it is difficult to normalize these aspects as the definition of aspect varies from paper to paper. Also, these aspects are not mutually independent, making it difficult to analyze results based on these ratings. Therefore, we consider the ranking method as it is commonly used (Hsu et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b;Hsu et al., 2021a)  Automatic Metrics Automatic evaluation metrics are widely used in language generation tasks. Most reference-based metrics (e.g., BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), and ROUGE (Lin, 2004)) evaluate the ngram similarity between a generated text and the reference. However, referenced metrics correlate poorly with human judgment (Wang et al., 2018b;Hsu et al., 2019;Modi and Parde, 2019) in dialog generation and story generation tasks: the generated text is given unreasonable scores due to incongruity with the reference. To account for this, several reference-free metrics (Sinha et al., 2020;Guan and Huang, 2020) have been designed to measure generated texts without any reference. BERT-Score (Zhang et al., 2019), for instance, uses contextual embedding to calculate the similarity between candidates and references, and BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020) uses referenced automatic metrics as supervision signals for pretraining and is fine-tuned on a human judgment evaluation dataset. UNION (Guan and Huang, 2020) uses pre-defined negative samples to train a model in an attempt to provide a metric that specializes in story generation. Beyond VIST, automatic evaluation in open-ended generation spans machine translation metrics such as BLEU and chrF (Popović, 2015), summarization metrics like ROUGE and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019), dialogue metrics including ADEM (Lowe et al., 2017) and USR (Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020), learned quality estimators such as COMET (Rei et al., 2020) and BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020), and reference-free variants including MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019), BaryScore (Colombo et al., 2021), and QuestEval (Scialom et al., 2021). In our analysis, current metrics remain unable to mimic human judgment to discern quality differences in story pairs.

spans: Prior research on value representation and social choice in computational settings spans value-based argumentation frameworks, crowd-sourced moral judgments, moral foundations in text classification, and ethical benchmarks for language models (Bench-Capon, 2003; Mikhail, 2007; Graham et al., 2009; Alkhatib, 2021; Forbes et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2021; Lourie et al., 2021), as well as datasets on stance and persuasion such as UKP ConvArg2, IBM Debater, Persuasion for Good, and Moral Stories, and methods involving weak supervision, prompt-based learning, and rationalization extraction.
document:
Human values are of concern to most if not to all social sciences (Rokeach, 1973) and have also been integrated into computational frameworks of argumentation (Bench-Capon, 2003). In NLP, values have been analyzed for personality profiling (Maheshwari et al., 2017), but not yet for argument mining, as considered here.

Argument mining aims to detect claims, premises, and their relations to structure reasoning in text. Value-centric argument mining would extend this by linking argumentative units to human values such as fairness, security, or autonomy. While initial taxonomies and psychological theories provide conceptual clarity, operationalizing them for automatic annotation remains difficult. Prior research on value representation and social choice in computational settings spans value-based argumentation frameworks, crowd-sourced moral judgments, moral foundations in text classification, and ethical benchmarks for language models (Bench-Capon, 2003; Mikhail, 2007; Graham et al., 2009; Alkhatib, 2021; Forbes et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2021; Lourie et al., 2021), as well as datasets on stance and persuasion such as UKP ConvArg2, IBM Debater, Persuasion for Good, and Moral Stories, and methods involving weak supervision, prompt-based learning, and rationalization extraction.

Despite this growing interest, the bridge between abstract value taxonomies and argument-structure prediction is thin. Existing argument mining datasets rarely encode explicit value labels attached to claim-premise pairs. Methods for moral or norm detection typically focus on topical cues rather than argumentative roles. Moreover, cross-domain generalization is underexplored when values shift across communities. These gaps suggest a need for resources and models that couple argument components with value semantics.

We frame value-aware argument mining as a multi-label prediction problem aligned with a compact value inventory. The approach separates argumentative unit detection from value attribution to enable modular analysis. We emphasize transparent annotation guidelines to improve inter-annotator agreement on value categories. Our modeling setup compares sequence labeling with span classification and incorporates lexical and discourse features. To assess robustness, we evaluate across topics featuring contested values.

Our contributions are threefold. First, we introduce an argument mining dataset annotated with value categories at the component level. Second, we provide baselines using contextual encoders with multi-task objectives for joint component and value prediction. Third, we release evaluation scripts that quantify both structural and value-centric performance. Together, these elements establish a foundation for systematic study of values in argument mining.

spans: Recent progress on multi-modal or cross-modal pre-training has also been reported across disparate threads of work, including joint speech-text representation learning with multimodal contrastive objectives (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Baevski et al., 2022; Hsu et al., 2021), unified encoder-decoder formulations for conditional generation (Raffel et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2022), multi-task training recipes for speech recognition, synthesis, and translation (Watanabe et al., 2018; Kharitonov et al., 2021; Popov et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020), and benchmark-driven evaluations of representation quality over diverse speech tasks (Yang et al., 2021; Tsai et al., 2022; Conneau et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021a).

Large-scale pre-training models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) have drawn much attention in the NLP and speech communities, due to its strong capability of generalization and efficient usage of largescale data (Devlin et al., 2019;Yang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2021c;Baevski et al., 2020;Kharitonov et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021a).
However, the research mentioned above effects gear towards single-modal learning, hence they can only be used in either text or speech modeling.
Although some speech-language pre-training work (Chung et al., 2021b;Kim et al., 2021; attempts to improve spoken language understanding tasks, these methods only focus on an encoder with task-specific layers for different tasks and do not pre-train a decoder for generation tasks such as speech synthesis or text generation.
Recent progress on multi-modal or cross-modal pre-training has also been reported across disparate threads of work, including joint speech-text representation learning with multimodal contrastive objectives (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Baevski et al., 2022; Hsu et al., 2021), unified encoder-decoder formulations for conditional generation (Raffel et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2022), multi-task training recipes for speech recognition, synthesis, and translation (Watanabe et al., 2018; Kharitonov et al., 2021; Popov et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020), and benchmark-driven evaluations of representation quality over diverse speech tasks (Yang et al., 2021; Tsai et al., 2022; Conneau et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021a).
The proposed SpeechT5 method is most related to T5 (Raffel et al., 2019).
The core idea of the T5 model, a unified framework for a variety of text-based language problems, is to treat every text processing problem as a "text-to-text" problem.
SpeechT5 is also related to Speech Chain (Tjandra et al., 2020), which leverages the ASR model and TTS model to build a closed-loop machine speech chain to train models on the concatenation of both labeled and unlabeled data, and SpeechNet (Chen et al., 2021b), which designs a universal modularized model to perform multiple speech processing tasks with multi-task learning.
The differences from the above models are that (1) SpeechT5 is a shared cross-modal encoder-decoder framework, whose input and output are speech or text through multiple pre/post-nets; (2) SpeechT5 attempts to pre-train and improve the universal model with large-scale unlabeled text and speech data.
Another related work is SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021), a benchmark to examine the capability of pre-trained models such as HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021).
SUPERB focuses on investigating a simple framework to learn SUPERB tasks with a frozen and shared pre-trained encoder and lightweight prediction modules fine-tuned for each task.
In contrast, the goal of SpeechT5 is to learn all spoken language processing tasks by fine-tuning a unifiedmodal encoder-decoder model, which is pre-trained on unlabeled speech and text corpus.
To enable this goal, we consider a pre-training objective that aligns speech and text representations while preserving modality-specific information needed for generation.
We further incorporate task-agnostic pre/post-nets to flexibly adapt the shared backbone to different input and output modalities.
During fine-tuning, the same architecture is applied with minimal task-specific adjustments, avoiding extensive per-task heads.
This design facilitates transfer across ASR, TTS, speech translation, and spoken intent understanding with a single set of parameters.
We evaluate on diverse datasets to ensure robustness across domains and speaker conditions.
Our experiments compare against strong encoder-only and encoder-decoder baselines in both speech-only and text-only regimes.
We report results under both full-resource and limited-resource settings to assess data efficiency.
Finally, we analyze ablations to isolate the effect of cross-modal sharing and the contribution of each pre/post-net component.

In this section, we first go through some of the most related works for BERT compression in the literature and then review the few works that have used Kronecker decomposition for compression of CNNs and RNNs.

Transformer compression research has rapidly evolved alongside deployment constraints that prioritize latency and energy efficiency.
Prior work on compressing large language models spans pruning (Han et al., 2015; Zhu and Gupta, 2017; Michel et al., 2019), quantization (Gong et al., 2014; Jacob et al., 2018; Zafrir et al., 2019; Dettmers et al., 2022), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020), and low-rank or tensorized factorization (Denton et al., 2014; Jaderberg et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2015; Novikov et al., 2015), as well as structured matrix approaches such as Toeplitz and circulant layers (Sindhwani et al., 2015; Cheng et al., 2015; Moczulski et al., 2016), and recent dynamic sparsity methods (Evci et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2023; Gale et al., 2019).
In the BERT-specific literature, distillation reduces model size by transferring knowledge from a teacher to a student while preserving downstream task accuracy.
TinyBERT, DistilBERT, and MobileBERT exemplify this line with architectural and objective tailoring to maintain attention patterns and intermediate feature alignment.
Quantization-aware training and post-training quantization for BERT have been shown to retain accuracy at 8-bit and, with calibration, 4-bit precision on several GLUE tasks.
Structured and unstructured pruning strategies exploit sensitivity of attention heads and feed-forward blocks to sparsity, often guided by magnitude or movement scores.
Low-rank factorizations of the projection and feed-forward matrices compress parameters while controlling reconstruction error, and tensor factorizations extend this to higher-order decompositions.

Kronecker-structured parameterization provides a complementary path by expressing large weight matrices as sums of Kronecker products of small factors.
This idea predates Transformers in convolutional models where filters admit separable or Kroneckerized forms that reduce FLOPs and memory.
For RNNs, Kronecker factorizations have been used to compress recurrent and input-to-hidden matrices while stabilizing training through spectral constraints.
Recent work integrates Kronecker structure with Bayesian or variational formulations to regularize factor scales and mitigate overfitting in low-data regimes.
Hybrid schemes combine Kronecker structure with quantization or sparsity to multiply compression ratios without severe accuracy drop.
In language models, adapting Kronecker forms to multi-head attention and MLP blocks requires aligning factor shapes with projection matrices and residual pathways.
Such adaptations motivate evaluations on both pretraining-like masked language modeling and downstream fine-tuning benchmarks.

Despite progress, several trade-offs remain salient for practical deployment.
Compression interacts with calibration, robustness to distribution shift, and throughput on real hardware backends.
Operator fusion and kernel availability can dominate realized speedups even when theoretical FLOP reductions are large.
Moreover, fine-tuning stability under aggressive compression hinges on optimization schedules, layer-wise learning rates, and factor initialization.
Finally, evaluation must consider end-to-end constraints, including sequence length, batch size, and memory-bandwidth ceilings.
These considerations frame our discussion of compression design choices in the context of BERT and related Transformer encoders.
We next detail the Kronecker parameterizations and experimental setups used to study their accuracy–efficiency trade-offs.

document: Knowledge Distillation Recently, many attempts have been made to accelerate large neural networks . Knowledge distillation is a prominent method for training compact networks to achieve comparable performance to a deep network. Hinton et al. (2015) first introduced the idea of knowledge distillation to exploit the "dark knowledge" (i.e., soft label distribution) from a large teacher model as additional supervision for training a smaller student model. Since its introduction, several works (Romero et al., 2015;Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2017;Tung & Mori, 2019;Park et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2019) have investigated methods that align different latent representations between the student and teacher models for better knowledge transfer. Recently, several strands of research have explored complementary or alternative supervision signals and distillation setups, including logit-based temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), feature-map transfer and hint layers (Romero et al., 2015; Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2017), attention and relational knowledge transfer (Park et al., 2019; Tung & Mori, 2019), contrastive or instance-level objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020), self- and data-free distillation (Yuan et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2019), and task- or architecture-agnostic protocols spanning vision and language benchmarks (Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Sanh et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019). In the context of knowledge distillation, MetaDistil shares some common ideas with the line of work that utilizes a sequence of intermediate teacher models to make the teacher network better adapt to the capacity of the student model throughout the training process, including teacher assistant knowledge distillation (TAKD) (Mirzadeh et al., 2020) and route constraint optimization (RCO) . However, the intermediate teachers are heuristically selected independently of the training process and the evolution of the teacher network is discrete. In contrast, MetaDistil employs meta learning to make the teacher model adapt to the current state of the student model and provide a continuously evolving meta-teacher that can better teach the student. Concurrently, Park et al. (2021) and Shi et al. (2021) propose to update the teacher model jointly with the student model with task specific objectives (e.g., cross-entropy loss) during the KD process and add constraints to keep student and teacher similar to each other. Their approaches makes the teacher model aware of the student model by constraining the teacher model's capacity. However, the teacher models in their methods are still not optimized for knowledge transfer. In addition, Zhang et al. (2018) introduced deep mutual learning where multiple models learn collaboratively and teach each other throughout the training process. While it is focused on a different setting where different models have approximately the same capacity and are learned from scratch, it also encourages the teacher model to behave similarly to the student model. Different from all aforementioned methods, MetaDistil employs meta learning to explicitly optimize the teacher model for better knowledge transfer ability, and leads to improved performance of the resulting student model.

spans: Beyond GLUE-style robustness benchmarks, adversarial stress-testing for sequence labeling has drawn on a wide array of resources and techniques, including perturbation lexicons and counterfactual templates (Kaushik et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021), character-level noise and keyboard-aware edits (Eger et al., 2019; Pruthi et al., 2019), entity replacement using gazetteers and Wikidata-derived aliases (Rahimi et al., 2019; Ghaddar and Langlais, 2018), and cross-domain transfer evaluations spanning OntoNotes, CoNLL-2003, WNUT-17, and CrossNER (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Derczynski et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2021; Hovy et al., 2006).

Adversarial GLUE (Wang et al., 2021) employed the use of expert-annotated templates for constructing adversarial data, However, it does not include NER as a task. A few previous works have explored the generation of adversarial examples specifically for the NER task. For instance, Gui et al. (2021) performed augmentations by concatenating sentences, swapping/inserting/deleting a random character in an entity, entity swapping with Out-of-Vocabulary entities, and cross category swapping. Similarly, Zeng et al. (2020) also took random entity swap approach but only selected entities of the same label to preserve linguistic correctness. In our work, we purposely alter the entity and add surrounding context with the goal of changing the label to target the NER hard cases.

Beyond GLUE-style robustness benchmarks, adversarial stress-testing for sequence labeling has drawn on a wide array of resources and techniques, including perturbation lexicons and counterfactual templates (Kaushik et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021), character-level noise and keyboard-aware edits (Eger et al., 2019; Pruthi et al., 2019), entity replacement using gazetteers and Wikidata-derived aliases (Rahimi et al., 2019; Ghaddar and Langlais, 2018), and cross-domain transfer evaluations spanning OntoNotes, CoNLL-2003, WNUT-17, and CrossNER (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Derczynski et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2021; Hovy et al., 2006). In addition to adversarial generation, calibration and uncertainty estimation are closely related to robustness in NER. Temperature scaling and label smoothing have been shown to reduce overconfidence in token classifiers under shift. Data augmentation strategies for NER extend beyond entity swaps to include paraphrasing and back-translation, though these can dilute entity boundary fidelity without careful constraints. Recent work also considers structure-aware perturbations that preserve BIO consistency while varying mention spans.

In terms of techniques to improve generalization, we take a closer look at mixup, proposed in Zhang et al. (2018). Mixup involves linear interpolation of data points to create a virtual vicinity distribution around the original data space, which improves the classifier's generalization ability when trained on these interpolated data points. Several previous studies (Thulasidasan et al., 2020;Verma et al., 2019) showed how using mixup allows neural models to be less confident during distribution shifts than models not using mixup. It also leads to a smoother decision-making surface than Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM), thereby making models more robust to adversarial examples (Liang et al., 2018). In this work, we use mixup to further improve the generalization ability of NER models when training on our expert-informed augmented data.

spans: Recent approaches have focused on data synthesis (Yin et al., 2021), meta-learning (Wang et al., 2021), relation-aware schema encoding (Wang et al., 2020), and encoder pre-training (Yin et al., 2020;Herzig et al., 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Deng et al., 2021), alongside prompt-based finetuning methods for semantic parsing and retrieval-augmented modeling (Liu et al., 2021;Schick and Schütze, 2021;Rajpurkar et al., 2016;Lewis et al., 2020), schema linking strategies such as global graph encoders and relation networks (Guo et al., 2019;Bogin et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020), and data augmentation with paraphrase generation and SQL sketch mining (Iyer et al., 2018;Yu et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2019;Pouran Ben Veyseh et al., 2020).
